Name: F-AG10607-ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Dscription: ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Project Summary: Asia–Africa BlueTech Superhighway (AABS) harnesses South–South collaboration to assess, adapt and scale evidence-based innovations and models for delivering nature-positive impact through aquatic food systems. Implemented by WorldFish in collaboration with a host of partners, AABS aims to transform livelihoods of coastal communities across African and Asian countries, particularly for women and youth, and help restore marine and coastal ecosystems by developing sustainable and resilient aquatic food systems. Funded by the UK Government under its Blue Planet Fund, AABS is being implemented in two phases over the course of seven years (2023–2030).

Name: L-MML007-Antimicrobial Resistance and One Health, including Animal Health, the Environment and Practitioner Engagement (AMROH) Eastern and Southern Africa (ESA)
Dscription: Antimicrobial Resistance and One Health, including Animal Health, the Environment and Practitioner Engagement (AMROH) Eastern and Southern Africa (ESA)
Project Summary: Regional grant funded by the UK Department of Health and Social Care through the Fleming Fund Grants Programme. Its primary objective is to provide technical support to prioritized countries—Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, Malawi, Eswatini, Zimbabwe, Zambia, and potentially Rwanda, to strengthen AMR and AMU surveillance, focusing on animal health, environmental AMR monitoring, and animal health practitioner engagement. Key activities providing technical input to development of integrated surveillance protocols, enhancing both field and laboratory capacities, supporting data analysis, and strengthening AMR policy frameworks. The grant also focuses on engaging animal health practitioners to promote the rational use of antibiotics in food animals. ILRI partners with University of Copenhagen (DK), The Brooke (UK), and York University (CA), and collaborates closely with the Fleming Fund country grantees and their national partners to to achieve these objectives.

Name: L-CIA024-Anti-methanogenic feedstock for livestock systems in Global South
Dscription: Anti-methanogenic feedstock for livestock systems in Global South
Project Summary:
This project aims to discover and deploy high-yielding, tropically adapted forages that reduce cattle methane emissions and improve productivity for small-scale livestock farmers and pastoralists. The focus areas are:

Identifying and promoting AMC-rich forage legumes in ruminant production systems in the Global South through partnerships, including new public-private partnerships (PPP).
Breeding methane-reducing traits into widely used forage-grass cultivars using conventional or precision-breeding approaches.
Enabling the deployment of AMC-rich forages into livestock systems, including cattle grazing and cut-and-carry smallholder systems, through scaling and impact investments.
The project leverages a partnership between science, philanthropy, and public and private sector partners to develop and deploy AMC-enriched forages, reducing methane production in cattle while maintaining or improving productivity. This strategy offers a cost-effective solution for reducing livestock emissions in the Global South.

Dscription: Accelerating genomics surveillance for COVID-19 response in Africa

Dscription: Accelerated Intervention Delivery Initiative (Southern Africa AID-I Rapid Delivery Hub)

This provides access to improved breeds of backyard village chickens, support small-scale poultry enterprises, and reduced poultry disease.

Name: SCIENTIFIC AND TECHNICAL SUPPORT TO ASEAN COUNTRIES' SUSTAINABLE AGRICULTURE - 3S
Dscription: Both description and project summary filled. The 3S Project intends to support ASEAN Member States (AMS) to strengthen their primary sector by improving the resilience of agriculture to the impacts of climate change through sustainable agriculture and  agricultural mechanisation solutions for the Cassava, Maize and Sugar Cane value chains. The expected impact: “Contribute to the adoption of sustainable food production systems and the implementation of resilient agricultural practices that by 2030 will increase productivity and production, help maintain ecosystems, strengthen capacity for adaptation to climate change, extreme weather, drought, flooding and other disasters, and progressively improve land and soil quality in AMS” is aligned and with Goal 2 of the 2030 agenda, and contributes also to SDG1, SDG 5, SDG 13 and SDG 15. Two Specific Objectives are hence identified:  1. Sustainable agriculture contributes to the fight against climate change in AMS; 2. Effective, safe, accessible, and appropriate mechanical solutions increase the productivity and competitiveness of AMS, consolidating food security. The initiative is harmonised with and relies on the food security and agricultural development targets expressed in the ASEAN Guidelines for Promoting Climate Smart Agriculture (CSA) Practices; the ASEAN Regional Guidelines for Sustainable Agriculture; the ASEAN Economic Community Blueprint 2025; in the ASEAN Comprehensive Recovery Framework; in the ASEAN Cooperation in Food, Agriculture, and forestry 2016 – 2025; in the ASEAN Integrated Food Security (AIFS) Framework; and in the Strategic Plan of Action on Food Security in the ASEAN Region (SPA-FS) 2021-2025.
Project Summary: Expected results: R1 The bottlenecks in the maize, cassava and sugar cane supply chains are identified and solutions to address them are identified in terms of training and innovation, in the context of agricultural mechanisation and CSA. R2: The ASEAN Sectoral Working Group on Crops (ASWGC), the ASEAN Sectoral Working Group on Agriculture Training and Extension (AWGATE), Agricultural Research Institutes and national farmers associations/cooperatives increase their knowledge on advanced techniques of sustainable agriculture (e.g. CSA, agroecology, circular economy and certifications) and on sustainable mechanisation of corn, cassava and sugarcane supply chains. R3: Technical officials from ASWGC, AWGATE, agricultural research institutes and national farmers' associations/cooperatives increase their knowledge on the safe use of agricultural machinery. Among the outputs of this result there is also the production of a training manual. R4: Trainers from ASWGC, AWGATE, agricultural research institutes and national farmers' associations / cooperatives are trained and certified so that they themselves can subsequently train and disseminate the acquired know-how among farmers/trade associations and research centers in their countries of origin and ensure the sustainability of the action. R5:  Pilot projects are implemented with selected farming communities in 3 ASEAN member states. In this framework, local farmers organisations / agro-processors will be exposed to agricultural machinery and trained by certified trainers (R4), with the support of the project partners. R6: The results of the pilot action (R5) together with the supply chain strengthening methodology developed under the initiative are disseminated among participating ASEAN Member States in the closing workshop of the initiative. The project will be implemented for a duration of 3 years by a consortium composed by the Department of Agricultural and Environmental Sciences - Production, Territory and Agroenergy of the University of Milan (UNIMI-DISAA), the Scuola Superiore Sant'Anna (SSSA) of Pisa and the Alliance of Bioversity International and CIAT, in strict coordination with the ASEAN secretariat, the ASEAN Working Group on Crops (ASWGC) and the ASEAN Sectoral Working Group on Agriculture Training and Extension (AWGATE) and Research Institutes and National Farmers' Associations from AMS. UNIMI-DIISA will lead the consortium, while The Alliance will ensure the institutional and technical coordination of the activties from its offices in Jakarta and Hanoi.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: Rural and sustainable innovation for regional development in conditions of climate variability in the Central RAP-E Region
Dscription: Both description and project summary filled. The Initiative aims to strengthen biodiversity conservation processes and ecosystem services in the Central Region of Colombia, through the application of the integrated management approach of Productive Landscapes, taking into consideration its social, economic, and environmental sustainability. This, in addition, includes the launching of local, national, and global initiatives for the generation of Guidelines for inter-departmental policies that promote the appropriation of sustainable rural development models and the leadership of women and youth.
Project Summary:
The Project aims to promote the use of sustainable production technologies and practices in the value chains present in priority Productive Landscapes, with particular attention to the circular economy and green businesses. Activities include: the development of sustainable management tools for priority Productive Landscapes; the introduction of economic and market incentives to encourage the adoption of these measures; the generation of marketing channels for Productive Landscape products; the promotion of sustainable tourism; the participation and leadership of women and youth in the creation of regionally adapted Guidelines for the management ofProductive Landscapes; the promotion of research on integral management;and the creation of a knowledge management platform to influence regional decision-making. Finally, the project aims to improve the capacity to care for river basins and the conservation of strategic ecosystems, giving priority to
women who manage natural resources and small and medium-sized producers. The Project, whose responsible/executing body is the International Italian-
Latin American Organization (IILA), is divided into 3 parts/modules where the "Women and Youth" component is preponderant in the third and last module, although it is an axis of the whole Project and includes activities related to territorial, agricultural, environmental and tourism development, sectors in which IILA is recognized as an innovative leader and has gained proven multi-year experience in cooperation projects in Latin America. The execution of the activities will also be carried out through the negotiation of OOII or allies of the Italian system recognized as good practices and innovative leaders and who have been pre-identified (Bioversity - CIAT, CREA, CNR, University of Bologna, Emilia – Romagna Region and the Veneto Region, among others) and whose definition will be carried out in greater detail in the start-up phase of the initiative, also involving sustainable private companies in the agri-environmental sector. The Project will have a duration of 36 months to support transformative changes in how institutions coordinate and intervene at the environmental landscape level, as well as how stakeholders interact and make decisions that impact both individual farms and the overall landscape.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: BIUSA (Bezos Earth Fund)-Using genetic diversity to capture carbon through deep root systems in tropical soils  (Research and Capacity development)
Dscription:
Both description and project summary both filled. The “Using Genetic Diversity to Capture Carbon Through Deep Root Systems in Tropical Soils” project focuses on leveraging genetic diversity to develop new root ideotypes of tropical forage grass and rice. The goal is to enhance carbon assimilation via photosynthesis and deep rooting ability, contributing to increased carbon storage in tropical soils. The project aims to support the completion and maintenance of the Future Seeds facility, develop novel high-throughput phenotyping methods to evaluate genetic diversity in deep rooting ability, and build capacity for conducting research on root systems and soil carbon storage in tropical regions. Scope and Activities:
1. Development and Phenotyping:
o Develop high-throughput phenotyping methods to screen and evaluate genetic diversity in root systems of tropical forage grass and rice.
o Identify and improve forage and rice genotypes with deep rooting traits to enhance soil organic carbon (SOC) storage.
2. Research and Innovation:
o Conduct field trials to test the performance of new genotypes in different agroecological niches.
o Apply gene editing technologies to improve root traits and photosynthetic efficiency.
3. Capacity Building and Dissemination:
o Train young researchers in genetic diversity and carbon farming.
o Develop and implement strategies to disseminate improved root ideotypes to farmers in tropical regions.
4. Impact and Monitoring:
o Monitor the impact of deep-rooted genotypes on SOC storage and agricultural productivity.
o Evaluate the benefits of carbon removal from the atmosphere and its implications for climate change mitigation.
Project Summary: The "Using Genetic Diversity to Capture Carbon Through Deep Root Systems in Tropical Soils" project aims to enhance carbon storage in tropical soils by developing new root ideotypes of tropical forage grasses and rice. By leveraging genetic diversity, the project seeks to improve carbon assimilation and deep rooting traits, contributing to increased soil organic carbon storage. The project will develop high-throughput phenotyping methods, conduct field trials, and apply gene editing technologies to enhance root systems. Additionally, it aims to build research capacity and disseminate improved genotypes to farmers. Expected outcomes include improved soil health, increased agricultural productivity, and significant contributions to climate change mitigation through enhanced carbon sequestration.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: 1571-FAO0 - Youth, Citizen Science and E-commerce: scaling integrated conservation solutions and farmers’ rights by connecting key diversity hotspots: Bolivia, Chile, and Peru
Dscription: Youth, Citizen Science and E-commerce: scaling integrated conservation solutions and farmers’ rights by connecting key diversity hotspots: Bolivia, Chile, and Peru
Project Summary: The main objective of this project is to scale integrated conservation solutions and farmers’ rights by connecting key diversity hotspots in Bolivia, Chile, and Peru. The aim is to generate intergenerational dialogue towards the effective conservation of genetic resources (potato, maize and quinoa), to document and monitor diversity, and to provide different types of incentives to custodian farmers.
Center: International Potato Center / Centro Internacional de la Papa

Name: L-IUC001-Sustainable Investments for Large-scale Rangeland R
Dscription: Sustainable Investments for Large-scale Rangeland Restoration (STELARR) Project
Project Summary: The Sustainable Investments for Large-Scale Rangeland Restoration (STELARR) project is a global initiative aimed at reversing rangeland degradation, enhancing productivity, and supporting sustainable livestock value chains. Funded by the Global Environmental Facility (GEF) and executed by the International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI) in partnership with the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN), the project focuses on improving rangeland management practices to reduce poverty and secure livelihoods, with a particular emphasis on benefiting women and youth. STELARR supports governments and stakeholders in making strong commitments to sustainable rangeland restoration, aligning with global frameworks such as the UN Decade on Ecosystem Restoration. By promoting inclusive and climate-friendly practices, the project contributes to the long-term sustainability and resilience of rangelands worldwide

Name: ITALY-Italy Voluntary contribution 2024 - Sustainable cocoa value chains in West Africa (A1701)
Dscription: See project summary.
Project Summary: Comprehensive assessments, feasibility study, and stakeholder consultations conducted in Côte d'Ivoire and Guinea, resulting in the preparation and submission of a Technical Assistance Programme designed to increase the value and resilience of cocoa value chains in West Africa, securing necessary funding for implementation. This phase focuses NOT on execution or research but on packaging existing knowledge and evidence to write for a comprehensive regional project proposal targeting EUR 150-200M, with a technical assistance (TA) component.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: UK – GCBC (UK-Defra) , Deploying Diversity as a Strategic Asset for Scaling of  Ethiopia’s Green Legacy Initiative for Climate Change  Resilience and Livelihoods
Dscription: See project summary.
Project Summary: The diversity for resilience and livelihood project  is designed to support the GLI with the aims of i) assessing and identifying the type and diversity of wild edible fruits and vegetables in GLI coverage areas and designing sustainable conservation strategies for such crops; ii) evaluating and quantifying the potential of GLI for socioeconomic welfare of the surrounding communities; iii) to evaluate and quantify the ecosystem services provided by GLI in comparison of non-GLI areas; iv) estimate the carbon sequestration potential of GLI and v) to assess community's willingness to pay for GLI drived economic and ecosystem services. Measurement and documentation of the sensitivity of GLI for biodiversity, diversity assessment and ecosystem service quantification are among the key results expected.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: P-1580-IAI0-Market-led Transformation of the Root and Tuber
Dscription: Market-led Transformation of the Root and Tuber Crops Subsector in Malawi (RTC-MARKET)
Center: International Potato Center / Centro Internacional de la Papa

Name: Landscape resource management for augmenting groundwater recharge and sustainable intensification of farming systems and building resilience for improved livelihoods of farmers in dryland of Maharashtra, India
Dscription: Landscape resource management for augmenting groundwater recharge and sustainable intensification of farming systems and building resilience for improved livelihoods of farmers in dryland of Maharashtra, India
Project Summary: The project aims to establish seven benchmark learning sites by utilizing a Land Resource Inventory (LRI) and a hydrology-based landscape management approach. This initiative will focus on groundwater augmentation, sustainable intensification of farming systems, and reducing water, energy, and carbon footprints. Additionally, the project will design profitable and water-efficient cropping systems that integrate climate-resilient, nutrient-dense crops, along with effective management practices and market linkages to enhance farmer income and resource use efficiency. Finally, the project will track key performance indicators using advanced instrumentation and digital tools to assess the techno-economic feasibility and develop strategies for scaling up.
Center: International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics

Name: V0179-Intercropping project CROP/2022/111
Dscription: Intercropping project CROP/2022/111
Project Summary: The primary focus of this project is on sustainably improving cropping system productivity, however the effects of wide-row, additive intercropping at the smallholder farm level will be considered, including potential food and nutrition benefits for the household.
Center: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center / Centro Internacional de Mejoramiento de Maíz y Trigo

Name: W-P-1641-IKI Indo-German Cooperation on Agrivoltaics (IGCA)
Dscription: Indo-German Cooperation on Agrivoltaics (IGCA)
Project Summary: The objective of this project is to contribute to an accelerated just energy transition through increased uptake of APV, generating benefits for the rural population without compromising food security (Outcome). It is expected that the project will financially and technically support the installation of 6 APV systems with a total capacity of around 10 MWp in 3 different states (direct effect). In addition, it will contribute to the further development of the APV market segment incl. additional 6 APV plants within the project implementation period alone (indirect effect). This will reduce the carbon intensity of the Indian power sector, improve air quality, and strengthen the social acceptance of the energy transition, especially among rural populations (transformational character and impact).
Project results: The Indo-German Cooperation on Agrivoltaics (IGCA) will follow a farmer centric approach to develop models that not only deliver solar generation capacity but enhance rural livelihoods and village economies.  The proposal development will  be mainly through planning and co-organizing a series of consultations and events aimed at defining the contours of the detailed proposal and identifying key partners who can contribute to this intended initiative.

Name: W-D-0526-GEF: G4DR in Africa
Dscription: Groundwater for Deep Resilience in Africa
Project Summary: The Groundwater for aDvancing Resilience (G4DR) in Africa project aims to bring groundwater and its sustainable development and protection to the forefront of water security planning and investment in Africa, enhancing resilience for humans and ecosystems.
Project results:
The Groundwater for aDvancing Resilience (G4DR) in Africa project aims to bring groundwater and its sustainable development and protection to the forefront of water security planning and investment in Africa, enhancing resilience for humans and ecosystems. It will do so through the following components:
•	Strategic Planning: Supporting the African Ministers’ Council on Water (AMCOW), through their Pan-African Groundwater Program (APAGroP). 
•	Evidence and Capacity for G4DR in Africa: Identifying aquifers that present risk and opportunity to enhance resilience, as well as populations/socio-economic contexts in Africa informing investments 
•	Demonstrating benefit: Utilizing evidence-based planning to realize on-the-ground impacts in pilots
•	Incorporating G4DR into pan-African Youth Forums: enhancing the beyond-project capacity, outreach, networking, and uptake of long-term workable and sustainable strategies and solutions.
•	Supporting Knowledge Management and M&E: Supporting capture, exchange and dissemination of key project advancements, as well as evaluation of project progress relative to targets.
Countries: Benin, Togo, Mozambique, South Africa, Malawi, Uganda

Name: Enhancing Crop Productivity and Climate Resilience for Food and Nutrition Security in Mali (FNS-Mali)
Dscription: Enhancing Crop Productivity and Climate Resilience for Food and Nutrition Security in Mali
Project Summary: The project aims to demonstrate that circular food systems can decouple growth in social and economic benefits from consumption of natural resources (particularly water and land) and production of greenhouse gas emissions in agriculture, reducing urbanization by creating local opportunities for the women and youth. We will test how smallholder irrigation schemes might become the engine of local resilience and economic development by being integrated with livestock, and dryland agricultural production to support strategic and inclusive development of small, value-adding business to form climate smart circular food systems in East and Southern Africa.
Center: International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics

Name: PREPARATION PHASE - "Supporting Colombia’s Just Energy Transition, climate resilience, and food security through the introduction of inclusive agri-photovoltaic systems" (JET-AgriSOL)
Dscription: Description and project summary both filled. To support the Colombian Government in achieving its sustainable and Just Energy Transition goal, and meeting its agricultural and energy sectoral NDC targets through the nationwide expansion of photovoltaic capacity (impact), farmer communities have to co-develop and adopt inclusive and economically viable agri-photovoltaic (APV) solutions that can be further scaled through SDG-compliant public and private investments (outcome). This development will be initiated during the project’s first piloting phase, consisting of an APV pilot that will be installed on CIAT’s campus to carry out “higher-risk” research activities (output III). During the second piloting phase, lower-risk research activities will be conducted under real conditions on medium-scale APV pilots, on horticultural, arable, and pastoral land (output I). The diverse APV pilots form the basis for knowledge co-creation and serve for demonstration purposes, thereby promoting APV innovation-uptake and -scaling. Furthermore, women and men members of rural communities will be trained through a new praxis-oriented, and gender-responsive APV curriculum that will be developed and implemented with SENA’s (Colombia’s National Training Service) in-situ Rural Entrepreneurship (SER) program, focussing especially on developing capacities of disadvantaged groups (output II). This holistic skills development empowers rural community members to become more climate resilient and take advantage of new income opportunities. Finally, during the project’s third piloting phase, lessons learned from the experimental and medium-scale pilots will be integrated into the implementation of community-scale APV pilots, e.g., on the land of cacao producer organisations in PDET municipalities. Community-scale APV pilot will expand the green energy capacity of producer organisations and their communities, while promoting forest- and landscape-restoration activities (output I). The community-scale pilots play a key role in scaling APV systems and generating positive environmental impacts, while contributing to the energy-sovereignty of vulnerable rural communities. Moreover, inclusive and accessible APV-tailored financial mechanisms will be co-designed with local and international financial institutions (IFIs), further attracting investments to the APV sector, and enabling and encouraging small-scale farmers to adopt APV solutions (output IV). To further facilitate the inclusive adoption and scaling of APV systems, key binding constraints in the enabling environment will be identified and addressed through a multistakeholder process (output V).
Project Summary: The proposed project will support the Colombian Government in achieving its Just Energy Transition objective and NDC targets. By introducing and scaling APV systems, the project will contribute to promoting the energy-sovereignty and climate resilience of small-scale farmers, reducing GHG emissions, empowering women and youth to improve their livelihoods, and enhancing biodiversity. The project will set up micro- and medium-scale pilot and demonstration APV systems. Capacities for managing APV systems and combining them with climate smart agriculture will be strengthened by co-creating a vocational APV training program with Colombia´s National Training Service. Inclusive, participatory and interdisciplinary research will be conducted on the pilot APV systems, and findings will be shared with a broad range of stakeholders. Further, context-specific and inclusive APV finance mechanisms will be co-designed, and public and private stakeholders supported to upgrade the enabling environment.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: FRANCE – FFEM Promover Oportunidades Sostenibles en la Cadena de Valor del Cacao de Excelencia (IA Lead of A1537)
Dscription: Description and project summary both filled. The project works to upscale sustainable cocoa production of high quality, on sustainably managed zero-deforestation land, and provide farmers with reliable incomes. The project also aims to increase the value given to high-quality cocoa in the three countries, safeguarding traditional varieties that are important to the countries’ agricultural heritage.  The Agroecological Regenerative Cocoa project’s objective is to demonstrate the feasibility of establishing environmentally and economically sustainable cocoa value chains at the landscape level that contribute to ecosystems conservation.
Project Summary: This innovative project benefits from multi-actor participation between NGOs, private sector partners, and local communities. The partners implement the undervalued but effective methods shared by local actors on cocoa agroforestry, soils recovery, cocoa fermentation, and cooperative-private company partnership models. The strengthening of sustainable agricultural landscapes in Colombia, Ecuador and Peru in the cocoa sector is having positive impacts that could be reproduced at the provincial, national, regional and international levels.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: USA-USAID-Amazon Biodiversity Fund Brazil Fundo de Investimento em Participações Multiestratégia
Dscription: Description and project summary both filled. Strategic goal/large description : 1) To conserve biodiversity in the Amazonia Legal by investing in conservation, sustainable production, supply chains and related impact businesses; 2) To advance private sector engagement, leadership and investments in Brazilian Amazon biodiversity conservation and sustainable development by strengthening, expanding and consolidating the Partnership Platform for the Amazon; 3) To lead a robust monitoring, evaluation and learning process through a participatory action research model, co-designing processes, systems, metrics and approaches, jointly with the private sector and USAID/Brazil for monitoring, evaluation and capturing lessons learned and best practices for working with the private sector; 4) To ensure the independence, integrity and quality of the tools implemented and research conducted, and share those models and approaches through a strategic communication and outreach process, with a community of practice to provide evidence base for USAID/Brazil’s Private sector engagement strategy in Brazil. And 5) to collaborate in the development of outreach and education materials regarding eh programs for external audiences.
Project Summary: Objectives in common (USAID-CIAT): 1) Strengthen conservation and rural community livelihoods in Protected Areas, Indigenous, Quilombola and other Traditional Territories, conservation areas and private lands; 2) Strengthen buffer zones and forest-dependent value chains of smallholders in and around biologically significant areas; 3) Strengthen farming value chains that strategically reduce deforestation, or reforestation on degraded land in and around biologically significant areas and 4) Improve the availability of biodiversity friend services, technology and/or innovative finance providers. Outcomes: 1) The Amazon’s topical forest, ecosystems and biodiversity are conserved in biologically significant ways areas; 2) The Well-being of rural communities in the Amazon is improved; 3) Buffer zones and ecological corridors are conserved and/or recovered; 4) The enabling environment, and scalable, biodiversity friendly business models, are established, and 5) Climate change is mitigated through avoided deforestation and reforestation.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: F-PP-2022-1020-Climate-Resilient Aquaculture Systems for Afr (AG10618)
Dscription: Climate-Resilient Aquaculture Systems for Africa (CASA)
Project Summary: WorldFish and partners are Implementing the “Climate-Resilient Aquaculture Systems for Africa - CASA” in Kenya and Nigeria. CASA delivers solutions that simultaneously increase productivity and profitability, climate adaptation and environmental sustainability, food and nutrition security, and the expansion of employment and income opportunities within the aquaculture systems. CASA has four Interlinked Intervention Areas (IAs). Under IA1, CASA will deliver income and productivity gains for most of the farmers, with an estimated 1x - 2x increase in productivity for the average farmer per cycle, and the best-performing farmers obtaining 3x increase in productivity per cycle. Under IA2, CASA aims to reach 1,000 fish processors with improved, climate-smart fish processing technologies and practices. Under IA3, CASA will support the development and piloting of financial products and services which target the aquaculture value chains. Under IA4, CASA will support the strengthening of the policy environment and the capacity development of policymakers.

Name: N-643015-CoIMPACT INDIA
Dscription: CGIAR support to the Promise of Commons Initiative in India
Project Summary: IFPRI and CGIAR (particularly Alliance and CIFOR-ICRAF) will support the work under the Promise of Commons initiative. This includes i) supporting learning, measurement, and evaluation systems, including capacity building and knowledge products from evaluation studies; ii) collaborative development of evidence and tools for gender-equitable and inclusive change; and iii) methods and tools for community engagement to strengthen collective action.

Name: Building Community Resilience by Alleviating Water scarcity and Land Degradation through Integrated Natural Resource Management in Bundelkhand, Uttar Pradesh
Dscription: Building Community Resilience by Alleviating Water scarcity and Land Degradation through Integrated Natural Resource Management in Bundelkhand, Uttar Pradesh
Project Summary:
The proposed project follows an integrated approach for resource creation, management and livelihood improvement. Following are the key features of the project:
- Creation and management of natural resources
- Diversified farming system
- Institutional and capacity building
Objectives of the proposed project:
The overall objective of the proposal is to build resilient farming communities and, this is proposed to be achieved by
• Enhancing groundwater availability and reducing land degradation through intensive rainwater harvesting and appropriate natural resource management technologies;
• Developing capacity of the farming community through knowledge sharing and farmer participatory research /demonstrations;
• Integrating crop-livestock-tree based farming for diversifying rural livelihood; and
• Facilitating market linkages through promotion of collective action and community-based organization
Center: International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics

Name: IUCN (GEF)-Restoration Challenge Grant Platform for Smallholders and Communities, with Blockchain-Enabled Crowdfunding
Dscription: Both description and summary filled. The Restoration Challenge Grant Platform for Smallholders and Communities, with Blockchain-Enabled Crowdfunding (hereafter referred to as the Platform), will facilitate and support enhanced smallholder and rural community member engagement and investment in restoration. The Platform will utilize mobile cellular technology and payment transfer services to provide small grants/payments for smallholder-, community-, and school-led restoration work – principally tree-planting – matched by co-investment (in-kind and/or cash). Cellular technology will allow for efficient and effective verification of work and transfer of payments. Another key innovative focus of the Platform will be the integration of blockchain technology throughout the restoration value chain to provide transparency, build trust, facilitate real-time monitoring, evaluation and verification, and support mobilization of funding for restoration. From appropriate species selection, seed sourcing and seedling propagation, to tree planting and maintenance and transfer of payments, each transaction will be marked by a unique, traceable, unchangeable and verifiable blockchain. A second phase of the Platform will utilize a public-facing web platform and partnerships with other tree planting and restoration investment matching platforms to facilitate crowdfunding of Platform-supported restoration and financial sustainability, drawing upon the blockchain technology and ledger for security and transparency and trustworthyness of crowdfunded transactions. A range of engagement approaches and selection of a diverse group of landscapes will allow for cross comparison and learning to inform restoration initiatives going forward. Upon successful development and implementation of the Platform including blockchain-enabled crowdfunding, the Platform will function as a freestanding initiative – one that can inform and link with other global and regional initiatives and platforms supporting the restoration agenda, including the Trillion Trees Initiative, UN Decade on Ecosystem Restoration, and others.
Project Summary: Project Objective: To facilitate, support, and mobilize investment in, smallholder and community-led restoration of critical landscapes to provide global environmental benefits and enhanced resilient economic development and livelihoods, in support of the Bonn Challenge, AFR100, the Trillion Tree Campaign, and other global and national restoration efforts
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: IFAD (EC) Agroecological Transitions for Building Resilient, Inclusive, Agricultural and Food Systems (TRANSITIONS): Private sector incentives and investments
Dscription: Both description and project summary filled. Transforming global food production systems, while also reducing their impact on the natural environment and improving welfare, remains a complex agenda characterized by overlapping trade-offs. Transitioning from “business as usual” requires simultaneous transformation across multiple levels – from socioecological systems, economics, business, and politico-institutional systems – to ensure the adoption of a more regenerative use of natural resources. However, too little attention is paid to the role of the private and public sectors in advancing to agroecological transition pathways. Although many business and private sector stakeholders are aligning to agroecological principles in food systems, there remains concerns among actors on the motivation, incentives, investments, and transparency of private sector initiatives. Under the TRANSITIONS program, the Private Sector Incentives and Investments (PSii) project will fill persistent knowledge gaps on the role of private and public sector incentives and investments in supporting agroecological transitions for farmers in low- and middle-income countries. The project will: (1) Review exisitng incentives mechanisms for supporting the promotion of agroecological practices, design incentive packages for scaling adoption of agroecological practices within agroecology smart landscapes for transitioning rice, wheat and cococa production systems, (2) Apply holistic metrics to digital traceability tools, and implement within at least one supply chain, (3) Improve enabling environment business models for supporting agroecological produced products, (4) Co-design and co-create incentive packages and building local capacity
Project Summary: The PSii project focuses on developing innovative private and public sector incentive pathways for scaling agroecological practices to improve landscapes. Developing a better understanding and application for long-term incentives and transparent private sector engagement to support agroecological transitions for farmers and creating an enabling environment for scaling innovations. Key outcomes include: (1) Supported at least 100,000 households to engage with improved private and public sector incentive models and investments to adopt agroecological practices. (2) Improved the intergration of agroecological principles in transparent supply chains for rice, cocoa, and wheat value chains for at least 50,000 households. (3) Encreased the integration of agroecological metrics in supply chain transparency and traceability tools. (4) Improved policy enabling environment and existing business models for supporting agroecological transition for rice, cocoa and wheat value chains, and (5) Ehanced building local capacity, engaging multi-stakeholder platforms and co-mobilizing private sector investments to support agroecological transitions.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: UK FCDO - Seed to tree: value  chains and partnerships for resilient restored forests in Malaysia
Dscription: Both description and project summary filled. The three-year project contributes to improved species diversity, establishment success rate, and participation of indigenous peoples and local communities (IPLCs) in forest restoration projects, leading into increased carbon sequestration potential, conservation value, habitat connectivity, and jobs and incomes for 200 IPLC (≥30% female) across 220,000 ha of the Central Forest Spine and Heart of Borneo landscapes in three Malaysian states. The developed methods and tools are scalable to other ASEAN member states and will contribute to the implementation of the ASEAN Centre for Biodiversity’s new Ecosystem Restoration Programme.
Project Summary: The three-year project contributes to improved species diversity, establishment success rate, and participation of indigenous peoples and local communities (IPLCs) in forest restoration projects, leading into increased carbon sequestration potential, conservation value, habitat connectivity, and jobs and incomes for 200 IPLC (≥30% female) across 220,000 ha of the Central Forest Spine and Heart of Borneo landscapes in three Malaysian states. The developed methods and tools are scalable to other ASEAN member states and will contribute to the implementation of the ASEAN Centre for Biodiversity’s new Ecosystem Restoration Programme.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: ANONYMOUS DONOR - Fruitful Lands India Phase II: Harnessing Fruit Tree Biodiversity as a Nature-Based Solution for Food Security, Nutrition, Human and Planetary Health
Dscription: Both description and project summary filled. The GLI is supposed to serve as a buffer in maintaining livelihoods and represents natural insurance, providing environmental services such as carbon sequestration, flood protection, surface and groundwater conservation, soil fertility improvement, and preserving biodiversity as well as social, economic, and/or cultural benefits to the local people. Such nature-based government initiatives need support from various stakeholders such as the scientific community and development partners to generate evidence on multiple benefits of the initiative and its sustainability and scalability. Moreover, re-designing such initiatives in a more holistic agroecological approach that transforms food systems from mass-produced commodities into niche-specific and diversified system, which works within natural boundaries and ensure food and nutrition access to the community in a sustainable way, is needed.
Project Summary: CFL Principles are: 1) Be a catalyst and coordinate to fill gaps in current research around fruit trees; 2) Promote research and uptake of research; and 3) Build a reputable and long-lasting institution. These principles will be achieved through three core objectives: 1. Coordinate, promote, catalyze, and fund cross-disciplinary research; 2. Establish a participatory platform for exchange on research and outputs through conferences, workshops, policy papers, and communication products, and 3. Publicize success stories to influence policy makers, funders, and the common public. This document provides a detailed work plan for the next two years of phase 2 of CFL in the 3 phases (10-year program). Nimboli Fund has committed to 5 million dollars from 2024 to 2028. Key activities in the next 2 years include launching the Fruitful India annual conference and calling for research proposals, which will be the primary platform for ensuring CFL's objectives are achieved.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: THE FOOD PLANET PRIZE & THE CURT BERGFORS FOUNDATION Agrobiodiversity Index
Dscription: Both description and project summary filled. The Alliance Agrobiodiversity Index has won the 2023 Food Planet Prize. The Agrobiodiversity Index has a vision of using science and empirical evidence to quantify and measure the sustainability of the food system, and translate this into a quantitative index for farmers, businesses, and policy, in order to accelerate the adoption of sustainable and healthy food systems. Itprovides the means to measure the gap between the agricultural biodiversity we have in our food system, and the thousands of plant and food species and varieties we could have, to help maintain healthy diets and resilient production systems now and in the future.
Project Summary: The Agrobiodiversity Index is useful in a context where Governments and development partners, including multilateral agencies and development banks, needs to design and monitor policies and interventions and measure progress towards global targets. Also, where Companies needs help to reduce risks in the supply chain, enhance environmental stewardship and improve the sustainability of their production, responding to consumers and investors needs. It also supports Global conventions and treaties to monitor how well countries are doing with their commitments towards agrobiodiversity and Investors to rate the policies and performance of food and agriculture companies, and make appropriate decisions. Agrobiodiversity is measured through 3 pillars : 1) Status – the current state of agrobiodiversity in markets and consumption, in agricultural production, and in genetic resource management; 2) Actions – what countries, companies, or projects are concretely doing to increase agrobiodiversity across the food system and 3)Commitments – to what extent country, company or project strategies, policies and codes of conduct are contributing to sustainable use and conservation of agrobiodiversity for healthy diets, sustainable agriculture and future use options.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: F-AG10607-ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Dscription: ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Project Summary: Asia–Africa BlueTech Superhighway (AABS) harnesses South–South collaboration to assess, adapt and scale evidence-based innovations and models for delivering nature-positive impact through aquatic food systems. Implemented by WorldFish in collaboration with a host of partners, AABS aims to transform livelihoods of coastal communities across African and Asian countries, particularly for women and youth, and help restore marine and coastal ecosystems by developing sustainable and resilient aquatic food systems. Funded by the UK Government under its Blue Planet Fund, AABS is being implemented in two phases over the course of seven years (2023–2030).

Name: W-D-0440-AL MURANA
Dscription: AL MURANA –  building climate resilience  through enhanced water security in MENA
Project Summary: The Middle East and North Africa (MENA) is the most water-scarce region in the world. Throughout the MENA region both surface and groundwater resources are over exploited because of increasing water demand. Agriculture is by far the largest user of water and is a major cause of depleted aquifers, rivers and reservoirs. The goal of this project is to increase water security in the MENA region through the integration of Nature Based Solutions for Water (NBSW) and Agricultural Water Management (AWM) in the face of climate change and land degradation.  Implemented by the MENA regional office of the International Water Management Institute (IWMI) in partnership with the International Union for the Conservation of Nature (IUCN), the central premise of this project is that appropriately designed on-the-ground NBSW and AWM interventions can, in tandem, enhance water security in the agricultural regions of the Middle East, thereby increasing the resilience of households and communities. The program will strengthen, empower and catalyse  national capacities for the implementation and up-scaling of integrated NBSW/AWM. Working with government and non-government stakeholders in Jordan, Lebanon, Palestine and Egypt, it will develop action-oriented field examples and recommendations that overcome technical and non-technical barriers to uptake and long-term sustainability of NBSW/AWM. It will generate evidence and understanding of the contribution NBSW/AWM can make to sustainably increase water security. Women, youth and marginalized people will be specifically targeted, with their engagement and input contributing to the delivery of co-developed pragmatic approaches to investing in NBSW/AWM in the region.
Project results: The goal of this project is to increase water security in the MENA region through the integration of Nature Based Solutions for Water (NBSW) and Agricultural Water Management (AWM) in the face of climate change and land degradation.
