The program will strengthen, empower and catalyse  national capacities for the implementation and up-scaling of integrated NBSW/AWM. Working with government and non-government stakeholders in Jordan, Lebanon, Palestine and Egypt, it will develop action-oriented field examples and recommendations that overcome technical and non-technical barriers to uptake and long-term sustainability of NBSW/AWM. It will generate evidence and understanding of the contribution NBSW/AWM can make to sustainably increase water security. Women, youth and marginalized people will be specifically targeted, with their engagement and input contributing to the delivery of co-developed pragmatic approaches to investing in NBSW/AWM in the region.

Name: V0165-ACIAR-ICCCAD
Dscription: ACIAR-ICCCAD
Project Summary: The aim of this project is is to develop and demonstrate locally led learning processes that (i) enable farming communities in the polders of coastal Bangladesh to adapt to climate change challenges in the management of agriculture, (ii) can be institutionalized, securing financial and human social capital to be self-sustaining in the long term, and (iii) that influence and are supported by multi-stakeholder learning and governance.
Center: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center / Centro Internacional de Mejoramiento de Maíz y Trigo

Name: Fertilize Right Colombia
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: N-601170-WASTING DETECTION&TREATME
Dscription: WASTING DETECTION&TREATME
Project Summary: The research activities will be led and coordinated across countries by IFPRI in close collaboration and coordination with local research partners in each country. We will use an iterative process of extensive consultation and discussion with relevant stakeholders in each of the target countries, starting from the research design phase all the way through the implementation, publication, and dissemination of the results. IFPRI will draw from its deep policy research and engagement experience to ensure that findings from the study are effectively translated into action and used for program and policy decisions for the benefit of families and children in Sub Saharan Africa and beyond. The research will include two phases: 1) a design phase of approximately eight months; and 2) a three-year research implementation phase.

Name: P-1529-UNGP-Technical Assistance for Sweetpotato processing
Dscription: Technical Assistance for the Promotion of the Production and Processing of Orange-Fresh Sweet Potatoes of the MIONJO Project
Project Summary: Implemented in the Southern regions of Madagascar, the project seeks to improve livelihood opportunities and resilience of community-based organizations or “Sweet Livelihood Clubs” with women and youth members through the establishment of sustainable seed systems and profitable value chains of vitamin A rich orange-fleshed sweetpotato (OFSP) with a strengthened enabling environment at the commune level.
Center: International Potato Center / Centro Internacional de la Papa

Name: R-A-2023-11-Specialty Rice
Dscription: SpecialtyRice
Project Summary: The major goal of this project is to develop a sustained system for specialty rice in Odisha for improved farm economics of growers and healthier rice choice for consumers.

Name: N-301055-SOCIAL TRANS IMPROV NUTR
Dscription: SOCIAL TRANS IMPROV NUTR
Project Summary: The objective of the study is to assess whether social transfer programs in Bangladesh can be leverage to improve maternal and newborn health and nutrition. In the design phase (about 8 months) we will carry out a literature review to synthesize available evidence; carry out a landscape analysis of social transfer programs in Bangladesh; identify a suitable program for incorporating a maternal nutrition component aimed at improving maternal and newborn health and nutrition outcomes; and design the effectiveness evaluation protocol for the study to be carried out in the second phase. In the second phase (about 3 years), a cluster-randomized controlled longitudinal trial will be carried out to estimate the impact of adding a micronutrient-fortified balanced energy and protein supplement (or an equivalent amount of cash) + behaviour change communication targeted to pregnant women to an existing social transfer program.

Name: N-303028-SMALLHOLDERS ACCESS-SOLAR
Dscription: SMALLHOLDERS ACCESS-SOLAR
Project Summary: This project promotes solar-powered water pumps to address smallholders’ access to renewable energy and improve irrigation efficiency in crisis-prone countries in the Middle East & North Africa, Egypt, and Yemen. Our interventions will provide automatic, solar-powered water pumps for rural communities in Egypt and Yemen that have been impacted by deteriorating food security situations, rising diesel prices, water shortages, and water management challenges (especially after the conflict in Ukraine). Such interventions are expected to improve smallholders’ access to affordable renewable energy while also facilitating sustainable production systems. Solar-Powered Irrigation Systems (SPIS) are particularly appealing in the context of Egypt and Yemen, where irrigation has been widely used for the production of various food items as well as for household water needs, while many smallholders are not yet connected to electric grids. SPIS can improve smallholders’ production potential while addressing future water scarcity and related climate challenges in Egypt and Yemen. In collaboration with a private sector and a Japanese manufacturer, we will promote SPIS by offering subsidized solar-powered water pumps to smallholders. This project will provide these subsidized solar-powered water pumps to farmer groups in both Egypt and Yemen, who will agree to contribute financially and manage these solar-powered water pumps, combined with the necessary training and complementary inputs to promote modern, sustainable practices to maximize yields and quality, and improve nutrition situations through the production of nutrition rich foods such as vegetables and fruits. The project aims to improve smallholders’ production potential and overall livelihood and the impact evaluation built in this work will provide us with pilot nature but rigorous evidence that can be useful to improving the effectiveness and efficiency of Japan’s ODA.

Name: R-A-2023-116-Entrep Rice Value Addition
Dscription: Promoting Self Help Group-led Entrepreneurship through Rice Value Addition
Project Summary: The project´s major purpose is to develop women Self Help Group-led small-scale food enterprises for rice value-added products in Odisha through unique rice and millet-based food items with increased commercial potential by combining: i) milled rice as a premium commodity with nutritional benefits from traditional rice landraces and/or nutritious high yielding varieties of rice grains rich with nutraceuticals carrying human health benefits, and ii) product formulations employing rice by-products such as broken rice and rice bran to develop value-added rice food products. It also aims to provide WSHGs with the skills they need to process and prepare high-quality, nutritious, and marketable food items.

Name: N-301058-POSHAN PH 3
Dscription: POSHAN PH 3
Project Summary: POSHAN was launched in 2011 to identify the major evidence gaps on nutrition and support the use of data and evidence to guide policies and practices for nutrition in India. POSHAN uses a combination of secondary data analyses, policy process studies, and implementation research to highlight malnutrition problems, their determinants, and to identify solutions to address them. An integral part of POSHAN’s work is mobilizing evidence and supporting diverse stakeholders to use evidence for nutrition policy and practice decisions. During the third phase of POSHAN (Sept 1, 2022-Dec 30, 2025), IFPRI proposes work on generating and disseminating high quality evidence and mobilizing nutrition stakeholders around multiple forms of evidence. This work will focus on (1) undernutrition across the lifecycle, (2) anemia, and (3) emerging nutrition challenges. There will also be greater emphasis on the sustainability of the initiative’s impacts, through strengthening the capacity of national institutions (e.g., co-developing research workplans, conducting joint research, co-developing knowledge products and publishing research outputs jointly).

Name: T-PJ-003948: Fair Food and Trade Systems for Africa through Food Convergence Innovation
Dscription: PJ-003948: Fair Food and Trade Systems for Africa through Food Convergence Innovation

Name: T-PJ-003517: Chicken, Fish and Pigs Feed and Organic Fertilizer Value Chain Development Using BSF-Based Urban Biowaste Processing in Ghana, Mali, Niger and the Democratic Republic of Congo
Dscription: PJ-003517: Chicken, Fish and Pigs Feed and Organic Fertilizer Value Chain Development Using BSF-Based Urban Biowaste Processing in Ghana, Mali, Niger and the Democratic Republic of Congo
Project results: Develop a biowaste management business model by analyzing current and future streams. Establish value chains for chicken and fish feed and organic fertilizer. Optimize Black Soldier Fly (BSF) biowaste processing and build capacity for youth agripreneurs and stakeholders.

Name: N-632006-PARES
Dscription: PARTNER SUST EDUC-PARES
Project Summary: The PARES project's purpose is to improve the literacy skills of school-aged children and promote the use of good health, nutrition and diet practices through school meals and literacy programming. IFPRI's research examines strategies to strengthen community involvement in the handover of school meals from World Vision to GoM.

Name: N-306005-NUTRITION STATUS-BIHAR&OD
Dscription: NUTRITION STATUS-BIHAR&OD
Project Summary: This program will lay the foundation to scale and mainstream biofortification globally in Bihar, Odisha, and India as a whole, serving as a springboard for proliferation of biofortified crops throughout these states and those bordering, impacting the entire value chain. For this purpose, HarvestPlus will work to compile the evidence base on the nutritional impact, cost effectiveness and scalability of biofortification throughout this process for future application. It is anticipated that this program will result in improved micronutrient intake for over a million people within five years and millions more in the years to follow and will demonstrate the capacity of agriculture to make a significant contribution in the fight against micronutrient malnutrition.

Name: N-606021-MEAL PROGRAMS INDIA
Dscription: MEAL PROGRAMS INDIA
Project Summary: The proposed program intends to reach two million children with biofortified food products every school day to combat high levels of micronutrient deficiency, providing a roadmap to scale the approach through the national program which reaches 120 million students. The project will be implanted in three stages with the following interventions: A. Demand: 1. Menu development, sensory evaluation and nutritional efficacy trials. 2. Roll out of selected menus to subset of blocks in aspirational districts through AP kitchens. 3. Full coverage for high priority districts in collaboration with government and other non-profits. B. Supply: 1. Supply feasibility study keeping sustainability in view. 2. Aggregating traceable supply of millet through clustered farmer producer groups, and market links with school feeding procurement system. Community engagement and training on nutrition awareness and to traders, processors to meet SQMS requirements and traceability. Flagship program’s effectiveness trial. 3. Expansion in high priority districts through commercial linkages and government support.

Name: N-606024-KTM SCHOOL FEED
Dscription: KTM SCHOOL FEED
Project Summary: The Grant aims to contribute to improve the nutrition of school-going age children through increased demand for biofortified crops/foods in school meals in Kenya, Malawi, and Tanzania.

Name: N-644H06-HP-WV-REACTS-IN-Finance
Dscription: HP-WV-REACTS-IN
Project Summary: Intervening at the community level to address the multi-sectoral issues needed to reclaim the nutrition gains lost and build resilience, the Project will improve nutrition, nutrition-related rights, and gender equality for the poorest women, adolescent girls, and children in Bangladesh (Thakugaron district), Kenya (Elgeyo Marakwet County), Somalia (Baidoa district) and Tanzania (Meatu and Maswa districts of Simiyu Region). The REACTS-IN Project will support the strengthening of nutrition and health systems with a focus on the gender dimensions of malnutrition. Activities are designed to: i) foster behavior change; ii) strengthen systems and community based service delivery; and iii) connect community groups’ voices to advocacy and policy platforms to increase their ability to influence decision-making on resources at the sub-national and national levels.

Name: N-644H03-HP–GAC–ENFS
Dscription: HP–GAC–ENFS
Project Summary: Expanding the reach of nutrient-enriched staples for sustainable food systems transformation with the goal of improved access to and control of nutritious climte-smart seed, food, and knowledge, for vulnerable communities, especially women and girls, in Bangladesh, Pakistan, India, Uganda, and Zimbabwe.

Name: N-644H12 - NUTRIHARVEST
Dscription: HP-Cargill-Nutriharvest
Project Summary: Improving Food Security and Dietary Diversity through Biofortification, Underutilized Crops, and Poultry

Name: R-A-2024-145-Healthier Rice
Dscription: Healthier Rice
Project Summary: This is a co-funding by USAID in support of the main BMGF investment that aims to obtain regulatory approvals and see the deployment of Golden Rice not only in Bangladesh and the Philippines but as well in Indonesia. obtain regulatory approvals for high iron and zinc rice (HIZR), and initiate the stacking of Golden Rice and HIZR traits in important rice varieties.

Name: P-1490-DFAT-Generating Revenues and Opportunities for Women
Dscription: Generating Revenues and Opportunities for Women to Improve Nutrition in Ghana (GROWING)
Project Summary: The Generating Revenues for improved nutrition in northern Ghana (GROWING) project targets women of reproductive age (15-49 years) and their children from rural households in the north of Ghana from across an estimated 72 villages. The ultimate outcome of the initiative is the improved nutritional and financial security of women, youth, and young children in 6 selected districts. This outcome will be achieved through a multi-sectoral nutrition-sensitive value chain development approach, using activities such as: (1) providing Healthy Baby Toolkits, Goal cards, planting materials of orange-fleshed sweet potato, moringa and papaya to Healthy Living Club participant households; (2) producing gender-responsive videos, related media tools, and collaborations for facilitating training activities and nutritious crop uptake in targeted communities: and (3) providing technical assistance in consultation with communities to identify and train male and female gender champions.
Center: International Potato Center / Centro Internacional de la Papa

Name: PJ-003978: Cassava Source Sink (CASS) Phase III
Dscription: FAU/IITA- Cassava Source Sink (CASS) Phase III- PJ-3978
Project Summary: This third phase of the Cassava Source Sink Project aims to significantly enhance cassava yield and quality on smallholder farms in Sub-Saharan Africa by optimizing plant physiology and source-sink relationships. This project's objectives are to develop nutrient-efficient genotypes with improved sink capacity, modify the shoot-root ratio and enhance traits like photosynthesis and starch accumulation through genetic engineering and breeding. The expected outputs of the project include high-yield, nutrient-rich cassava plants and a comprehensive database (CASSbase) for future research. This project targets increased cassava production, which will boost food security and economic stability for smallholder farmers and benefit agricultural researchers and local economies.
Project results: Develop high-yield, nutritionally enhanced cassava genotypes through genetic and metabolic engineering. Increase smallholder cassava production for food security and economic stability. Establish CASSbase, a comprehensive database for research and breeding initiatives.

Name: T-PJ-003835-EU/IITA - FOOD SAFETY FOR AFRICA - ADDRESSING FO
Dscription: EU/IITA - FOOD SAFETY FOR AFRICA - ADDRESSING FOOD SAFETY CHALLENGES IN THE AFRICAN INFORMAL SECTOR THROUGH INNOVATIVE STRATEGIES & USE CASES (RAFS)-PJ-3835...T.FALADE
Project results: Generate GAP analysis and landscape reports on the informal sector with evidence-based recommendations. Develop food safety knowledge platforms, directories, and evaluation reports. Establish communication, advocacy, and innovation plans, alongside ethical committee setup, work plans, and data management guidelines.
Countries: South Africa, Nigeria, Kenya, Ghana, Egypt, Cameroon

Name: Enhancing Crop Productivity and Climate Resilience for Food and Nutrition Security in Mali (FNS-Mali)
Dscription: Enhancing Crop Productivity and Climate Resilience for Food and Nutrition Security in Mali
Project Summary: The project aims to demonstrate that circular food systems can decouple growth in social and economic benefits from consumption of natural resources (particularly water and land) and production of greenhouse gas emissions in agriculture, reducing urbanization by creating local opportunities for the women and youth. We will test how smallholder irrigation schemes might become the engine of local resilience and economic development by being integrated with livestock, and dryland agricultural production to support strategic and inclusive development of small, value-adding business to form climate smart circular food systems in East and Southern Africa.
Center: International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics

Name: N-303026-CHILD SCHOOL LUNCH-GHANA
Dscription: CHILD SCHOOL LUNCH-GHANA
Project Summary: School meals intervention is a multi-sectoral approach that plays an important role in long-term human capital formation by improving young children’s nutrition conditions and learning performance. In many developing countries, however, sustainable school meal systems have not been introduced due to a lack of suitable food, appropriate school facilities, certified dieticians, and so on, which is a serious problem when people live under the current threat of food insecurity and food price spike, mainly caused by the Ukraine-Russia war, often exacerbated by local political instability, social fragility, and conflict and violence. In collaboration with Japanese experts working on school lunch from the University of Niigata Prefecture and Japan Society of Nutrition and Dietetics, we plan to introduce a sustainable school meals intervention aiming to improve and upscale the school lunch program in Ghana by redesigning menus and strengthening linkages to local agriculture based on Japan’s experience in the school lunch program. The intervention will improve child nutritional status, health and learning performance, thus increasing resilience of the school system, children, and local livelihoods to food insecurity crisis and COVID-19, which has been negatively impacting economic activities, household income, and nutrition intake of young children.

Name: T-PJ-003911-CGIAR Genetic Innovations Public-Private Partnership Platform
Dscription: CGIAR Genetic Innovations Public-Private Partnership Platform
Project Summary: The Genetic Innovations-Public Private Partnership (GI-PPP) platform aims to advance the modernization of Genetic Innovations (GI) by facilitating collaborations with private sector companies. The platform's main objective is to streamline the engagement process between these companies and CGIAR breeding programs, reducing transaction costs through a specialized unit adept in forming and managing partnerships. By creating at least five robust public-private partnerships, the GI-PPP intends to significantly enhance the effectiveness of GI breeding programs. These partnerships are expected to bring substantial in-kind contributions from the private sector, which will help offset modernization costs and accelerate the adoption of innovative breeding technologies.
Project results: Develop five public-private partnerships, securing private sector funding and in-kind investments for CG crop breeding. Implement industry best practices across Genetic Innovation (GI) breeding programs and integrate the GI public-private partnership platform into core Genetic Innovation programming.

Name: EC Combating malnutrition in Africa through Diversification of the Food system (HealthyDiets4Africa)
Dscription: Both description and project summary filled. Various forms of malnutrition, including hunger, micronutrient deficiencies and non-communicable diseases caused by unbalanced nutrition, threaten food security in Africa. The project HealthyDiest4Africa is based on the central hypothesis that diversification of the food system helps to combat all forms of malnutrition while minimizing its environmental footprint. To this aim, we monitor dietary diversity in eight African countries representing different regions, and develop target group-specific metrics to measure the diversity of the food system and its health outcomes. A production-oriented cluster of the project explore environmentally sustainable options to diversity food production that specifically address the nutritional requirements identified for the target populations. This includes the development of novel and biofortified crop varieties, utilization of orphan crops with high nutritional value, and diversification of cropping systems and food processing technology. We link these efforts with a consumption-oriented cluster exploring the consumer acceptance, food safety, and nutritional health effects of diet diversification options. The best solutions that contribute to healthy diets via diversification while also promoting environmental health will be up-scaled via a network of food system stakeholders in eight African countries. HealthyDiets4Africa also devise communication and policy strategies to maximize outreach and impact of diversified food systems. Thereby the project help to develop safe, healthy and affordable diets by adopting a diversification strategy that simultaneously reduce the pressure of food production on the environment
Project Summary: The project contributes to better informed nutrition policies that can be scaled-up by initiatives such as SUN (scaling-up nutrition) and UNFSS (United Food Systems Summit) post-processes via the following outputs: (i) Understanding of dietary habits across sub-Saharan Africa and intervention effects on dietary diversity; (ii) Novel and simple a priori indices that reflect total dietary diversity, quality for resource-poor settings developed and food environment risk factors for malnutrition; (iii) Identification of gender and nutritional health sensitive food environment indicators and metrics associated with malnutrition among different societal groups. Moreover, the project results contribute to improved nutrition in African countries reducing all forms of malnutrition through safe, healthy and affordable diets, including dietary shifts that reduce the pressure of food production on land and water use, and reduces the climate footprint. Outputs contributing to this aim include: (i) A database of vitamins, microelement contents and glycaemic value of traditional landraces, wild species, and breeding materials generated. (ii) Methods to increase the productivity and sustainability of diversified healthy foods documented; (iii) Policy debates, policies briefs, advocacy and communication tools that favor accessibility, affordability and sustainability of healthy food production and consumption elaborated and tested.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)
Countries: Ghana, Cameroon, Uganda, Benin, Liberia, Kenya, Nigeria

Name: N-606015-BIO-FORTIFICATION DEPLOYM
Dscription: BIO-FORTIFICATION DEPLOYM
Project Summary: The DRC government has secured significant funding (US$500 million) from its partner, the World Bank, for the implementation of a multi-sector nutrition and health program (PMNS) to increase the use of specific nutrition and sensory nutrition services targeting children aged 0 to 23 months, pregnant women and breastfeeding women living in the intervention areas of the four provinces targeted by the PMNS, namely Kasai, Kasai Central, South Kivu, and Kwilu. Harvest Plus, in collaboration with the Ministry of Agriculture will support the increase in agricultural production and the availability of organic fortified varieties of staple crops at the household level. This will include activities related to development, maintenance, improving agronomic performance, spreading organic fortified crops promoted by the project, with the help of INERA for the production of basic seeds. The aim is to contribute to improving the nutritional status of vulnerable targeted households through the availability and accessibility of micronutrient-rich foods by promoting sustainable food systems that meet food needs.

Name: Bihar Centre Of Excellence For Millets Value Chain (BCoEMVC)
Dscription: Bihar Centre Of Excellence For Millets Value Chain (BCoEMVC)
Project Summary:
ICRISAT, in collaboration with the Government of Bihar, will establish a “Centre of Excellence for Millets and Value Chain (CoE-MVC)”, in collaboration with the Government of Bihar will establish a “Centre of Excellence for Millets and Value Chain (CoE-MVC)”, to promote research, breeding, production, mechanization, and value chain as a long-term vision for sustainable promotion of millets to diversify its crop and food systems. Overall, this concept aims to enhance Bihar’s crop and food systems by addressing the challenges of Bihar state with respect to nutritional deficiencies, food insecurity, climate risk and low farm income through crop diversification and value chain development activities. 
As part of the project, ICRISAT will conduct a techno-economic feasibility study and develop Ready-to-Cook (RTC) and Ready-to-Eat (RTE) millet products, which will be integrated into the state's mid-day meal program.
Center: International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics

Name: N333009
Dscription: Behavioral Change Nutrition Education
Project Summary: School meals intervention is a multi-sectoral approach that plays an important role in long-term human capital formation by improving young children’s nutrition conditions and learning performance. In many developing countries, however, sustainable school meal systems have not been introduced due to a lack of suitable food, appropriate school facilities, certified dieticians, and so on, which is a serious problem when people live under the current threat of food insecurity and food price spike, exacerbated by Covid-19 and the recent Ukraine-Russia war, often exacerbated by local political instability, social fragility, and conflict and violence. In collaboration with Japanese experts working on school lunch from the City of Niigata and the University of Niigata Prefecture (Japanese Society of Nutrition and Dietetics), we plan to introduce a systematic school-based nutrition education – Shoku-iku intervention aiming to improve the effectiveness of school lunch program in Cote d’Ivoire. The intervention includes training of specialists from Cote d’Ivoire in Niigata. The intervention will create behavioral change among children and families and thus improve child nutritional status, health and learning performance, that together increase resilience of the school system, children, and local livelihoods to food insecurity crisis.

Name: F-AG10607-ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Dscription: ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Project Summary: Asia–Africa BlueTech Superhighway (AABS) harnesses South–South collaboration to assess, adapt and scale evidence-based innovations and models for delivering nature-positive impact through aquatic food systems. Implemented by WorldFish in collaboration with a host of partners, AABS aims to transform livelihoods of coastal communities across African and Asian countries, particularly for women and youth, and help restore marine and coastal ecosystems by developing sustainable and resilient aquatic food systems. Funded by the UK Government under its Blue Planet Fund, AABS is being implemented in two phases over the course of seven years (2023–2030).

Name: PJ-003953: APPUI A LA TRANSFORMATION ET A LA RESILIENCE DES SYSTEMES ALIMENTAIRES DE MADAGASCAR A TRAVERS LA RECHERCHE POUR LE DEVELOPPEMENT
Dscription: AfricaRice/IITA- APPUI A LA TRANSFORMATION ET A LA RESILIENCE DES SYSTEMES ALIMENTAIRES DE MADAGASCAR A TRAVERS LA RECHERCHE POUR LE DEVELOPPEMENT
Project results: The project focused on strengthening research and agricultural development through capacity building, infrastructure enhancement, and improved seed systems. Key outputs included research center evaluations, technical training for scientists and staff, development of variety registration guides, and studies on production systems. It also established demonstration sites, introduced climate-smart agricultural equipment, improved seed and livestock varieties, and supported seed enterprises. Additionally, it promoted disease management and agronomic best practices among farmers.

Name: PJ-003938: Food Systems Resilience Program interventions in Feed Salone
Dscription: MAFFS/IITA - FOOD SYSTEMS RESILIENCE PROGRAM (FSRP) INTERVENTIONS IN FEED SALONE (SIERRA LEONE) (RAFS) PJ-3938
Project results: Introduce mechanized irrigation, develop a sustainable seed and input system, and enhance value chains for inclusive growth. Create business and partnership opportunities, develop business plans for women and youth, and provide regular reports on processes, activities, and business models.

Name: A-AG10156 AICCRA PROJECT
Dscription: A-AICCRA
Project Summary: The AICCRA (Accelerating Impacts of CGIAR Climate Research for Africa) project, implemented by AfricaRice, is designed to enhance climate resilience in African agriculture. This initiative focuses on integrating climate change adaptation strategies into agricultural practices to address the impacts of a changing climate on food systems. The AICCRA project employs a multi-dimensional approach to build resilience among smallholder farmers and agricultural communities. It emphasizes the development and promotion of climate-smart agricultural practices, including the adoption of drought-resistant crop varieties, improved soil management techniques, and efficient water use strategies. These measures aim to improve productivity while mitigating the negative effects of climate variability. The project also focuses on strengthening agricultural research and extension services to provide farmers with timely and relevant information about climate adaptation. By fostering partnerships with local research institutions, government agencies, and farmer organizations, AICCRA ensures that adaptation strategies are tailored to the specific needs and conditions of different regions.
Project results: A-AG10156 AICCRA PROJECT

Name: D-200086-Ultra-Low Energy Drip Irrigation for MENA Countries
Dscription: Ultra-Low Energy Drip Irrigation for MENA Countries
Project Summary: Not available yet
Center: International Center for Agricultural Research in the Dry Areas

Name: R-A-2023-24-ASEAN-CGIAR Innovate for Food Regional Program
Dscription: ASEAN-CGIAR Innovate for Food Regional Program
Project Summary: The ASEAN-CGIAR Innovate for Food regional program (IFRP) aims to scale up and out bold integrated innovations that will make ASEAN agri-food systems more resilient in the face of climate change while delivering better livelihoods for food producers and other stakeholders along the associated value chains, as well as more affordable, nutritious and healthy food for consumers and a better natural environment for all in the next ten years.
Countries: Philippines, Thailand, Indonesia, Singapore, Malaysia, Cambodia, Myanmar
Funder: Fundación para el Desarrollo Empresarial y Agrícola

Name: R-A-2022-118-Credits Offsetting Rice Emissions
Dscription: Credits Offsetting
Project Summary: The project´s core objective is to facilitate enabling conditions for changing smallholder farmers and value chain actors´ access to the carbon market. This will be achieved by improving the evidence-base for combined emission reduction practices, developing a new methodology for generating carbon credits from shifting current crop management, analyzing emissions through post-harvest losses, guiding policy and developing support tools for partner organizations’ extension teams and farmers based on needs.

Name: R0223-Mining useful alleles for climate change adaptation
Dscription: Mining useful alleles for climate change adaptation from CGIAR gene banks
Project Summary: The project will support faster and more cost-effective discovery and deployment of climate-adaptive alleles from the world’s germplasm collections. It will test integrated approaches for five major crops (i.e., cassava, maize, sorghum, cowpea, and rice), providing a scalable model for the rapid and cost-effective discovery and deployment of climate-adaptive alleles.
Center: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center / Centro Internacional de Mejoramiento de Maíz y Trigo
Countries: Philippines, Kenya, Mexico, United States of America

Name: D0001-SGP-AVISA Accelerated varietal improvement and seed delivery of legumes and cereals in Africa
Dscription: SGP-AVISA Accelerated varietal improvement and seed delivery of legumes and cereals in Africa
Project Summary: The Accelerated Varietal Improvement and Seed Alliance (AVISA) – Transition Investment is a strategic initiative led by CIMMYT and funded by the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation (BMGF) to enhance breeding and seed delivery systems for key dryland cereal and legume crops in Sub-Saharan Africa. The project focuses on developing climate-resilient, high-yielding crop varieties that improve productivity and adaptability for small-scale farmers. Through collaborative breeding programs, technology transfer, and private sector engagement, AVISA supports the scaling of superior varieties while strengthening local seed systems to ensure long-term sustainability. The initiative also emphasizes gender inclusion, farmer capacity-building, and policy advocacy to facilitate the widespread adoption of improved crop varieties. By integrating cutting-edge research with on-the-ground implementation, AVISA aims to enhance food security, boost farmer resilience to climate change, and improve agricultural livelihoods in the region.
Center: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center / Centro Internacional de Mejoramiento de Maíz y Trigo

Name: R0230-Mining useful alleles for climate change adaptation
Dscription: Mining useful alleles for climate change adaptation from CGIAR gene banks
Project Summary: The project builds on BMGF's investment in allele mining for climate adaptation by enhancing the discovery and deployment of climate-adaptive alleles from global germplasm collections. It will expand to include common bean and extend maize deployment, focusing on breeding programs in Guatemala, Honduras, and El Salvador. The initiative aims to test integrated approaches and develop a scalable model for rapid and cost-effective deployment of climate-adaptive alleles from CGIAR genebanks.
Center: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center / Centro Internacional de Mejoramiento de Maíz y Trigo
Countries: Colombia, Mexico, Honduras, El Salvador, Guatemala

Name: V0230 - Potential climate change effects on food security, nutrition, and health of the Mexican population.
Dscription: Potential climate change effects on food security, nutrition, and health of the Mexican population.
Project Summary: The Potential Climate Change Effects on Food Security, Nutrition, and Health in Mexico project is a collaborative research initiative led by CIMMYT, aiming to assess and mitigate the impacts of climate change on food systems, nutrition, and public health. The project focuses on analyzing food market dynamics under climate stress, identifying crop production vulnerabilities (especially in maize and beans), and evaluating health implications linked to dietary shifts. A key component is the co-design of integrated policy solutions, engaging stakeholders through participatory workshops and discussions. The project collaborates with SINSAMAC, a multi-stakeholder food policy system, and leverages CIMMYT’s Innovation Hubs to facilitate policy development and knowledge-sharing. By generating risk maps, datasets, and policy briefs, the initiative provides science-driven recommendations for improving food security, nutrition, and resilience to climate change, ultimately contributing to evidence-based decision-making and sustainable food systems in Mexico.
Center: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center / Centro Internacional de Mejoramiento de Maíz y Trigo

Name: USA - USAID - Business Acceleration for Youth project (BAFY)
Project Summary:
The Business Acceleration for Youth Project (formally known as the Youth Business Acceleration and
Investment Facility) is a three-year (2021-2024), innovative initiative funded by USAID Malawi and led by the
Alliance of Bioversity International and International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT). Framed around
a resilience-led sustainability strategy, the project aims to strengthen the resilience of youth and women-led
business start-ups in Balaka, Chikwawa, Chiradzulu, Machinga, Mangochi, Mulanje, Thyolo, Nsanje and
Zomba, through the delivery of business incubation services creating growth-orientated, sustainable firms
able to secure and effectively utilize investment.
Nationally, the project is supporting Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) through the delivery of bespoke
technical support, industry specific expertise, mentorship and access to finance. The purpose of the project
is to accelerate and invest in youth-led enterprises that result in job creation, greater access to financing,
diversified incomes, a more inclusive private sector and improved household resilience in vulnerable districts.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: R-A-2024-191 AGRI
Dscription: Accelerating development and scaling of agricultural innovations for reducing  GHG emissions in ASEAN countries
Project Summary: This project will install new gas chromatography (known as GC) in the soil laboratory, IRRI Headquarters in Los Baños, Philippines. The GC will be used for training programs and the provision of GHG measurement services. Furthermore, training on data analytics, including the application of modeling work that will support scaling of the Tier 3 approach in GHG estimates, will be organized to enhance the capacity of IRRI staff and partners in analyzing GHG emission data and optimizing rice-based farming systems. The training program will be open to participants from countries beyond the ASEAN region, while the GC service will primarily focus on supporting activities within the Philippines.
Project results: Accelerate the development and scaling of agricultural innovations for reducing GHG emissions in ASEAN countries

Name: L-CIA023-Accelerating Impacts of CGIAR Climate Research for Africa II
Dscription: Accelerating Impacts of CGIAR Climate Research for Africa II
Project Summary: This project aims to enhance the resilience of African agriculture to climate change by accelerating the adoption of climate-smart agricultural practices and technologies. The project focuses on scaling up research innovations that address climate risks, improve food security, and support sustainable livelihoods for smallholder farmers across Africa.

Name: R-A-2024-18
Dscription: Accelerating Scaling of Low-Emissions Rice in Southeast Asia
Project Summary: The AcceLER project will support national governments in Southeast Asia for accelerated dissemination of low-emission rice technologies and practices. It will focus on two main aspects: (1) working closely with national partners to co-develop roadmaps for national implementation towards an aspirational 15% reduction of methane emissions of the target countries’ rice sectors by 2030; and (2) developing and mainstreaming remote sensing-enabled monitoring, reporting and verification (MRV) systems that allow better targeting of technology dissemination and tracking of progress towards the 2030 goals.

Name: V0165-ACIAR-ICCCAD
Dscription: ACIAR-ICCCAD
Project Summary: The aim of this project is is to develop and demonstrate locally led learning processes that (i) enable farming communities in the polders of coastal Bangladesh to adapt to climate change challenges in the management of agriculture, (ii) can be institutionalized, securing financial and human social capital to be self-sustaining in the long term, and (iii) that influence and are supported by multi-stakeholder learning and governance.
Center: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center / Centro Internacional de Mejoramiento de Maíz y Trigo

Name: PJ-003953: APPUI A LA TRANSFORMATION ET A LA RESILIENCE DES SYSTEMES ALIMENTAIRES DE MADAGASCAR A TRAVERS LA RECHERCHE POUR LE DEVELOPPEMENT
Dscription: AfricaRice/IITA- APPUI A LA TRANSFORMATION ET A LA RESILIENCE DES SYSTEMES ALIMENTAIRES DE MADAGASCAR A TRAVERS LA RECHERCHE POUR LE DEVELOPPEMENT

It also established demonstration sites, introduced climate-smart agricultural equipment, improved seed and livestock varieties, and supported seed enterprises. Additionally, it promoted disease management and agronomic best practices among farmers.

Name: W-D-0440-AL MURANA
Dscription: AL MURANA –  building climate resilience  through enhanced water security in MENA
Project Summary: The Middle East and North Africa (MENA) is the most water-scarce region in the world. Throughout the MENA region both surface and groundwater resources are over exploited because of increasing water demand. Agriculture is by far the largest user of water and is a major cause of depleted aquifers, rivers and reservoirs. The goal of this project is to increase water security in the MENA region through the integration of Nature Based Solutions for Water (NBSW) and Agricultural Water Management (AWM) in the face of climate change and land degradation.  Implemented by the MENA regional office of the International Water Management Institute (IWMI) in partnership with the International Union for the Conservation of Nature (IUCN), the central premise of this project is that appropriately designed on-the-ground NBSW and AWM interventions can, in tandem, enhance water security in the agricultural regions of the Middle East, thereby increasing the resilience of households and communities. The program will strengthen, empower and catalyse  national capacities for the implementation and up-scaling of integrated NBSW/AWM. Working with government and non-government stakeholders in Jordan, Lebanon, Palestine and Egypt, it will develop action-oriented field examples and recommendations that overcome technical and non-technical barriers to uptake and long-term sustainability of NBSW/AWM. It will generate evidence and understanding of the contribution NBSW/AWM can make to sustainably increase water security. Women, youth and marginalized people will be specifically targeted, with their engagement and input contributing to the delivery of co-developed pragmatic approaches to investing in NBSW/AWM in the region.

Name: F-AG10607-ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Dscription: ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Project Summary: Asia–Africa BlueTech Superhighway (AABS) harnesses South–South collaboration to assess, adapt and scale evidence-based innovations and models for delivering nature-positive impact through aquatic food systems. Implemented by WorldFish in collaboration with a host of partners, AABS aims to transform livelihoods of coastal communities across African and Asian countries, particularly for women and youth, and help restore marine and coastal ecosystems by developing sustainable and resilient aquatic food systems. Funded by the UK Government under its Blue Planet Fund, AABS is being implemented in two phases over the course of seven years (2023–2030).

Name: W-D-0536 - Blue Peace Central Asia Initiative 2.0 (BPCA 2.0)
Dscription: Blue Peace Central Asia Initiative 2.0 (BPCA 2.0)
Project Summary: The overall goal of Blue Peace Central Asia 2.0 is that “Central Asian populations benefit from equitably shared and effectively managed common water resources thanks to climate resilient and sustainable regional water management as a basis for socio-economic development and stability.”
Project results:
cooperation and governance in Central Asia, ultimately contributing to regional stability, climate resilience, and sustainable development.  
This will be achieved through equitably shared and effectively managed common water resources, Small Transboundary Tributaries STTs in Central Asia collaborate and implement joint management and investment plans and development programs and thirdly through enhanced skilled community of next-generation water specialists dedicated to advancing cooperation in water resource management across Central Asia.

Name: Building Community Resilience by Alleviating Water scarcity and Land Degradation through Integrated Natural Resource Management in Bundelkhand, Uttar Pradesh
Dscription: Building Community Resilience by Alleviating Water scarcity and Land Degradation through Integrated Natural Resource Management in Bundelkhand, Uttar Pradesh
Project Summary:
The proposed project follows an integrated approach for resource creation, management and livelihood improvement. Following are the key features of the project:
- Creation and management of natural resources
- Diversified farming system
- Institutional and capacity building
Objectives of the proposed project:
The overall objective of the proposal is to build resilient farming communities and, this is proposed to be achieved by
• Enhancing groundwater availability and reducing land degradation through intensive rainwater harvesting and appropriate natural resource management technologies;
• Developing capacity of the farming community through knowledge sharing and farmer participatory research /demonstrations;
• Integrating crop-livestock-tree based farming for diversifying rural livelihood; and
• Facilitating market linkages through promotion of collective action and community-based organization
Center: International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics

Name: F-PP-2022-1020-Climate-Resilient Aquaculture Systems for Afr (AG10618)
Dscription: Climate-Resilient Aquaculture Systems for Africa (CASA)
Project Summary: WorldFish and partners are Implementing the “Climate-Resilient Aquaculture Systems for Africa - CASA” in Kenya and Nigeria. CASA delivers solutions that simultaneously increase productivity and profitability, climate adaptation and environmental sustainability, food and nutrition security, and the expansion of employment and income opportunities within the aquaculture systems. CASA has four Interlinked Intervention Areas (IAs). Under IA1, CASA will deliver income and productivity gains for most of the farmers, with an estimated 1x - 2x increase in productivity for the average farmer per cycle, and the best-performing farmers obtaining 3x increase in productivity per cycle. Under IA2, CASA aims to reach 1,000 fish processors with improved, climate-smart fish processing technologies and practices. Under IA3, CASA will support the development and piloting of financial products and services which target the aquaculture value chains. Under IA4, CASA will support the strengthening of the policy environment and the capacity development of policymakers.

Name: BIUSA (BEF)-Screening, developing, and deploying anti-methanogenic feedstock into livestock systems in the Global South
Dscription:
Description and project summary both filled. The "Screening, Developing, and Deploying Anti-Methanogenic Feedstock into Livestock Systems in the Global South" project aims to reduce methane (CH4) emissions from livestock, the single largest emitter in the agricultural sector, by leveraging the potential of novel compounds in CGIAR’s global collections of forages. This initiative involves screening, developing, and deploying anti-methanogenic feedstock into livestock systems in the Global South to achieve a significant reduction in greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, equivalent to taking 7 million internal combustion engine cars off the road each year. Scope and Activities:
1. Discovery (WP1):
o Screen CGIAR gene bank collections to identify accessions with high levels of anti-methanogenic compounds (AMCs).
o Conduct metabolomics analyses to identify known and novel AMCs.
o Validate the anti-methanogenic activity of selected accessions through in vivo and in vitro assays.
2. Development (WP2):
o Promote the use of AMC-rich forage legumes directly in grazing systems.
o Enhance AMC levels in widely used forage-grass cultivars through conventional breeding and gene-editing techniques.
3. Deployment (WP3):
o Establish public-private partnerships for large-scale multi-environment feeding/grazing trials.
o Develop Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification (MRV) systems for AMC-rich forages.
o Facilitate the adoption of AMC-rich forages in target regions.
Project Summary: The "Screening, Developing, and Deploying Anti-Methanogenic Feedstock into Livestock Systems in the Global South" project targets a significant reduction in methane emissions from livestock. By leveraging CGIAR’s global collections of forages, the project will screen, develop, and deploy anti-methanogenic feedstock, aiming to mitigate greenhouse gas emissions on a large scale. The project involves three main phases: discovery of AMC-rich forages, development of these forages through breeding and gene-editing, and deployment in livestock systems through public-private partnerships. Major outcomes include reduced methane emissions, improved livestock productivity, and enhanced sustainability of agricultural practices in the Global South
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: PREPARATION PHASE - "Supporting Colombia’s Just Energy Transition, climate resilience, and food security through the introduction of inclusive agri-photovoltaic systems" (JET-AgriSOL)
Dscription: Description and project summary both filled. To support the Colombian Government in achieving its sustainable and Just Energy Transition goal, and meeting its agricultural and energy sectoral NDC targets through the nationwide expansion of photovoltaic capacity (impact), farmer communities have to co-develop and adopt inclusive and economically viable agri-photovoltaic (APV) solutions that can be further scaled through SDG-compliant public and private investments (outcome). This development will be initiated during the project’s first piloting phase, consisting of an APV pilot that will be installed on CIAT’s campus to carry out “higher-risk” research activities (output III). During the second piloting phase, lower-risk research activities will be conducted under real conditions on medium-scale APV pilots, on horticultural, arable, and pastoral land (output I). The diverse APV pilots form the basis for knowledge co-creation and serve for demonstration purposes, thereby promoting APV innovation-uptake and -scaling. Furthermore, women and men members of rural communities will be trained through a new praxis-oriented, and gender-responsive APV curriculum that will be developed and implemented with SENA’s (Colombia’s National Training Service) in-situ Rural Entrepreneurship (SER) program, focussing especially on developing capacities of disadvantaged groups (output II). This holistic skills development empowers rural community members to become more climate resilient and take advantage of new income opportunities. Finally, during the project’s third piloting phase, lessons learned from the experimental and medium-scale pilots will be integrated into the implementation of community-scale APV pilots, e.g., on the land of cacao producer organisations in PDET municipalities. Community-scale APV pilot will expand the green energy capacity of producer organisations and their communities, while promoting forest- and landscape-restoration activities (output I). The community-scale pilots play a key role in scaling APV systems and generating positive environmental impacts, while contributing to the energy-sovereignty of vulnerable rural communities. Moreover, inclusive and accessible APV-tailored financial mechanisms will be co-designed with local and international financial institutions (IFIs), further attracting investments to the APV sector, and enabling and encouraging small-scale farmers to adopt APV solutions (output IV). To further facilitate the inclusive adoption and scaling of APV systems, key binding constraints in the enabling environment will be identified and addressed through a multistakeholder process (output V).
Project Summary: The proposed project will support the Colombian Government in achieving its Just Energy Transition objective and NDC targets. By introducing and scaling APV systems, the project will contribute to promoting the energy-sovereignty and climate resilience of small-scale farmers, reducing GHG emissions, empowering women and youth to improve their livelihoods, and enhancing biodiversity. The project will set up micro- and medium-scale pilot and demonstration APV systems. Capacities for managing APV systems and combining them with climate smart agriculture will be strengthened by co-creating a vocational APV training program with Colombia´s National Training Service. Inclusive, participatory and interdisciplinary research will be conducted on the pilot APV systems, and findings will be shared with a broad range of stakeholders. Further, context-specific and inclusive APV finance mechanisms will be co-designed, and public and private stakeholders supported to upgrade the enabling environment.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: 1414-EC00 DESIRA
Dscription: DeSIRA- Climate-smart innovations to improve productivity, profitability, and sustainability of agriculture and food systems in Malawi through multidisciplinary research
Project Summary: The goal of the project is to improve productivity, profitability and sustainability of agriculture and food systems in Malawi. Activities will aim at achieving three specific objectives. 1) develop climate-smart integrated technological innovations that can contribute to enhanced resilience of agricultural and food systems; 2) gaining insights in pathways to adoption of technological innovation; 3) information that is of interest to policy makers and scaling partner will be generated and shared with local, national, and international policy makers and scaling partners.
Center: International Potato Center / Centro Internacional de la Papa

Name: W-P-1482-Climate Water Nexus IWRM in Niger Basin
Dscription: Federal Ministry of Economics and Climate Protection (BMWK)
Project Summary: The project aims to improve implementation of transboundary Integrated Water Resource Management (IWRM) measures for climate change adaptation (CCA) and mitigation (CCM) in the Niger basin, which will drive long-term positive impacts for the resilience of livelihoods and ecosystems. Capacities of regional, national and local decision makers and institutions are strengthened to accelerate implementation of transboundary IWRM, which is aligned to CCA and CCM measures as planned in member states’ NDCs and NAPs. The improved decision-making processes are informed and supported by successful transboundary and climate-smart IWRM solutions from 3 selected sub-basins where mitigation opportunities are identified and the resilience of the local populations is strengthened by climate-smart agriculture, protecting biodiversity, improving the management of floods and droughts. This is accompanied by evidence-based planning tools, positive behaviour change communication and upscaling mechanisms.
Project results: The project aims to improve implementation of transboundary Integrated Water Resource Management (IWRM) measures for climate change adaptation (CCA) and climate change mitigation (CCM) in the Niger basin. This will drive long-term positive impacts for the resilience of livelihoods and ecosystems. Capacities of regional, national and local decision makers and institutions are strengthened to accelerate implementation of transboundary IWRM, aligned to CCA and CCM measures as planned in member states’ Nationally Determined Contributions and National Adaptation Plans. The improved decision-making processes are informed and supported by successful transboundary and climate-smart IWRM solutions from three selected sub-basins where mitigation opportunities are identified and the resilience of the local populations is strengthened by climate-smart agriculture, protecting biodiversity, improving the management of floods and droughts. This is accompanied by evidence-based planning tools, positive behavior change communication and upscaling mechanisms.

Name: R-A-2023-56
Dscription: Fertilize Right Vietnam
Project Summary: This project will introduce the integrated interventions of technologies and practices, capacity development, and enabling policies in favor of innovations of precision crop establishment to reduce seed and fertilizer use, site-specific nutrient management, and low-carbon and organic alternatives to chemical fertilizers. These interventions would substantially contribute to the following goal and objectives of the USDA’s Fertilize Right project i) increased fertilizer-use efficiency, (ii) enhanced fertilizer effectiveness, and (iii) developed alternatives to traditional manufactured chemical fertilizers to improve soil health and soil fertility.

Name: N-300053-FTF CLIMATE GENDER NUTRI.
Dscription: FTF CLIMATE GENDER NUTRI.
Project Summary: To address these challenges, the Feed the Future Climate, Gender and Nutrition Integration Initiative will have the following four main components: 1. Support to USAID missions in selected Feed the Future countries to identify specific challenges and needs, and provide tailored information to ensure that climate-resilient agricultural planning promotes gender equity and nutrition objectives. 2. Research to create a framework and analyze the linkages among climate smart agriculture, gender, and nutrition at multiple scales and over different time horizons, including prioritization of policies, investments, and farming practices across these key dimensions. 3. Increasing the value and impact of open data in agriculture through data harmonization, student fellowships, and hackathons. 4. Advisory services to provide sound and timely information and advice to USAID staff in Washington DC or missions regarding climate, gender and nutrition integration issues.

Name: N-343001-GCAN INITIATIVE
Dscription: GCAN INITIATIVE
Project Summary: This project expands and deepens the work of the Gender, Climate Change and Nutrition Integration Initiative with a focus on capacity strengthening, advisory services, and strategic research. The overall vision of the project is to ensure that investments, policies, and actions on climate change fully integrate gender equality, food security and nutrition objectives. The activity has 3 main objectives: (1) enhanced capacity of country governments and partners to design, implement, and monitor the effectiveness of climate change programs that contribute to gender equality, women’s empowerment, and food and nutrition security, (2) provision of technical assistance and advisory services to the BMGF Agricultural Development Team and a selected set of implementing partners, and (3) strategic research to fill key evidence gaps on the intersection of climate change, gender equality/women’s empowerment, and food security/nutrition. Focal countries are India, Kenya, Senegal, Ethiopia and Nigeria.

Name: R-A-2019-188-GCF Thailand
Dscription: GCF Thailand
Project Summary: The Thai Rice project is the first GCF project to focus solely on transforming rice production systems with a focus on sustainable, low-emission and circular economy solutions. Globally, rice food systems account for GHG emissions equivalent to those of the aviation sector. This project will support Thai rice farmers and service providers in the adoption of a series of climate-smart technologies and practices and deliver farmer-friendly financial products to sustainably manage rice farming. The intervention is projected to significantly decrease methane emissions and water-usage from rice farming in Thailand, while also boosting farmers’ overall climate resilience. The project will also allow for Thai rice-farmers to recycle their rice straw into new products and create new local market opportunities.

Name: W-D-0526-GEF: G4DR in Africa
Dscription: Groundwater for Deep Resilience in Africa
Project Summary: The Groundwater for aDvancing Resilience (G4DR) in Africa project aims to bring groundwater and its sustainable development and protection to the forefront of water security planning and investment in Africa, enhancing resilience for humans and ecosystems.
Project results:
The Groundwater for aDvancing Resilience (G4DR) in Africa project aims to bring groundwater and its sustainable development and protection to the forefront of water security planning and investment in Africa, enhancing resilience for humans and ecosystems. It will do so through the following components:
•	Strategic Planning: Supporting the African Ministers’ Council on Water (AMCOW), through their Pan-African Groundwater Program (APAGroP). 
•	Evidence and Capacity for G4DR in Africa: Identifying aquifers that present risk and opportunity to enhance resilience, as well as populations/socio-economic contexts in Africa informing investments 
•	Demonstrating benefit: Utilizing evidence-based planning to realize on-the-ground impacts in pilots
•	Incorporating G4DR into pan-African Youth Forums: enhancing the beyond-project capacity, outreach, networking, and uptake of long-term workable and sustainable strategies and solutions.
•	Supporting Knowledge Management and M&E: Supporting capture, exchange and dissemination of key project advancements, as well as evaluation of project progress relative to targets.
Countries: Benin, Togo, Malawi, Mozambique, South Africa, Uganda

Name: UNITED KINGDOM - FCDO -iSPARK: Innovation in sustainability, policy, adaptation and resilience in Kenya (IA Lead of D511)
Dscription: In Kenya, environmental degradation and climate change threaten the sustainability and resilience of smallholder farming systems. Farmers need timely and accurate climate information combined with agro-advisories to make informed decisions. Meanwhile, policy makers need evidence to prioritize policy choices and investment decisions that contribute to environmental sustainability, climate resilience, and other development goals by mid-century. iSPARK is designed to tailor evidence towards supporting appropriate and contextualised advisory services, policies, and investment in equity-sensitive sustainable and climate-smart innovation in western Kenya, building on CGIAR Initiatives on Climate Resilience and Excellence in Agronomy. The core of iSPARK is developing and using a metric-based approach called Innovation in Sustainability, Resilience, and Adaptation (ISRA) to provide specific evidence for innovations and pathways at different levels, from individual farms to regional policies. iSPARK aims to integrate ISRA metrics into agro-advisories reaching 100,000 farmers; create/support at least one plan/policy; and create/support at least one investment case or funding proposal by 2026.

Name: W-P-1641-IKI Indo-German Cooperation on Agrivoltaics (IGCA)
Dscription: Indo-German Cooperation on Agrivoltaics (IGCA)
Project Summary: The objective of this project is to contribute to an accelerated just energy transition through increased uptake of APV, generating benefits for the rural population without compromising food security (Outcome). It is expected that the project will financially and technically support the installation of 6 APV systems with a total capacity of around 10 MWp in 3 different states (direct effect). In addition, it will contribute to the further development of the APV market segment incl. additional 6 APV plants within the project implementation period alone (indirect effect). This will reduce the carbon intensity of the Indian power sector, improve air quality, and strengthen the social acceptance of the energy transition, especially among rural populations (transformational character and impact).
Project results: The Indo-German Cooperation on Agrivoltaics (IGCA) will follow a farmer centric approach to develop models that not only deliver solar generation capacity but enhance rural livelihoods and village economies.  The proposal development will  be mainly through planning and co-organizing a series of consultations and events aimed at defining the contours of the detailed proposal and identifying key partners who can contribute to this intended initiative.

Name: R-A-2024-19-ISARC Phase III
Dscription: ISARC Phase II
Project Summary: The ISARC program will increase farmers’ incomes, improve food and nutrition security, health, and well-being of smallholder farmers through increased system productivity, reduced yield gaps, enhanced climate resilience, mechanized and digital farming, improved market linkages, modernized value chains enabling entrepreneurship for women and youth, and capacity development.

Name: W-P-XXX1-Jahez
Dscription: Jahez: Improving Anticipatory Action and Climate Change Adaptation in Refugee-Host Systems in JordanAL MURANA
Project Summary: This project has the objective of building climate resilience in refugee-hosting communities and along the humanitarian-development spectrum, through scalable interventions that integrate localized impact-based weather and climate information systems with community-based disaster risk reduction (DRR) and nature-based solutions (NbS), leveraging climate finance and other funding mechanisms. The cornerstone of Jahez will be a set of innovative, practical pilot projects in refugee-hosting communities in Jordan that prove the concept, and demonstrate the effectiveness to build resilience through holistic integration of community-based DRR and NbS.
Project results: The “Jahez (Ready)” project – has the objective of improving anticipatory action and climate change adaptation to build the resilience of refugees and their host communities in Jordan. It focuses on supporting effective localized use of impact-based weather and climate change forecasts to inform implementation of inclusive and equitable community-based Disaster Risk Reduction and Nature Based Solutions that address unique vulnerabilities of refugee–host communities.

Name: R-A-2024-25-Madhya Pradesh_Rice Inbreds and Hybrids
Dscription: Madhya Pradesh_Rice Inbreds and Hybrids
Project Summary: The project aims to (1) develop an annual plan and strategy for sustainable and low carbon rice transformation in target countries; (2) develop training materials on scale-appropriate sustainable and low carbon rice (including hands-on manual, e-learning, and videos); 3) enhance knowledge and adoption of sustainable and low carbon rice through training, field demonstrations, and technology transfers.

Name: MILLETS in upland regions of Odisha for crop diversification, climate resilience and enhanced Food and Nutritional Security
Dscription: MILLETS in upland regions of Odisha for crop diversification, climate resilience and enhanced Food and Nutritional Security
Project Summary: The project “MILLETS in upland regions of Odisha for crop diversification, climate resilience and enhanced Food and Nutritional Security” is analysing the temporal rice cropping pattern in various ecosystems of Odisha state to identify the drought stress-prone areas in the Uplands which have high frequency of water shortage over years. Promising available varieties, hybrids and improved breeding lines of Millets are being evaluated in the targeted identified ecology to identify promising cultivars of Millets for the state, along with the identification of the cropping systems in which these millet crops can be established in the Uplands. The breeding pipeline for these Millet crops are being developed and strengthened in the Odisha state for long-term sustainability of these crops, this will be also coupled with development of standard management practices to have high productivity of Millets in the state, followed by strengthening of the seed systems of Millets in the Odisha state.
Center: International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics

Name: GAC-SCALING UP CLIMATE INFORMATION SERVICES AND CLIMATE- SMART AGRICULTURE FOR WOMEN FARMERS IN NICARAGUA’S  DRY CORRIDOR
Dscription:
Nicaragua is the fourth poorest country1 in Latin America and the Caribbean (LAC) after Haiti, Honduras and Guatemala, and ranks 21st out of 181 countries with the highest disaster risk.2 The development of Nicaragua, which relies heavily on agriculture, is disproportionally impacted by its vulnerability to climatic events: 25% of the population lives in areas at risk of hurricanes and tropical storms and 40% is affected by recurring drought, with communities in the Dry Corridor experiencing the greatest impacts of climate change. According to climate models, Nicaragua could become as warm as the Sahara by 2070, with average annual temperatures over 29°C, only found in 0.8% of the
globe today. The country will suffer more intense droughts and variable rainfall patterns. This threatens food and water security, along with biodiversity, and is expected to worsen the existing poverty and climate vulnerability of millions of households in Nicaragua, and further exacerbate irregular migration.Accessible, credible, and relevant climate and weather information services (CWIS)3 can vastly improve decision-making in disaster management, agriculture, biodiversity conservation and water
resources management. However, the access and use of CWIS is still limited in Nicaragua. The poorest and most vulnerable, especially in remote rural communities, are not benefitting from the scientific and technological advancements that could help manage climate risks and adapt their farming practices. Women farmers (including those from indigenous and ethnic minority groups) face the most barriers in accessing CWIS, as gender norms and patriarchal values in Nicaragua reinforce inequalities in access to and management of productive resources. Through the Project, the Organization will scale-up climate information and weather services and promote the adoption of climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices for at least 1,000 women from rural communities in the Dry Corridor of Nicaragua. Activities are designed to support the following:  
• Rural women smallholders and their organizations active in Nicaragua’s Dry Corridor actively access and use CWIS tailored to their specific information needs to manage climate and weather risk more effectively.
• Women smallholders empowered to adopt CSA technologies (climate smart crop varieties, best-fit crop management practices, digital tools, etc.) at a greater rate. 
• Service providers (including farmers’ organizations, National Agricultural Research and Extension Systems, NGOs, etc.) improve the quality and gender-responsiveness of the services and information they provide to women farmers and value chain actors.
• A network of at least 15 female community leaders become powerful advocates for women and girls in the design of gender responsive CWIS, the adaptation of CSA innovation bundles, and in climate governance and decision-making.
Men and women in farming households participate in community dialogues and household methodologies to reflect on and question harmful gender norms and attitudes and to foster more equitable gender relations and workloads.
• Women and girls in the Dry Corridor use access to small grants (incentives) and training and technical assistance (TTA) products to scale up adoption of CSA and increase income from farm business ideas centering around climate adaptation, biodiversity conservation, nature-positive solutions, and circular economy principles.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: T-PJ-003517: Chicken, Fish and Pigs Feed and Organic Fertilizer Value Chain Development Using BSF-Based Urban Biowaste Processing in Ghana, Mali, Niger and the Democratic Republic of Congo
Dscription: PJ-003517: Chicken, Fish and Pigs Feed and Organic Fertilizer Value Chain Development Using BSF-Based Urban Biowaste Processing in Ghana, Mali, Niger and the Democratic Republic of Congo
Project results: Develop a biowaste management business model by analyzing current and future streams. Establish value chains for chicken and fish feed and organic fertilizer. Optimize Black Soldier Fly (BSF) biowaste processing and build capacity for youth agripreneurs and stakeholders.

Name: R-A-2022-80-REMET
Dscription: Reducing methane emissions from rice: from mechanistic understanding to scalable crop management options (REMET)
Project Summary: The project will link scientific upstream research on methanogenesis to the development of practical and scalable crop management recommendations to be used by farmers in the field. Innovative practices will be geared toward maximizing multiple benefits by aligning low methane emissions with carbon sequestration as well as with improved agronomic performance. In terms of cropping systems, the project will focus on wetland rice in rice-rice and rice-maize rotations (as general proxy for rice-upland crop rotations) typical for many rice-growing environments of Asia (especially the major delta areas). New mechanistic and empirical findings will be encapsulated in deterministic and process-based simulation modelling to allow for extrapolation and prediction under well-defined environmental conditions (current and future climates).

Name: N-601158-SPIR II
Dscription: SPIR II
Project Summary: The learning agenda is designed to support IFPRI and its learning partners to collaborate with World Vision and the implementation partners to develop and deliver research and learning that will inform Strengthen PSNP 4 Institutions and Resilience (SPIR) II implementation in line with USAID’s Collaboration, Learning and Adaptation (CLA) approach and will provide lessons for a broad set of stakeholders through learning events.

Name: Technologies for African Agricultural Transformation Phase II (TAAT II)
Dscription: Technologies for African Agricultural Transformation Phase II (TAAT II)
Project Summary:
The development objective remains to "increase agricultural productivity and household incomes by expanding access to climate-resilient and productivity-increasing technologies in low-income RMCs across Africa by 2025". Specifically, TAAT II is to “enhance agricultural productivity and policy reform to support the selected RMCs to implement the AEFPF”.
TAAT II Compact partners and deliverables
Compact: Sorghum/Millet - ICRISAT, Country-based partners (e.g., NAREs, CBOs, NGOs, and the private sector)
Key objective: To support production, certification and distribution of improved seed varieties. To improve food security & livelihoods of farming families in the Sahel region through sustainable intensification and better profitability of the two major staple crops of the region; Improve the food and nutrition security of smallholder families, especially of women and children; Increase production and productivity of sorghum/millet through sustainable use of improved varieties and GAPs; Increase smallholder farmers income, and create innovative jobs for youth in the rural sector; Increase value chain efficiency through the reduction of postharvest losses, increase produce quality, aggregation, traceability, large-scale processing and transformation in sorghum and millet value chains; Enhance crop-livestock integration through the increased production and efficient utilization of sorghum and millet stover
Center: International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics
