Name: BMGF-Adaptation Atlas: Refinement and Transition
Dscription:
The Adaptation Atlas is designed to contribute directly to two primary outcomes within the existing AgDev Results Framework:
Increase quality, availability, and utility of data and evidence
Reduce impact of agricultural volatility for small-scale producers (SSPs)
 
Improved assessment of the patterns of climate-induced shocks and stresses can support the development and targeting of adaptation interventions that reduce negative impacts on the assets and well-being of vulnerable SSP households.
The Atlas will be an open, cloud-based, global public good. It will be hosted, maintained, and deployed in service of One CGIAR's reshaped and strongly climate-focused global R&D agenda in addition to separate instances and/or co-branding with a broader set of regional or national efforts, e.g., AGRA or the Global Commission on Adaptation. The Atlas will be a valuable tool in support of the planning and implementation of AgDev's climate adaptation investments and will contribute to our IAT goals by empowering in-country decision makers with accessible and locally targeted climate risk and solution data. The Adaptation Atlas’ (“the Atlas”) primary outcomes will be greater availability, awareness, and use of robust data and evidence to support climate change adaptation investments, policy making, and other programmatic decisions. This will be the consequence of our intensive co-design and development process, communications efforts, and transitioning the refined platform into One CGIAR (Fig. 1). Activities will aim to enhance user participation during Atlas creation; hyper-focus on the Atlas’ useability, utility, and direct responsiveness to needs, amplify its robustness and quality, and ensure sustained access for diverse users. The Atlas Platform itself is and will remain the main output of this effort. Its construction and dissemination will also produce ancillary outputs including harmonized datasets; software and code; modeling results; evaluations of user experience; and science, communications, and outreach materials such as journal articles, training videos, briefs, and feasibility reports. Intangible outputs, among them a hack-a-thon, small grants program, and outreach initiatives, will cultivate an informal user community engaged in improving the Atlas’ data and usage.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: Climate-smart initiatives for climate change adaptation and sustainability in prioritized agricultural production systems in Colombia (CSICAP)
Dscription: The Climate-Smart Initiatives for Climate Change Adaptation and Sustainability in Prioritized Agricultural Production Systems in Colombia (CSICAP) project aims to safeguard livelihoods, boost productivity, and enhance profitability in nine of Colombia's key agri-food value chains, while increasing resilience to climate change impacts and reducing greenhouse gas emissions in the agriculture sector. With a USD $100 million budget, funded by the Green Climate Fund (GCF), Development Bank of Latin America (CAF), the government of Colombia, and partner contributions, CSICAP aims to benefit 200,000 people directly and nearly one million indirectly. The project will help reduce over 2 million tons of CO2 equivalent emissions through digital agroclimatic services, climate-resilient crops, water-efficient climate-smart practices, inclusive capacity-building, and innovative business models. It will be implemented in 22 out of 32 departments in Colombia.

Name: CANADA-MEDA-The Adaptation and Valorization of Entrepreneurship in Irrigated Agriculture _ AVENIR
Project Summary: The objective of this project is to improve the socio-economic well-being and resilience of farming households by promoting climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices and agricultural entrepreneurship in the Tambacounda and Sédhiou regions in Senegal. AVENIR is funded by GAC (Global Affairs Canada) and implemented by MEDA (Mennonite Economic Development Associates) in collaboration with The Alliance.

Name: W0500-Verify global potential of biologic. Nitrific. inhib
Dscription: Verify global potential of biologic. Nitrific. inhibition
Project Summary: The purpose of the Project is to accelerate breeding and deployment of novel wheat lines with greatly improved nitrogen-use efficiency due to root exudation of nitrification-inhibiting secondary metabolites.
Center: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center / Centro Internacional de Mejoramiento de Maíz y Trigo

Name: W-D-0532-Water Tracker - Phase 2
Dscription: Water Resilience Tracker for National Climate Planning
Project Summary: As a component of the FCDO funded Just Transitions for Water Security  (JTWS), the Water Resilience Tracker for National Climate Planning will provide policy and technical assistance to increase political leadership, cooperation, and capacities across ministries and geographies to facilitate the design and implementation of inclusive, water resilient development plans, policies, programmes, and projects.  The knowledge generated through the implementation of the Water Resilience Tracker will be used to influence and guide decision making around water and climate. The integration of the Water Resilience Tracker within user sector agencies will encourage “silo-busting” and greater interactions amongst these sector agencies to identify common ground and meeting points around water, leading to more equitable and sustainable use and management of water as a resource, and enhance pathways for long-term water security.
Project results: The Water Resilience Tracker for Climate Planning (WRT) aims to mainstream climate resilient water management in planning and strategies across sectors. It offers both policy and technical support to governments, empowering them to plan, design, and implement inclusive climate-resilient policies that recognize the critical role of water in coping with climate change. By evaluating existing institutional structures and policies, the Tracker identifies gaps and subsequently, provides strategic as well as technical support at different levels to foster cooperation, strengthen leadership, and unlock access to climate finance

Name: W-D-0424-WRAP C1
Dscription: Water Resource Accountability in Pakistan (WRAP) Component 1
Project Summary: The long-term goal of Component 1 is to strengthen capacity to manage water resources at Federal, Provincial and District levels. Potential institutions that will be supported under the project include the Provincial Planning and Development Department, water resources commissions, water regulatory authorities, irrigation, and on-farm water management departments. This Component will also improve the enabling environment for water sector reforms through better accountability, monitoring and narrative setting. Women, youth, and marginalized people will be specifically targeted with their engagement.
Project results: IWMI is implementing Component 1 of the Water Resource Accountability in Pakistan (WRAP) project. The long-term goal of this Component is to strengthen capacity to manage water resources at federal, provincial and district levels. Potential institutions that will be supported under the project include the Provincial Planning and Development Department, water resources commissions, water regulatory authorities, irrigation, and on-farm water management departments. This Component will also improve the enabling environment for water sector reforms through better accountability, monitoring and narrative setting. Women, youth, and marginalized people will be specifically targeted with their engagement, inputs and roles.

Name: A-AG10181-BMGF MultiHarvestRice
Dscription: BMGF MultiHarvestRice
Project Summary: The MultiHarvestRice project, led by AfricaRice, is a transformative initiative aimed at enhancing rice production across Africa through the introduction of high-yielding and climate-resilient rice varieties. The project addresses the critical need for increased rice productivity and sustainability in the face of growing environmental challenges and food security concerns. A central component of the project is the development and promotion of advanced rice varieties that are not only high-yielding but also resilient to climate-related stresses such as droughts, floods, and heat. These new varieties are designed to thrive in diverse environmental conditions, thereby reducing the risks associated with climate variability and improving overall crop stability. By providing farmers with access to these improved varieties, the project helps to boost rice yields and ensure more reliable production.
Project results: A-AG10181-BMGF MultiHarvestRice

Name: R-A-2022-118-Credits Offsetting Rice Emissions
Dscription: Credits Offsetting
Project Summary: The project´s core objective is to facilitate enabling conditions for changing smallholder farmers and value chain actors´ access to the carbon market. This will be achieved by improving the evidence-base for combined emission reduction practices, developing a new methodology for generating carbon credits from shifting current crop management, analyzing emissions through post-harvest losses, guiding policy and developing support tools for partner organizations’ extension teams and farmers based on needs.

Name: D-200337-WEFE4MED - Towards a Mediterranean WEFE Nexus
Dscription: WEFE4MED - Towards a Mediterranean WEFE Nexus Community of Practice
Project Summary: WEFE4MED will gather scientists, practitioners, policymakers, investors, local authorities, the media and other stakeholders in a Mediterranean WEFE Nexus Community of Practice (NCoP) to provide convincing practical and scientific evidence of the Nexus benefits as a basis for decision-making in integrated natural resources management. WEFE4MED will achieve this by identifying, federating and promoting examples of best practice around the Mediterranean, including demonstrator projects, having Nexus at their core. It will foster long-lasting cooperation between actors as well as demonstrator projects to replicate and upscale them in other countries, and to disseminate their achievements, including as part of a science-based advocacy effort aimed at policy makers and investors. In doing so, WEFE4MED will establish an open digital Nexus Knowledge Hub, twins flagship demos and promote mutual learning through participatory approaches among knowledge holders while promoting external funding opportunities.
Center: International Center for Agricultural Research in the Dry Areas

Name: R-A-2023-24-ASEAN-CGIAR Innovate for Food Regional Program
Dscription: ASEAN-CGIAR Innovate for Food Regional Program
Project Summary: The ASEAN-CGIAR Innovate for Food regional program (IFRP) aims to scale up and out bold integrated innovations that will make ASEAN agri-food systems more resilient in the face of climate change while delivering better livelihoods for food producers and other stakeholders along the associated value chains, as well as more affordable, nutritious and healthy food for consumers and a better natural environment for all in the next ten years.

Name: PJ-003897: Soil Values
Dscription: IFDC/IITA - SOIL VALUES PROJECTS (RAFS) - PJ-3897
Project Summary: The Netherlands Directorate-General for International Cooperation (DGIS) Soil Fertility Grant Program (SFGP) aims to enhance soil fertility and food productivity in the Sahel, focusing on small-scale food producers. This Soil Values initiative, part of this program, seeks to integrate soil fertility management with market development and policy support, fostering widespread adoption of sustainable practices. Key outputs include improved soil health, scalable investment mechanisms and strengthened stakeholder collaboration. Over the next decade, Soil Values aims to enhance soil fertility across 2 million hectares and support 1.5 million small-scale producers in Burkina Faso, Mali, Niger and Northern Nigeria, with regional integration efforts extending to Ghana and Côte d’Ivoire.
Project results: Enhance seed system functionality to meet smallholder farmers' needs, increasing productivity, market access, and the availability of nutrient-dense foods.

Name: A-AG10185-A-AG10185-Building resilient seed systems for rice
Dscription: A-AG10185-Building resilient seed systems for rice, cassava, cocoa, coffee, and fish value chains to strengthen food and economic diversilication in Liberia (Seeds4liberia)
Project Summary: The Seeds4Liberia project aims to enhance food and nutrition security in Liberia by developing a demand-driven, well-structured, and regulated seed system. The focus is on four critical agricultural commodities (rice, cassava, cocoa, and coffee) collectively referred to as the "target crops," as well as fish farming. The primary objectives of Seeds4Liberia include strengthening seed systems by creating reliable networks for seed production and distribution that are responsive to the specific needs of Liberian farmers, ensuring they have access to high-quality seeds that are resilient and high yielding. By providing improved seeds and promoting best agricultural practices, the project seeks to significantly boost the productivity of these target crops, leading to increased food production and better livelihoods for smallholder farmers. The project also aims to enhance food and nutrition security by increasing the availability of diverse and nutritious foods through both crop and fish farming.
Project results: A-AG10185-A-AG10185-Building resilient seed systems for rice

Name: A-AG10156 AICCRA PROJECT
Dscription: A-AICCRA
Project Summary: The AICCRA (Accelerating Impacts of CGIAR Climate Research for Africa) project, implemented by AfricaRice, is designed to enhance climate resilience in African agriculture. This initiative focuses on integrating climate change adaptation strategies into agricultural practices to address the impacts of a changing climate on food systems. The AICCRA project employs a multi-dimensional approach to build resilience among smallholder farmers and agricultural communities. It emphasizes the development and promotion of climate-smart agricultural practices, including the adoption of drought-resistant crop varieties, improved soil management techniques, and efficient water use strategies. These measures aim to improve productivity while mitigating the negative effects of climate variability. The project also focuses on strengthening agricultural research and extension services to provide farmers with timely and relevant information about climate adaptation. By fostering partnerships with local research institutions, government agencies, and farmer organizations, AICCRA ensures that adaptation strategies are tailored to the specific needs and conditions of different regions.
Project results: A-AG10156 AICCRA PROJECT

Name: THE FOOD PLANET PRIZE & THE CURT BERGFORS FOUNDATION Agrobiodiversity Index
Dscription: Both description and project summary filled. The Alliance Agrobiodiversity Index has won the 2023 Food Planet Prize. The Agrobiodiversity Index has a vision of using science and empirical evidence to quantify and measure the sustainability of the food system, and translate this into a quantitative index for farmers, businesses, and policy, in order to accelerate the adoption of sustainable and healthy food systems. Itprovides the means to measure the gap between the agricultural biodiversity we have in our food system, and the thousands of plant and food species and varieties we could have, to help maintain healthy diets and resilient production systems now and in the future.
Project Summary: The Agrobiodiversity Index is useful in a context where Governments and development partners, including multilateral agencies and development banks, needs to design and monitor policies and interventions and measure progress towards global targets. Also, where Companies needs help to reduce risks in the supply chain, enhance environmental stewardship and improve the sustainability of their production, responding to consumers and investors needs. It also supports Global conventions and treaties to monitor how well countries are doing with their commitments towards agrobiodiversity and Investors to rate the policies and performance of food and agriculture companies, and make appropriate decisions. Agrobiodiversity is measured through 3 pillars : 1) Status – the current state of agrobiodiversity in markets and consumption, in agricultural production, and in genetic resource management; 2) Actions – what countries, companies, or projects are concretely doing to increase agrobiodiversity across the food system and 3)Commitments – to what extent country, company or project strategies, policies and codes of conduct are contributing to sustainable use and conservation of agrobiodiversity for healthy diets, sustainable agriculture and future use options.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: IFAD (EC) Agroecological Transitions for Building Resilient, Inclusive, Agricultural and Food Systems (TRANSITIONS): Private sector incentives and investments
Dscription: Both description and project summary filled. Transforming global food production systems, while also reducing their impact on the natural environment and improving welfare, remains a complex agenda characterized by overlapping trade-offs. Transitioning from “business as usual” requires simultaneous transformation across multiple levels – from socioecological systems, economics, business, and politico-institutional systems – to ensure the adoption of a more regenerative use of natural resources. However, too little attention is paid to the role of the private and public sectors in advancing to agroecological transition pathways. Although many business and private sector stakeholders are aligning to agroecological principles in food systems, there remains concerns among actors on the motivation, incentives, investments, and transparency of private sector initiatives. Under the TRANSITIONS program, the Private Sector Incentives and Investments (PSii) project will fill persistent knowledge gaps on the role of private and public sector incentives and investments in supporting agroecological transitions for farmers in low- and middle-income countries. The project will: (1) Review exisitng incentives mechanisms for supporting the promotion of agroecological practices, design incentive packages for scaling adoption of agroecological practices within agroecology smart landscapes for transitioning rice, wheat and cococa production systems, (2) Apply holistic metrics to digital traceability tools, and implement within at least one supply chain, (3) Improve enabling environment business models for supporting agroecological produced products, (4) Co-design and co-create incentive packages and building local capacity
Project Summary: The PSii project focuses on developing innovative private and public sector incentive pathways for scaling agroecological practices to improve landscapes. Developing a better understanding and application for long-term incentives and transparent private sector engagement to support agroecological transitions for farmers and creating an enabling environment for scaling innovations. Key outcomes include: (1) Supported at least 100,000 households to engage with improved private and public sector incentive models and investments to adopt agroecological practices. (2) Improved the intergration of agroecological principles in transparent supply chains for rice, cocoa, and wheat value chains for at least 50,000 households. (3) Encreased the integration of agroecological metrics in supply chain transparency and traceability tools. (4) Improved policy enabling environment and existing business models for supporting agroecological transition for rice, cocoa and wheat value chains, and (5) Ehanced building local capacity, engaging multi-stakeholder platforms and co-mobilizing private sector investments to support agroecological transitions.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: T-PJ-003801-Cassava Value Chain Development to Support Food Security and the Bread Industry in DRC
Dscription: Cassava Value Chain Development to Support Food Security and the Bread Industry in DRC
Project Summary: This cassava value chain project, aligned with USAID's Feed the Future program, aims to enhance the economic potential of the cassava sector in the DRC. This project focuses on improving livelihoods for small producers, women and youth through the adoption of post-harvest technologies and scaling up cassava processing to produce high-quality, locally processed foods that can substitute for imports like wheat flour. Key outcomes include the development of the cassava agro-industrial sector, innovation in cassava processing and capacity building for bread bakers. This project also emphasizes the importance of government policies and infrastructure to support the sustainable growth of the cassava sector.
Project results: Produce 30,000 tons of high-quality cassava products annually from 120,000 tons of smallholder-grown roots. Cultivate 12,000 ha with improved varieties, benefiting 12,600 farmers, including 600 seed growers. Establish 1,200 ha of seed farms, with INERA supplying 60 ha of basic seeds.
Countries: The Democratic Republic of the Congo

Name: N-301059-CLIMATE CHG x AGRICULTURE
Dscription: CLIMATE CHG x AGRICULTURE
Project Summary: Smallholder farmers face various constraints in accessing financial products, which allow them to make productive investments. The aim of this investment is to advance financial services for farmers affected by climate change in Bangladesh over a two-year investment (Jan 2023-Mar 2025). During the first phase, we will focus on identifying the major evidence gaps on access to finance through a combination of secondary data analyses, desk research to consolidate/review existing evidence, and expert/stakeholder consultations to highlight the key challenges, their determinants, and to identify solutions to address them. Building on lessons learned from the first phase, the second phase will use this evidence to design and pilot evidence-based interventions to improve access to financial products among low-income smallholder farmers in Bangladesh, particularly those disproportionately affected by climate change. A gender lens will be applied throughout the investment to consider the unique access issues facing female smallholder farmers. Overall, this investment aims to support diverse stakeholders in using evidence for policy and practice decisions that have potential to enhance resilience and adaptation strategies of smallholder farmers in Bangladesh.

Name: PREPARATION PHASE - "Supporting Colombia’s Just Energy Transition, climate resilience, and food security through the introduction of inclusive agri-photovoltaic systems" (JET-AgriSOL)
Dscription: Description and project summary both filled. To support the Colombian Government in achieving its sustainable and Just Energy Transition goal, and meeting its agricultural and energy sectoral NDC targets through the nationwide expansion of photovoltaic capacity (impact), farmer communities have to co-develop and adopt inclusive and economically viable agri-photovoltaic (APV) solutions that can be further scaled through SDG-compliant public and private investments (outcome). This development will be initiated during the project’s first piloting phase, consisting of an APV pilot that will be installed on CIAT’s campus to carry out “higher-risk” research activities (output III). During the second piloting phase, lower-risk research activities will be conducted under real conditions on medium-scale APV pilots, on horticultural, arable, and pastoral land (output I). The diverse APV pilots form the basis for knowledge co-creation and serve for demonstration purposes, thereby promoting APV innovation-uptake and -scaling. Furthermore, women and men members of rural communities will be trained through a new praxis-oriented, and gender-responsive APV curriculum that will be developed and implemented with SENA’s (Colombia’s National Training Service) in-situ Rural Entrepreneurship (SER) program, focussing especially on developing capacities of disadvantaged groups (output II). This holistic skills development empowers rural community members to become more climate resilient and take advantage of new income opportunities. Finally, during the project’s third piloting phase, lessons learned from the experimental and medium-scale pilots will be integrated into the implementation of community-scale APV pilots, e.g., on the land of cacao producer organisations in PDET municipalities. Community-scale APV pilot will expand the green energy capacity of producer organisations and their communities, while promoting forest- and landscape-restoration activities (output I). The community-scale pilots play a key role in scaling APV systems and generating positive environmental impacts, while contributing to the energy-sovereignty of vulnerable rural communities. Moreover, inclusive and accessible APV-tailored financial mechanisms will be co-designed with local and international financial institutions (IFIs), further attracting investments to the APV sector, and enabling and encouraging small-scale farmers to adopt APV solutions (output IV). To further facilitate the inclusive adoption and scaling of APV systems, key binding constraints in the enabling environment will be identified and addressed through a multistakeholder process (output V).
Project Summary: The proposed project will support the Colombian Government in achieving its Just Energy Transition objective and NDC targets. By introducing and scaling APV systems, the project will contribute to promoting the energy-sovereignty and climate resilience of small-scale farmers, reducing GHG emissions, empowering women and youth to improve their livelihoods, and enhancing biodiversity. The project will set up micro- and medium-scale pilot and demonstration APV systems. Capacities for managing APV systems and combining them with climate smart agriculture will be strengthened by co-creating a vocational APV training program with Colombia´s National Training Service. Inclusive, participatory and interdisciplinary research will be conducted on the pilot APV systems, and findings will be shared with a broad range of stakeholders. Further, context-specific and inclusive APV finance mechanisms will be co-designed, and public and private stakeholders supported to upgrade the enabling environment.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: N-632001-EFF CASH TXFR ON COFFE PR
Dscription: EFF CASH TXFR ON COFFE PR
Project Summary: The objective of this project is to provide a short overview of proposed research designs for an evaluation analyzing the effect of cash transfers delivered by GiveDirectly to coffee farmers in Sidama region, Ethiopia. Coffee plays a central role in the Ethiopian economy; it is the most important export crop, and is grown by more than four million smallholders. Evidence suggests that coffee production enhances food security for households that grow it. However, coffee yields in Ethiopia remain relatively low (around 30% below the worldwide mean), and these relatively low yields may reflect in part poor farm management and agronomic practices. The key research questions for this project include: 1) What are the effects of cash transfers on a range of economic outcomes for coffee producing households, including: investment in coffee production and coffee yield and profits; investment in other economic activities; assets, consumption, and poverty? 2) How do these effects vary with respect to the timing of the cash transfer (in the lean season vis-à-vis in the key season for coffee investments and/or vis-à-vis a chosen disbursement schedule)? 3) Are the effects of transfers of various timings also heterogeneous for households that have different time preferences at baseline, such that households that are more impatient show more positive effects of a transfer timed to key investment opportunities?

Name: N-303008-GUATEMALA FOOD SECURITY
Dscription: GUATEMALA FOOD SECURITY
Project Summary: The national Strategy for Chronic Malnutrition Prevention 2016-2019 requires technical assistance in various aspects such as in designing and implementing the new strategy as well as in developing a monitoring system and impact evaluation strategy. This collaboration seeks to: 1. Provide technical support in designing and implementing the new strategy. Name: N-642001-HIGH-RESOLUTION CROP STAT
Dscription: HIGH-RESOLUTION CROP STAT
Project Summary: Growing competition for finite land resources is resulting in a global land squeeze in which agriculture is increasingly at odds with nature. The World Resources Institute has embarked on an integrated approach, “Produce-Protect-Reduce-Restore”, to address this grand challenge. To achieve the objectives of this strategy requires detailed annual mapping of crop-specific harvested areas, productivity and crop use information. We will build annual global high-resolution crop and crop usage data for the last twenty years (2001-2020) to support WRI’s four-part strategy to address global land squeeze. We will collect subnational crop statistics for the top global crops from countries around the world. We will digitize and catalog them into a standard format. We will then tabulate and geo-reference the data so it could map seamlessly globally across scales. In addition, we will compute sector-specific crop usage data using consistent methods. Our annual high-resolution and crop-specific maps spanning the years 2001 to 2020 will then be used as input in the analysis of crop and agricultural sector production, and for land use change emissions calculations. We will lead and also contribute to publications and reports led by WRI researchers using these newly built spatially explicit datasets. The University of Minnesota’s Ray’s crop dataset and the IFPRI’s SPAM (Spatial Production Allocation Model) dataset are the only two high-resolution global gridded crop specific maps that can be called observationally-based reflecting ground reality. They have been used in numerous scientific investigations and reports in the past. The combined Minnesota-IFPRI team will bring together the world’s best two spatial agriculture data development teams. WRI will be able to use this high-resolution annual gridded crop data to understand the global future of the “produce” part of the four-part strategy and land carbon accounting analysis; the broad scientific and policy community will gain access to this dataset revolutionizing the speed of global discoveries in agriculture at high-resolutions.

Name: N-633006-IICOF IN JIMMA ETH
Dscription: IICOF IN JIMMA ETH
Project Summary: The project includes a learning agenda intended to produce evidence on the effectiveness of bundled interventions as compared to narrowly designed projects that focus on addressing a specific adoption constraint. At the request of HereWeGrow for an evaluation partner organization to support its learning agenda, we present in this proposal an evaluation plan to rigorously measure the impact of the project. The proposed evaluation will mainly rely on a randomized controlled trial (RCT) to assess the causal effect of the interventions on the adoption of good agricultural practices, productivity, and income. The evaluation will also assess potential synergies between the interventions, provide insights into the pathways that help explain the realized impacts, and potential heterogeneity of impacts along economic and social dimensions. The RCT will be supplemented by a qualitative assessment both to have a deeper understanding of the impact pathways and mechanisms and gather evidence on project components that are not included in the experimental assessment.

Name: N-2023P013-IMPACT ASSESSMENT
Dscription: IMPACT ASSESSMENT
Project Summary: For this project, IFPRI will conduct two impact evaluations, one of the Char Development and Settlement Project IV in Bangladesh, and the other of the Smallholder Market-Led Project in eSwatini. The projects broadly have been meant to clarify land tenure for farmers in the former case and to link farmers to markets in the latter case; the project will identify control groups and then collect endline data for ex post impact evaluations in both cases that will help determine project impacts on both project level indicators and IFAD level indicators; the latter to be used for IFAD determinants of overall impact using meta-analysis.

Name: Landscape resource management for augmenting groundwater recharge and sustainable intensification of farming systems and building resilience for improved livelihoods of farmers in dryland of Maharashtra, India
Dscription: Landscape resource management for augmenting groundwater recharge and sustainable intensification of farming systems and building resilience for improved livelihoods of farmers in dryland of Maharashtra, India
Project Summary: The project aims to establish seven benchmark learning sites by utilizing a Land Resource Inventory (LRI) and a hydrology-based landscape management approach. This initiative will focus on groundwater augmentation, sustainable intensification of farming systems, and reducing water, energy, and carbon footprints. Additionally, the project will design profitable and water-efficient cropping systems that integrate climate-resilient, nutrient-dense crops, along with effective management practices and market linkages to enhance farmer income and resource use efficiency. Finally, the project will track key performance indicators using advanced instrumentation and digital tools to assess the techno-economic feasibility and develop strategies for scaling up.
Center: International Crops Research Institute for the Semi-Arid Tropics

Name: N-600273-MAPS TOOL
Dscription: MAPS TOOL
Project Summary: Micronutrient deficiency (MND) risks need to be understood better at various scales and time-horizons, to support evidence gathering for programmatic and policy interventions. This is especially required in nations which have not yet conducted detailed or recent micronutrient surveys (MNS) and rely on sparse secondary data. Even where MNS data do exist, there is a need to communicate how MND risks will change over time and how different interventions could deliver health benefits as food systems evolve and respond to environmental change. This project will enable the delivery of a co-designed, web-hosted tool, to provide the best possible estimates of MNDs at national, and sub-national scales in Africa. Through novel functionality, the tool will allow users to explore MNDs at various spatial and temporal scales, in both data-rich and data-poor contexts. The tool will also allow exploration of MND-associated health burdens, foresighting of food system changes, and estimation of cost-effectiveness of food system interventions, including dietary diversification efforts and (bio)fortification.

Name: A-HP2023-002-MasterCard - WAEMU
Dscription: Mastercard - RIZAO
Project Summary: The Mastercard-RIZAO project (West African Economic and Monetary Union), spearheaded by AfricaRice, aims to significantly improve agricultural productivity and economic development within the West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU) by addressing critical challenges in the rice sector. The initiative focuses on enhancing financial inclusion by leveraging MasterCard’s digital tools to provide rice farmers with access to credit, savings, and insurance, thereby enabling better investment and risk management. In parallel, AfricaRice will introduce and promote climate-smart and high-yielding rice varieties, alongside modern farming techniques in water management, pest control, and soil fertility, to boost productivity and resilience. The project also aims to strengthen market connections by improving the supply chain and reducing transaction costs, thereby increasing farmers' incomes and facilitating better access to local, regional, and international markets. Capacity building is a key component, with extensive training provided to farmers, extension workers, and other stakeholders to enhance rice quality and reduce post-harvest losses.
Project results: A-HP2023-002-MasterCard - WAEMU

Name: N-632006-PARES
Dscription: PARTNER SUST EDUC-PARES

IFPRI's research examines strategies to strengthen community involvement in the handover of school meals from World Vision to GoM.

Name: T-PJ-003866-Liberia Land and Soil Resources Knowledge project (Soils4Liberia)
Dscription: PJ-003866: Liberia Land and Soil Resources Knowledge project (Soils4Liberia)
Project Summary: This project aims to develop a Land & Soil Information System for Liberia to enhance land and soil management. Its main objectives are to create a functional system with reliable data, increase stakeholder awareness and support decision-making for resource conservation, sustainable agriculture and climate resilience. Key outputs include protocols, a comprehensive database, soil property maps, a land use map, Soil Quality Indicator (SQI) maps and land and crop suitability maps. These will aid government agencies, research institutions and NGOs by providing crucial data for policy development, agricultural planning and climate change mitigation. This project seeks to improve land and soil health and support sustainable development.
Project results: Develop standard protocols and tools for soil and land use inventory and field campaign management, including a database with primary soil and land use data, soil property maps, land use maps of Liberia, Soil Quality Indicator (SQI) maps, and land and crop suitability maps.

Name: T-PJ-003948: Fair Food and Trade Systems for Africa through Food Convergence Innovation
Dscription: PJ-003948: Fair Food and Trade Systems for Africa through Food Convergence Innovation

Name: N-641003-PNG OFFICE CAP STRENGTHEN
Dscription: PNG OFFICE CAP STRENGTHEN
Project Summary: After consultation with a variety of PNG government partners, including PM&NEC and DAL, there is an interest in forming an in-country IFPRI office in Port Moresby. IFPRI has a variety of country-offices across the globe, with the goal of building and strengthening data-driven policy environments through its Country Strategy Support Programs (CSSPs). These programs are designed in collaboration with policy makers and donors to identify key areas for greater analysis and to inform trade-offs and impact of different policies and investments. Several examples are IFPRI’s country strategy programs in Bangladesh and Ethiopia, which have bridged robust analysis with policy formulation and donor investment priorities. Given the expressed interest of the PNG government for an IFPRI Country Strategy Support Program to be housed in Port Moresby, this proposal outlines the activities needed to set up a medium-term country program office. In addition, it includes ongoing research needs with the objective of building partnerships and informing economic policy throughout 2023 and early 2024.
Funder: Canada-Departament of Foreign Affairs, Trade and Development

Name: T-PJ-003651-Program for Seed System Innovation for VPCs in Africa (PROSSIVA)
Dscription: Program for Seed System Innovation for VPCs in Africa (PROSSIVA)
Project Summary: The Africa Centre of Innovation for Vegetatively Propagated Crop (VPC) Seed Systems (ACI-VSS) aims to catalyze the development, validation and scaling of innovative packages to establish functional and profitable seed systems in target African countries. By 2030, it seeks to positively impact tens of millions of seed entrepreneurs, smallholder farmers and consumers, contributing to poverty reduction, improved livelihoods and new job opportunities, especially for women and youth. Operating under the OneCGIAR Initiative ‘SeEdQUAL: Delivering Genetic Gains in Farmers’ Fields’, ACI-VSS will develop and deploy technological, marketing and institutional innovations to address seed delivery bottlenecks for major VPCs. Its first phase (2022-2026) will focus on eight target countries, aiming to sustain operations through a diverse membership platform.
Project results: Functional Seed Systems for banana, cassava, potato, sweet potato and yam in the target countries of Sub-saharan Africa.

Name: N-600292-RCC IN AFRICA
Dscription: RCC IN AFRICA
Project Summary: This project aims to: 1) Improve the RCC product to minimize basis risk in product design by integrating other key environmental variables such as vegetation and soil moisture along with rainfall data based on state-of-the-art remote sensors, 2) develop and implement Social and Behavior Change Communication (SBCC) for improving diets in association with agricultural transformation triggered by RCC directly aimed at women farmers and their spouses; 3) deepen impact assessment by including impact of RCC on farm household nutrition; and 4) develop formal relations with an increasing number of RCC service providers and increase its efforts to building market and capacity development to scale-up RCC in kenya, Tanzania, Ethiopia and beyond to other developing countries that suffer from climate risk and climate change.

Name: N-313004-ReSAKSS SOUTH ASIA- IAT
Dscription: ReSAKSS SOUTH ASIA- IAT
Project Summary: This investment is part of the Asia ECS BoW 4, specifically on the regional sharing/learning of best practices and scaling promising solutions. It will support South Asia ReSAKSS in partnership with the Bay of Bengal Initiative for Multi Sectoral Technical and Economic Cooperation (BIMSTEC). Conceptually, the IFPRI-BIMSTEC partnership will be similar to ReSAKSS-Africa partnership with the Regional Economic communities such as Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA). Over the course of proposal development, we will outline specific activities and milestones that are critical to track progress of the network, including full buy-in of a regional institution, country representation from across South Asia, commitments to coordinate on emerging and shared challenges in agriculture, among others. The issues will naturally be identified based on individual country challenges/priorities and will then be prioritized to address those that are most relevant to the region. However, we expect that the ag policy issues that will emerge are on climate change, market connectivity and linkages, and research/R&D investments. At this point, it is too precarious to plan for explicit resource or policy commitments given the geopolitical dynamics in South Asia, but we may explore this at a later stage of the network's development. The key outcomes from this investment will include a) greater regional level engagement among policymakers, private firms, farmer organizations, and researchers; b) better understanding of common opportunities and challenges for inclusive rural transformation in the region; c) reduced barriers to transfer of technological and business innovations that can contribute to improving smallholders livelihood; d) a platform to facilitate Asia learning from Asia and e) generation of comparable indicators/evidence across countries on trade, food security, and climate change (or other areas identified by the partners and stakeholders under these broad themes). The study will be in India and Bangladesh.

Name: N333010
Dscription: Shea Butter Value Chain and Women
Project Summary: We introduce efficiency improving as well as value adding innovations to women’s cooperatives that engage in shea collection in disadvantaged areas of Ghana. The empowerment of women in the above context is expected to not only increase productivity and reduce loss in shea collection and thus women’s incomes but also create various welfare improving effects such as human capital investments in children and additional investments to generate household incomes. Specifically, the intervention introduces tricycles and dehusking/ drying equipment to all target cooperatives, and in some cooperatives, aggregation warehouses. In particular, the introduction of tricycles will be a breakthrough to dramatically increase productivity and reduce loss during shea collection and transport. Dehusking and drying equipment will increase value added. The interventions draw upon the expertise accumulated in a Japanese company, Fuji Oil Inc. and therefore will be implemented in the collaboration with the company.

Name: N-601158-SPIR II
Dscription: SPIR II
Project Summary: The learning agenda is designed to support IFPRI and its learning partners to collaborate with World Vision and the implementation partners to develop and deliver research and learning that will inform Strengthen PSNP 4 Institutions and Resilience (SPIR) II implementation in line with USAID’s Collaboration, Learning and Adaptation (CLA) approach and will provide lessons for a broad set of stakeholders through learning events.

Name: N-614039-Strengthening Nat'l Capability MCF
Dscription: Strengthening Nat'l Capability MCF
Project Summary: The overarching objective is to provide evidence to support the design of market-based, youth-sensitive interventions for linking to markets and reducing food loss, leveraging digital technologies for empowering young men and women to live dignified lives in selected countries in Africa. Sub-objective 1: To map and provide evidence on impacts of different market inclusion strategies and institutional arrangements for improving livelihoods of farmers, young men, and women in selected countries. Sub-objective 2: To improve how food loss in value chains is quantified, characterize the nature of food loss across value chains in eight countries, document innovations for post-harvest management and levels of adoption, and test effectiveness of these innovations in reducing losses. Sub-objective 3: To test the effectiveness of various models for increasing young women and men’s access to agricultural markets and fulfilling opportunities across value chains, and the tools (including digital tools) and the institutional arrangements that facilitate this. The evidence generated from these short- and medium-term sub-objectives will inform program implementation, feeding lessons learnt into the implementation process to course correct and increase the likelihood of impact. The results will also inform other organizations on how best to leverage interventions to improve the efficiency and inclusivity of markets across value chains. We will disseminate the evidence generated through various means including case studies, policy briefs, and papers. At the country level, we will present the results in workshops with implementing partners and other stakeholders working on inclusive markets, post-harvest management, youth livelihoods, and gender equality.

Name: N-302020-SUDAN STRATEGY SUPPORT PR
Dscription: The Republic of the Sudan STRATEGY SUPPORT PR
Project Summary: The CSSPs conduct research and capacity building activities that fall across numerous research themes and, as such, draw expertise from each IFPRI division to develop a common workplan. Different challenges and opportunities exist in different regions and countries, and IFPRI adapts its research approaches by context accordingly. IFPRI’s CSSPs achieve their objectives based on the following principles of engagement: a) Activities build on existing work and capacities in the country wherever possible by forming partnerships and networks. b) Activities are undertaken in close collaboration with national partners to improve the quality and relevance of the work and to help strengthen national capacity. c) IFPRI and its national partners work closely with policymakers at the national and regional levels in setting priorities and disseminating results from the program’s activities. d) Activities will be undertaken in The Republic of the Sudan to the greatest extent possible, and IFPRI would base its key team members in Khartoum for this purpose.

Name: N-302058-TAJIKISTAN EVAL AA-TEAA
Dscription: TAJIKISTAN EVAL AA-TEAA
Project Summary: This proposed activity will provide the agriculture sector research necessary to expand private sector opportunities, enhance household nutrition and resilience, and improve natural resource management. TEAA will contribute to USAID’s objective of sustainable and inclusive economic growth strengthened in the face of significant political, economic, and climate challenges. TEAA will pursue two objectives: 1) evaluate the status of the agri-food system and the socio-economic status of rural communities, 2) build the capacity of key stakeholders to research and prioritize strategic reforms that spur private sector investment in the agriculture sector and improve resource management in Tajikistan.

Name: W-D-0467-EU - Balochistan WR Programme
Dscription: Technical Assistance for the Revival of the Balochistan Water Resources Programme
Project Summary: The Revival of Balochistan Water Resources Programme is a European Union-funded initiative of USD 39.9 million. It is designed to contribute to the transition of agriculture and livestock farming systems in Balochistan achieving lower water intensity and improving environmental and economic sustainability. The Programme is implemented through two distinct but complementary implementing contracts working closely together under a project steering committee. The technical assistance component in Balochistan will contribute to: a) strengthening the governance of water resources and rangelands; b) developing alternative agricultural and livestock farming systems based on low water agro-economy; and c) increasing the availability of qualified agro-technology cadres. The consortium is led by Landell Mills. IWMI’s USD 5 million component includes the implementation of 4 field research studies. IWMI’s Project Leader is Dr Mohsin Hafeez.
Project results:
This programme is designed to contribute to the transition of agriculture and livestock farming systems in Balochistan achieving lower water intensity and improving environmental and economic sustainability. The program is implemented through two distinct but complementary implementing contracts working closely together under a project steering committee.

The technical assistance component in Balochistan will contribute to; strengthening the governance of water resources and rangelands; developing alternative agricultural and livestock farming systems based on low water agro-economy; and increasing the availability of qualified agro-technology cadres.

Name: W-D-0532-Water Tracker - Phase 2
Dscription: Water Resilience Tracker for National Climate Planning
Project Summary: As a component of the FCDO funded Just Transitions for Water Security  (JTWS), the Water Resilience Tracker for National Climate Planning will provide policy and technical assistance to increase political leadership, cooperation, and capacities across ministries and geographies to facilitate the design and implementation of inclusive, water resilient development plans, policies, programmes, and projects.  The knowledge generated through the implementation of the Water Resilience Tracker will be used to influence and guide decision making around water and climate. The integration of the Water Resilience Tracker within user sector agencies will encourage “silo-busting” and greater interactions amongst these sector agencies to identify common ground and meeting points around water, leading to more equitable and sustainable use and management of water as a resource, and enhance pathways for long-term water security.
Project results: The Water Resilience Tracker for Climate Planning (WRT) aims to mainstream climate resilient water management in planning and strategies across sectors. It offers both policy and technical support to governments, empowering them to plan, design, and implement inclusive climate-resilient policies that recognize the critical role of water in coping with climate change. By evaluating existing institutional structures and policies, the Tracker identifies gaps and subsequently, provides strategic as well as technical support at different levels to foster cooperation, strengthen leadership, and unlock access to climate finance

Name: W-D-0424-WRAP C1
Dscription: Water Resource Accountability in Pakistan (WRAP) Component 1

Potential institutions that will be supported under the project include the Provincial Planning and Development Department, water resources commissions, water regulatory authorities, irrigation, and on-farm water management departments. This Component will also improve the enabling environment for water sector reforms through better accountability, monitoring and narrative setting. Women, youth, and marginalized people will be specifically targeted with their engagement.
Project results: IWMI is implementing Component 1 of the Water Resource Accountability in Pakistan (WRAP) project. The long-term goal of this Component is to strengthen capacity to manage water resources at federal, provincial and district levels. Potential institutions that will be supported under the project include the Provincial Planning and Development Department, water resources commissions, water regulatory authorities, irrigation, and on-farm water management departments. This Component will also improve the enabling environment for water sector reforms through better accountability, monitoring and narrative setting. Women, youth, and marginalized people will be specifically targeted with their engagement, inputs and roles.

Name: A-HP2023-003-World Bank-Food Systems Resilience
Dscription: World Bank-Food Systems Resilience
Project Summary: The World Bank-Food Systems Resilience project, implemented by AfricaRice, is a comprehensive initiative aimed at bolstering the resilience of agricultural and food systems across Africa. Recognizing the growing impact of climate change and environmental pressures on food security, the project seeks to address these challenges by promoting adaptive and sustainable practices in agriculture. Central to the project is the focus on climate-smart agriculture, which involves the adoption of innovative technologies and practices designed to mitigate the effects of climate change while enhancing productivity. This includes the development and dissemination of resilient rice varieties that can withstand extreme weather conditions, such as droughts and floods. The project also supports the implementation of efficient irrigation systems and advanced soil management techniques to optimize resource use and improve crop yields.
Project results: (1) Regional food security enhancement; (2) Climate-resilient qgricultural practices: the program has promoted the adoption of climate-smart agriculture, leading to increased crop yields and resilience against climate change; (3) Infrastructure Development.

Name: N-601149-IPV RESEARCH COLLAB PH 2
Dscription: IPV RESEARCH COLLAB PH 2
Project Summary: IFPRI, in collaboration with partners, the University of North Carolina (UNC), the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine (LSHTM), University at Buffalo (UB, State University of New York) and Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health (JHSPH), propose a second phase of the collaborative research program on cash transfers and intimate partner violence (IPV). The overall long-term goal of the collaborative is to advance the research agenda linking cash transfers and IPV—defining and contributing evidence to knowledge gaps, aggregating evidence, bringing together researchers and practitioners, and disseminating research findings via channels which maximize evidence uptake for policy—in order to effectively leverage cash programming for IPV prevention among poor and vulnerable women in low- and middle-income counties (LIMCs). As part of this work, the collaborative will engage in the communication of research – in particular, bridging disciplinary boundaries between Social and Behavioral Scientists, Epidemiologists and Development Economists in order to bring together the strengths of each; build partnerships between researchers and implementers working in this area to bring coherence to future research; and raise the profile of IPV on the agenda of social protection policymakers and implementers. These efforts will seek to make IPV mitigation and prevention more explicit policy objectives within gender-sensitive and transformative social protection.
Project results: N/A

Name: W-P-XXX1-Jahez
Dscription: Jahez: Improving Anticipatory Action and Climate Change Adaptation in Refugee-Host Systems in JordanAL MURANA
Project Summary: This project has the objective of building climate resilience in refugee-hosting communities and along the humanitarian-development spectrum, through scalable interventions that integrate localized impact-based weather and climate information systems with community-based disaster risk reduction (DRR) and nature-based solutions (NbS), leveraging climate finance and other funding mechanisms. The cornerstone of Jahez will be a set of innovative, practical pilot projects in refugee-hosting communities in Jordan that prove the concept, and demonstrate the effectiveness to build resilience through holistic integration of community-based DRR and NbS.
Project results: The “Jahez (Ready)” project – has the objective of improving anticipatory action and climate change adaptation to build the resilience of refugees and their host communities in Jordan. It focuses on supporting effective localized use of impact-based weather and climate change forecasts to inform implementation of inclusive and equitable community-based Disaster Risk Reduction and Nature Based Solutions that address unique vulnerabilities of refugee–host communities.

Name: N-603217-FSP PHASE 4
Dscription: FSP PHASE 4
Project Summary: This Programme is a continuation of the EC-funded project on “World food crisis: Support for food security monitoring and analysis for appropriate policy responses” (Cris No: 2010 / 236-345). The first, three-year phase was initiated in 2010 in response to lessons that emerged from the 2007-08 world food crisis. Phase II of the same project ran from 2014-18 and focused upon expansion of the Global Food Security Portal (FSP) and development of an Africa South of the Sahara Food Security Portal (FSP-SSA). Several important lessons resulted from the world food price crisis, but a crucial one amongst them was that information for policymaking is not readily available at the country and regional levels. Lack of access to good information often results in inadequate and ineffective policy responses that not only exacerbate the immediate impacts of food crises but also tend to undermine long-term food security in developing countries. Such short-term policy responses may include panic purchases of food by food-deficit countries at above market prices, or food export restrictions introduced by food-surplus countries. Both responses have been shown to drive up world food prices and exacerbate price volatility. To avoid these “stop-go” policy decisions, governments require tools to rapidly assess the impact of world market and weather shocks on the food security situation and on the incomes and livelihoods of vulnerable populations. These tools inform policies and programmes that can adequately respond to the risks and opportunities presented by food crises. The 2007-08 global food price crisis also very clearly highlighted a great need for cross-country learning and dialogue. Dialogues have helped raise greater awareness about addressing food price volatility and preparedness to food price shocks. Moving forward, however, it should be recognized that, while food price volatility remains a concern today, bigger concerns are with food price volatility at the national level, more so than in global markets. Thus, greater emphasis will need to be put on monitoring country-level price changes and volatility for both staple and non-staple foods, especially in Africa.

Name: Climate-smart initiatives for climate change adaptation and sustainability in prioritized agricultural production systems in Colombia (CSICAP)
Dscription: The Climate-Smart Initiatives for Climate Change Adaptation and Sustainability in Prioritized Agricultural Production Systems in Colombia (CSICAP) project aims to safeguard livelihoods, boost productivity, and enhance profitability in nine of Colombia's key agri-food value chains, while increasing resilience to climate change impacts and reducing greenhouse gas emissions in the agriculture sector. With a USD $100 million budget, funded by the Green Climate Fund (GCF), Development Bank of Latin America (CAF), the government of Colombia, and partner contributions, CSICAP aims to benefit 200,000 people directly and nearly one million indirectly. The project will help reduce over 2 million tons of CO2 equivalent emissions through digital agroclimatic services, climate-resilient crops, water-efficient climate-smart practices, inclusive capacity-building, and innovative business models. It will be implemented in 22 out of 32 departments in Colombia.

Name: F-AG10607-ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Dscription: ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)

Name: D-100867-PRIMA: Lost and found: Neglected and Underutilized Species (NUS) and water harvesting for building climate change resilience, soil fertility, dietary diversity, and women’s empowerment
Dscription: PRIMA: Lost and found: Neglected and Underutilized Species (NUS) and water harvesting for building climate change resilience, soil fertility, dietary diversity, and women’s empowerment
Project Summary:
NUSTALGIC aims to empower farmers on arid land to adopt crops and related technologies, support agricultural and food product processors, and to educate consumers about the value of these products. Women and girls are disproportionately challenged in participating in these processes through restrictive social norms and access to information, resources, and agency. NUSTALGIC will develop ways for overcoming these barriers by introducing gender-transformative social enterprises.
This research line is designed to implement different water harvesting techniques to help capture and retain water in arid and semi-arid Mediterranean regions with the aim of increasing water harvesting and efficiency. The project aims to diversify agricultural systems, improve soil health, and enhance resilience to climate change. In addition, create job opportunities and develop new products and value chains using Neglected and Underutilized Species (NUS). Finally, the project will implement a robust multi-stakeholder engagement strategy across diverse sectors and levels.
Center: International Center for Agricultural Research in the Dry Areas

Dscription: Accelerating Scaling of Low-Emissions Rice in Southeast Asia
Project Summary:
The ScaLER-SEA project will support national governments in Southeast Asia for accelerated dissemination of low-emission rice technologies and practices. It will focus on two main aspects: (1) working closely with national partners to co-develop implementation roadmaps towards an aspirational 15% reduction of methane emissions of the target  countries’ rice sectors by 2030; and (2) developing and mainstreaming remote sensing-enabled monitoring, reporting and verification (MRV) systems that allow better targeting of
technology dissemination and tracking of progress towards the 2030 goals.

Name: V0145-Sustainable AgriFood Systems Approach for Sudan - SA
Dscription: Sustainable AgriFood Systems Approach for Sudan - SASAS
Project Summary: The SASAS Program, led by CIMMYT and funded by USAID under the Feed the Future Initiatives, was initially designed in 2022. Following the outbreak of war in Sudan on April 15, 2023, SASAS pivoted to focus on enhancing food security by building resilient agrifood systems. Key interventions include promoting smallholder agricultural production with improved technologies, boosting value chains and business development, managing natural resources for social cohesion, improving horticultural practices, and establishing livestock services. These efforts are aimed at strengthening the agricultural value chain and supporting Sudanese communities in a conflict-affected environment.
Center: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center / Centro Internacional de Mejoramiento de Maíz y Trigo

Name: A-AG10156 AICCRA PROJECT
Dscription: A-AICCRA
Project Summary: The AICCRA (Accelerating Impacts of CGIAR Climate Research for Africa) project, implemented by AfricaRice, is designed to enhance climate resilience in African agriculture. This initiative focuses on integrating climate change adaptation strategies into agricultural practices to address the impacts of a changing climate on food systems. The AICCRA project employs a multi-dimensional approach to build resilience among smallholder farmers and agricultural communities. It emphasizes the development and promotion of climate-smart agricultural practices, including the adoption of drought-resistant crop varieties, improved soil management techniques, and efficient water use strategies. These measures aim to improve productivity while mitigating the negative effects of climate variability. The project also focuses on strengthening agricultural research and extension services to provide farmers with timely and relevant information about climate adaptation. By fostering partnerships with local research institutions, government agencies, and farmer organizations, AICCRA ensures that adaptation strategies are tailored to the specific needs and conditions of different regions.
Project results: A-AG10156 AICCRA PROJECT

Name: T-PJ-003763-IITA Accelerated Innovation Delivery Initiative
Dscription: IITA Accelerated Innovation Delivery Initiative – AID-I: Great Lakes Highlands
Project Summary: The Accelerated Innovation Delivery Initiative in the Great Lakes Region (AID-I GLR) is a two-year initiative funded by USAID to enhance food and nutrition security in Burundi, the Democratic Republic of Congo and Rwanda. It aims to reach 1.2 million farmers by rapidly scaling up proven agricultural technologies and innovations developed primarily by CGIAR and partners. This initiative focuses on creating and structuring partnerships with the private sector, government agencies and NGOs to deliver these technologies, adapted to local needs, along with advisory services to a large network of farmers. AID-I GLR is part of the U.S. response to the global food security crisis, targeting both immediate and long-term impacts on food security in the region.
Project results: Two new partnerships were established in Burundi and DRC, and nine market actors enrolled. The project reached 774,965 participants (50% women, 22% youth), exceeding targets. Over 600,000 smallholder farmers received quality seeds, and 92,000 accessed fortified maize flour. Scaling events engaged 350,000 farmers, 770 demonstration plots were established, and 99,000 stakeholders trained. Additionally, 147.2 metric tons of Early Generation Seeds were produced, and market actors sold 8,200 Mt of inputs worth $3.6 million.

Name: C0042 USAID-CSISA-MEA
Dscription: USAID-CSISA-MI
Project Summary: The Cereal Systems Initiative for South Asia – Mechanization and Irrigation (CSISA-MI) is a CIMMYT-led project in partnership with iDE under USAID’s Feed the Future (FtF) initiative, aimed at enhancing agricultural productivity in southern Bangladesh. The project focuses on promoting efficient irrigation systems, such as Axial Flow Pumps (AFPs), to enable dry-season cropping, alongside mechanization technologies like Power Tiller Operated Seeders (PTOS) and reapers to reduce labor costs and improve efficiency. CSISA-MI also fosters private sector engagement by supporting local businesses to develop a sustainable supply chain for mechanization services. By improving farm-level efficiency, expanding access to modern agricultural equipment, and encouraging climate-resilient practices, the project seeks to increase farmer incomes, enhance food security, and promote sustainable agricultural growth in Bangladesh.
Center: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center / Centro Internacional de Mejoramiento de Maíz y Trigo

Dscription: Youth In Agribusiness: enabling scaling of innovative technologies for sustainable food solutions
Project results: In FCT, Kano, Ogun, and Oyo, the YAS project trained youth in aquaculture, poultry, and horticulture through strategic partnerships with organizations like NABC, GFFR, ILRI, and PANOG. Training covered technical skills, business scaling, and market linkages. High application rates showed strong youth interest, highlighting the need for increased funding and awareness to support agribusiness growth.

Name: A-HP2023-003-World Bank-Food Systems Resilience
Dscription: World Bank-Food Systems Resilience
Comments: We agree, but a 20% allocaton to a bilateral on scaliong with integration of IFIs that was aligned to the Progam seems small.
Project Summary: The World Bank-Food Systems Resilience project, implemented by AfricaRice, is a comprehensive initiative aimed at bolstering the resilience of agricultural and food systems across Africa. Recognizing the growing impact of climate change and environmental pressures on food security, the project seeks to address these challenges by promoting adaptive and sustainable practices in agriculture. Central to the project is the focus on climate-smart agriculture, which involves the adoption of innovative technologies and practices designed to mitigate the effects of climate change while enhancing productivity. This includes the development and dissemination of resilient rice varieties that can withstand extreme weather conditions, such as droughts and floods. The project also supports the implementation of efficient irrigation systems and advanced soil management techniques to optimize resource use and improve crop yields.
Project results: (1) Regional food security enhancement; (2) Climate-resilient qgricultural practices: the program has promoted the adoption of climate-smart agriculture, leading to increased crop yields and resilience against climate change; (3) Infrastructure Development.

Name: T-PJ-003942-IITA Accelerated Innovation Delivery Initiative (AID-I) for Mozambique

Project Summary: This initiative seeks to boost food and nutrition security, incomes and resilience for smallholder farmers in Mozambique's Nampula, Zambézia and Niassa provinces. It aims to scale proven agricultural and nutritional innovations to tackle low productivity, extreme weather impacts and global supply chain disruptions. This program focuses on improving seed systems, enhancing village livestock productivity, integrating soil and crop management, increasing access to nutrient-dense foods and expanding market access and credit. Targeting 750,000 smallholder farmers, this initiative emphasizes gender equality, social inclusion and capacity development. By addressing these key areas, this project intends to increase productivity and profitability, ultimately improving the livelihoods of farmers and their communities.
Project results: Improved functionality of seed systems that cater to the needs of smallholder farmers, leading to increased productivity, enhanced market access, and greater availability of nutrient-dense foods.

Name: USA - USAID - Business Acceleration for Youth project (BAFY)
Project Summary:
The Business Acceleration for Youth Project (formally known as the Youth Business Acceleration and
Investment Facility) is a three-year (2021-2024), innovative initiative funded by USAID Malawi and led by the
Alliance of Bioversity International and International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT). Framed around
a resilience-led sustainability strategy, the project aims to strengthen the resilience of youth and women-led
business start-ups in Balaka, Chikwawa, Chiradzulu, Machinga, Mangochi, Mulanje, Thyolo, Nsanje and
Zomba, through the delivery of business incubation services creating growth-orientated, sustainable firms
able to secure and effectively utilize investment.
Nationally, the project is supporting Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs) through the delivery of bespoke
technical support, industry specific expertise, mentorship and access to finance. The purpose of the project
is to accelerate and invest in youth-led enterprises that result in job creation, greater access to financing,
diversified incomes, a more inclusive private sector and improved household resilience in vulnerable districts.

Project Summary: The Seeds4Liberia project aims to enhance food and nutrition security in Liberia by developing a demand-driven, well-structured, and regulated seed system. The focus is on four critical agricultural commodities (rice, cassava, cocoa, and coffee) collectively referred to as the "target crops," as well as fish farming. The primary objectives of Seeds4Liberia include strengthening seed systems by creating reliable networks for seed production and distribution that are responsive to the specific needs of Liberian farmers, ensuring they have access to high-quality seeds that are resilient and high yielding. By providing improved seeds and promoting best agricultural practices, the project seeks to significantly boost the productivity of these target crops, leading to increased food production and better livelihoods for smallholder farmers. The project also aims to enhance food and nutrition security by increasing the availability of diverse and nutritious foods through both crop and fish farming.

Name: T-PJ-003262-An innovative approach to agribusiness training and start-up for Nigerias young people within the Young Africa Works Program
Dscription: An innovative approach to agribusiness training and start-up for Nigerias young people within the Young Africa Works Program
Project Summary: This IITA-led project, in partnership with the MasterCard Foundation's Young Africa Works (YAW) Program, aims to enhance self-employment and employment opportunities for Nigerian youth aged 15-35 in agrifood systems across Kaduna, Kano and Lagos. This project focuses on improving education and training systems, connecting youth with agribusinesses and providing access to specialized credit. Key outputs include strengthened training curricula, establishment of Youth Agribusiness Parks and development of youth-sensitive financial support programs. This project targets over 128,000 youth, creating approximately 9,180 new jobs and delivering services through a network of diverse partners. It is set to improve youth engagement and support in agriculture, especially for young women, over five years with a budget of $12.4 million.
Project results: In Q2YR5, I-Youth trained 4,286 youths (39% of the 10,940 target) for employment and entrepreneurship, with 81% and 49% female participation, respectively. 7,293 jobs were created, mainly in Kaduna and Adamawa. Business registration support was provided to 3,141 businesses, and ₦10.3M in loans was accessed. Agribusiness parks expanded in Kano, Lagos, and Kaduna, enhancing digital and e-commerce training.

This project focuses on improving livelihoods for small producers, women and youth through the adoption of post-harvest technologies and scaling up cassava processing to produce high-quality, locally processed foods that can substitute for imports like wheat flour. Key outcomes include the development of the cassava agro-industrial sector, innovation in cassava processing and capacity building for bread bakers. This project also emphasizes the importance of government policies and infrastructure to support the sustainable growth of the cassava sector.

And 5) to collaborate in the development of outreach and education materials regarding eh programs for external audiences.

Outcomes: 1) The Amazon’s topical forest, ecosystems and biodiversity are conserved in biologically significant ways areas; 2) The Well-being of rural communities in the Amazon is improved; 3) Buffer zones and ecological corridors are conserved and/or recovered; 4) The enabling environment, and scalable, biodiversity friendly business models, are established, and 5) Climate change is mitigated through avoided deforestation and reforestation.
