Name: BIUSA (BEF)-Screening, developing, and deploying anti-methanogenic feedstock into livestock systems in the Global South
Dscription:
Description and project summary both filled. The "Screening, Developing, and Deploying Anti-Methanogenic Feedstock into Livestock Systems in the Global South" project aims to reduce methane (CH4) emissions from livestock, the single largest emitter in the agricultural sector, by leveraging the potential of novel compounds in CGIAR’s global collections of forages. This initiative involves screening, developing, and deploying anti-methanogenic feedstock into livestock systems in the Global South to achieve a significant reduction in greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, equivalent to taking 7 million internal combustion engine cars off the road each year. Scope and Activities:
1. Discovery (WP1):
o Screen CGIAR gene bank collections to identify accessions with high levels of anti-methanogenic compounds (AMCs).
o Conduct metabolomics analyses to identify known and novel AMCs.
o Validate the anti-methanogenic activity of selected accessions through in vivo and in vitro assays.
2. Development (WP2):
o Promote the use of AMC-rich forage legumes directly in grazing systems.
o Enhance AMC levels in widely used forage-grass cultivars through conventional breeding and gene-editing techniques.
3. Deployment (WP3):
o Establish public-private partnerships for large-scale multi-environment feeding/grazing trials.
o Develop Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification (MRV) systems for AMC-rich forages.
o Facilitate the adoption of AMC-rich forages in target regions.
Comments: We agree, but have some concerns that a 5% allocation may result in high coordination and transactions costs relative to what can be acieved with the financial resources resulting from the mapping
Project Summary: The "Screening, Developing, and Deploying Anti-Methanogenic Feedstock into Livestock Systems in the Global South" project targets a significant reduction in methane emissions from livestock. By leveraging CGIAR’s global collections of forages, the project will screen, develop, and deploy anti-methanogenic feedstock, aiming to mitigate greenhouse gas emissions on a large scale. The project involves three main phases: discovery of AMC-rich forages, development of these forages through breeding and gene-editing, and deployment in livestock systems through public-private partnerships. Major outcomes include reduced methane emissions, improved livestock productivity, and enhanced sustainability of agricultural practices in the Global South
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: P-1568-WBS0-Developing Local Seed Potato Production System i
Dscription: Developing Local Seed Potato Production System in West Bengal
Project Summary: The ARC Project in West Bengal aims to tackle the critical issue of the unavailability of quality potato seeds of popular varieties, which leads to low productivity and poor tuber quality in the state. To address this, the project plans to enhance seed and varietal replacement and improve tuber quality by developing strategies to supply clean, healthy planting material of new and existing varieties preferred by all value chain actors, including the processing industry, using the low-cost apical rooted cutting (ARC) technology. This strategy involves establishing new infrastructure and efficiently utilizing existing facilities to expand the production of tissue culture plantlets, apical rooted cuttings, G0 production, and further multiplication.

(1) Rapid development of high yielding varieties with resistance/tolerance to drought, heat and diseases through innovative approaches  
(2) Asses the performance of the existing seed systems, and design, test and establish more efficient and gender responsive seed systems 
(3) Multiplication of existing improved varieties (recently released but not adopted)

Name: P-1743 SoLAR Phase 2
Dscription: Solar irrigation for Agricultural Resilience - Phase II
Project Summary: The program aims to strengthen the enabling environment and unlock investments for sustainable scaling of socially inclusive and climate-resilient solar agri-tech solutions in South Asia (India, Bangladesh) and East Africa (Ethiopia, Kenya). It will deliver across four main outcomes (OCs) that relate to Evidence-based policy design (OC1), Accelerating finance (OC2), Capacity Building (OC3) and Scaling Solar through Living Labs (OC4). Through this, the project will support improvement of energy and water security for vulnerable populations, help mitigate emissions from agriculture and increase adaptive capacity and resilience to climate change.
Project results:
The program outcomes will strengthen existing governance structures and systems and enable policies in the agriculture and energy sectors to ensure sustainable scaling and financing of solar energy for agriculture and food systems in South Asia and East Africa. The Project will deliver across four main outcomes in selected countries - South Asia (India, Bangladesh) and East Africa (Ethiopia, Kenya). The four outcomes together cover key elements needed to unpack barriers to scale solar energy for agriculture (SESA) - policy, finance, capacity and scaling (Figure 1). The resulting outputs and activities will work together synergistically in a logical and effective way, ensuring that the program goals and impact are achieved. Outcome 1 will develop decision-support tools and principles for effective scaling for policymakers, Outcome 2 will enhance these tools by introducing innovative financing options. However, without adequate capacity and buy-in from end-users, the challenges for scaling solar will persist - Outcomes 3 and 4 will leverage findings from Outcomes 1 and 2 to ensure that stakeholders are informed and equipped to utilize these innovations. Outcome 4 will focus on scaling and optimizing through existing platforms and living labs, such that sustainability of the innovations is well embedded beyond the timeframe of this program.

Tools, methodologies, and products from this phase will be co-designed and developed alongside partners who play leading roles in solar energy upscaling and policy development, and eventually will be embedded and hosted by stakeholders including government (e.g. Ministries such as Ministry of New and Renewable Energy of India, Ministry of Agriculture, Ethiopia) and implementing agencies (e.g. National Solar Help Desk of Bangladesh).

Name: T-PJ-003772-TAAT Clearinghouse: Re-invest to Accelerate Innovation Adoption
Dscription: TAAT Clearinghouse: Re-invest to Accelerate Innovation Adoption
Project Summary: The Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation (BMGF) and the African Development Bank (AfDB) have been co-funding the TAAT Clearinghouse (CH) for four years, with an annual budget of approximately $2 million. This CH aims to optimize the success of national and regional agricultural investment programs by integrating the most effective technologies into these initiatives. It serves as a broker, facilitating the adoption of CGIAR-led research technologies and others, into investment projects primarily funded by AfDB. By leveraging AfDB's African Development Fund and sovereign loans, the CH promotes the deployment and scaling of vetted technologies, focusing on improving agricultural productivity and outcomes across Africa.
Project results: The CH integrated 45 TAAT-vetted technologies into national and regional agricultural projects, exceeding the 25-case target. It participated in 12 AfDB missions, supporting new project designs. A remodeled Country Engagement Strategy enhanced technology brokerage, while new engagement officers expanded outreach. Gender and private sector strategies were developed, alongside monitoring tools and capacity-building initiatives.

Project Summary:
NUSTALGIC aims to empower farmers on arid land to adopt crops and related technologies, support agricultural and food product processors, and to educate consumers about the value of these products. Women and girls are disproportionately challenged in participating in these processes through restrictive social norms and access to information, resources, and agency. NUSTALGIC will develop ways for overcoming these barriers by introducing gender-transformative social enterprises.
This research line is designed to implement different water harvesting techniques to help capture and retain water in arid and semi-arid Mediterranean regions with the aim of increasing water harvesting and efficiency. The project aims to diversify agricultural systems, improve soil health, and enhance resilience to climate change. In addition, create job opportunities and develop new products and value chains using Neglected and Underutilized Species (NUS). Finally, the project will implement a robust multi-stakeholder engagement strategy across diverse sectors and levels.

Central to the project is the focus on climate-smart agriculture, which involves the adoption of innovative technologies and practices designed to mitigate the effects of climate change while enhancing productivity. This includes the development and dissemination of resilient rice varieties that can withstand extreme weather conditions, such as droughts and floods. The project also supports the implementation of efficient irrigation systems and advanced soil management techniques to optimize resource use and improve crop yields.
Project results: (1) Regional food security enhancement; (2) Climate-resilient qgricultural practices: the program has promoted the adoption of climate-smart agriculture, leading to increased crop yields and resilience against climate change; (3) Infrastructure Development.

Name: N333010
Dscription: Shea Butter Value Chain and Women
Project Summary: We introduce efficiency improving as well as value adding innovations to women’s cooperatives that engage in shea collection in disadvantaged areas of Ghana. The empowerment of women in the above context is expected to not only increase productivity and reduce loss in shea collection and thus women’s incomes but also create various welfare improving effects such as human capital investments in children and additional investments to generate household incomes. Specifically, the intervention introduces tricycles and dehusking/ drying equipment to all target cooperatives, and in some cooperatives, aggregation warehouses. In particular, the introduction of tricycles will be a breakthrough to dramatically increase productivity and reduce loss during shea collection and transport. Dehusking and drying equipment will increase value added. The interventions draw upon the expertise accumulated in a Japanese company, Fuji Oil Inc. and therefore will be implemented in the collaboration with the company.

Name: T-PJ-003778-SGP-RTB - Roots Tubers and Bananas Breeding: a consolidated investment
Dscription: PJ-003778: SGP-RTB - Roots Tubers and Bananas Breeding: a consolidated investment
Project Summary: This RTB Breeding Project aims to alleviate poverty and enhance food and nutrition security in sub-Saharan Africa by developing market-preferred, gender-sensitive and climate-resilient varieties of RTB crops. Key objectives include implementing modern breeding approaches, optimizing breeding networks and fostering cross-crop collaboration in Ghana, Mozambique, Nigeria, Tanzania and Uganda. Expected outcomes involve advancing breeding techniques, consolidating state-of-the-art practices and developing technologies essential for effective breeding. This project targets smallholder farmers, processors and consumers, with a focus on improving their quality of life and food security while promoting gender equality and environmental sustainability. The goal is to build a resilient and inclusive agricultural system across diverse regions.

Name: GAC-SCALING UP CLIMATE INFORMATION SERVICES AND CLIMATE- SMART AGRICULTURE FOR WOMEN FARMERS IN NICARAGUA’S  DRY CORRIDOR
Dscription:
Nicaragua is the fourth poorest country1 in Latin America and the Caribbean (LAC) after Haiti, Honduras and Guatemala, and ranks 21st out of 181 countries with the highest disaster risk.2 The development of Nicaragua, which relies heavily on agriculture, is disproportionally impacted by its vulnerability to climatic events: 25% of the population lives in areas at risk of hurricanes and tropical storms and 40% is affected by recurring drought, with communities in the Dry Corridor experiencing the greatest impacts of climate change. According to climate models, Nicaragua could become as warm as the Sahara by 2070, with average annual temperatures over 29°C, only found in 0.8% of the
globe today. The country will suffer more intense droughts and variable rainfall patterns. This threatens food and water security, along with biodiversity, and is expected to worsen the existing poverty and climate vulnerability of millions of households in Nicaragua, and further exacerbate irregular migration.Accessible, credible, and relevant climate and weather information services (CWIS)3 can vastly improve decision-making in disaster management, agriculture, biodiversity conservation and water
resources management. However, the access and use of CWIS is still limited in Nicaragua. The poorest and most vulnerable, especially in remote rural communities, are not benefitting from the scientific and technological advancements that could help manage climate risks and adapt their farming practices. Women farmers (including those from indigenous and ethnic minority groups) face the most barriers in accessing CWIS, as gender norms and patriarchal values in Nicaragua reinforce inequalities in access to and management of productive resources. Through the Project, the Organization will scale-up climate information and weather services and promote the adoption of climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices for at least 1,000 women from rural communities in the Dry Corridor of Nicaragua. Activities are designed to support the following:  
• Rural women smallholders and their organizations active in Nicaragua’s Dry Corridor actively access and use CWIS tailored to their specific information needs to manage climate and weather risk more effectively.
• Women smallholders empowered to adopt CSA technologies (climate smart crop varieties, best-fit crop management practices, digital tools, etc.) at a greater rate. 
• Service providers (including farmers’ organizations, National Agricultural Research and Extension Systems, NGOs, etc.) improve the quality and gender-responsiveness of the services and information they provide to women farmers and value chain actors.
• A network of at least 15 female community leaders become powerful advocates for women and girls in the design of gender responsive CWIS, the adaptation of CSA innovation bundles, and in climate governance and decision-making.
Men and women in farming households participate in community dialogues and household methodologies to reflect on and question harmful gender norms and attitudes and to foster more equitable gender relations and workloads.
• Women and girls in the Dry Corridor use access to small grants (incentives) and training and technical assistance (TTA) products to scale up adoption of CSA and increase income from farm business ideas centering around climate adaptation, biodiversity conservation, nature-positive solutions, and circular economy principles.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: W-D-0392-NET: WaPOR Phase 2
Dscription: Monitoring land and water productivity by Remote Sensing (WaPOR Phase 2)
Project Summary:
The outcome of the project is defined as “Increased capacity of FAO Members (Member Nations and Member Organizations) in monitoring terrestrial biomass production and evapotranspiration to assess and improve land and water productivity in rainfed and irrigated agriculture, and contribute to sustainable crop production intensification adapted to a changing climate.”

The three major Outputs of the project are:
1. Consolidated open-access information and related operational services on the terrestrial soil water balance and agricultural land and water productivity, including refinement and expansion towards global coverage of the spatial scales, and creation of longer time series (Spatial database);
2. Strengthened capacities of FAO Members to assess land and water productivity aimed at informed decision making for improved agricultural practices and efficient water management by multiple stakeholder groups to minimize impacts and adapt to climate change and other environmental and societal changes (Capacity development); and
3. Compendium of implementable solutions and tools, co-developed with various stakeholders at different operational scales, from farm to the national level, to effectively increase land and water productivity in agriculture sustainably at field level as well as to support water management, water governance and agricultural policies (Compendium of implementable solutions and tools).

Dscription: Mastercard - RIZAO
Project Summary: The Mastercard-RIZAO project (West African Economic and Monetary Union), spearheaded by AfricaRice, aims to significantly improve agricultural productivity and economic development within the West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU) by addressing critical challenges in the rice sector. The initiative focuses on enhancing financial inclusion by leveraging MasterCard’s digital tools to provide rice farmers with access to credit, savings, and insurance, thereby enabling better investment and risk management. In parallel, AfricaRice will introduce and promote climate-smart and high-yielding rice varieties, alongside modern farming techniques in water management, pest control, and soil fertility, to boost productivity and resilience. The project also aims to strengthen market connections by improving the supply chain and reducing transaction costs, thereby increasing farmers' incomes and facilitating better access to local, regional, and international markets. Capacity building is a key component, with extensive training provided to farmers, extension workers, and other stakeholders to enhance rice quality and reduce post-harvest losses.
Project results: A-HP2023-002-MasterCard - WAEMU

Name: P-1580-IAI0-Market-led Transformation of the Root and Tuber
Dscription: Market-led Transformation of the Root and Tuber Crops Subsector in Malawi (RTC-MARKET)
Center: International Potato Center / Centro Internacional de la Papa

Name: N-601149-IPV RESEARCH COLLAB PH 2
Dscription: IPV RESEARCH COLLAB PH 2
Project Summary: IFPRI, in collaboration with partners, the University of North Carolina (UNC), the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine (LSHTM), University at Buffalo (UB, State University of New York) and Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health (JHSPH), propose a second phase of the collaborative research program on cash transfers and intimate partner violence (IPV). The overall long-term goal of the collaborative is to advance the research agenda linking cash transfers and IPV—defining and contributing evidence to knowledge gaps, aggregating evidence, bringing together researchers and practitioners, and disseminating research findings via channels which maximize evidence uptake for policy—in order to effectively leverage cash programming for IPV prevention among poor and vulnerable women in low- and middle-income counties (LIMCs). As part of this work, the collaborative will engage in the communication of research – in particular, bridging disciplinary boundaries between Social and Behavioral Scientists, Epidemiologists and Development Economists in order to bring together the strengths of each; build partnerships between researchers and implementers working in this area to bring coherence to future research; and raise the profile of IPV on the agenda of social protection policymakers and implementers. These efforts will seek to make IPV mitigation and prevention more explicit policy objectives within gender-sensitive and transformative social protection.

Name: P-1490-DFAT-Generating Revenues and Opportunities for Women
Dscription: Generating Revenues and Opportunities for Women to Improve Nutrition in Ghana (GROWING)
Project Summary: The Generating Revenues for improved nutrition in northern Ghana (GROWING) project targets women of reproductive age (15-49 years) and their children from rural households in the north of Ghana from across an estimated 72 villages. The ultimate outcome of the initiative is the improved nutritional and financial security of women, youth, and young children in 6 selected districts. This outcome will be achieved through a multi-sectoral nutrition-sensitive value chain development approach, using activities such as: (1) providing Healthy Baby Toolkits, Goal cards, planting materials of orange-fleshed sweet potato, moringa and papaya to Healthy Living Club participant households; (2) producing gender-responsive videos, related media tools, and collaborations for facilitating training activities and nutritious crop uptake in targeted communities: and (3) providing technical assistance in consultation with communities to identify and train male and female gender champions.
Center: International Potato Center / Centro Internacional de la Papa

Name: N-2024P0X2-EVITA/IFAD GTM ETHIOPIA
Dscription: EVITA/IFAD GTM ETHIOPIA
Project Summary: The PACT-GTM Gender Impact Assessment Study is an independent assessment commissioned by the Gates Foundation in its capacity as the donor to the GTM facility. The PACT-GTM Gender Impact Assessment Study has been commissioned by UoW to its contracted service provider, IFPRI. This study will assess gender-relevant aspects of PACT and overall GTM value addition to PACT. The study includes selected indicators from the PACT logframe, the Project-level Women’s Empowerment in Agriculture Index for Market Inclusion (the Pro-WEAI+MI) indicators, and specific additional indicators requested by the GF. The ultimate objective is to assess GTM’s value addition and achievements of GF’s investment in the GTM component of PACT.

Name: N-332001-COST EFF WE-AG SOLUTIONS
Dscription: COST EFF WE-AG SOLUTIONS
Project Summary: The primary result of this investment will be a shared internal and external understanding of the 3-4 most effective and cost-effective interventions that governments can scale to improve WE in Ag outcomes in low-income countries. We will know that this result has been achieved when we and our partners are able to succinctly articulate the expected cost-effectiveness of different interventions to governments in our priority countries, based on evidence from a combination of impact evaluations, costing information and economywide modeling. The project approach will build on a stepwise approach developed by the Strengthening Economic Evaluation for Multisectoral Strategies for Nutrition (SEEMS-Nutrition) consortium, including a common approach to conduct economic evaluations integrated with program impact evaluations and costing analyses for multisector nutrition programs. This approach aims to strengthen the design and implementation of economic evaluations, with a focus on context-specific operational plans, or study protocols. It also utilizes a comprehensive and fully customizable set of cost data collection tools for estimating financial and economic costs. The SEEMS-Nutrition framework will be extended to fully account of women’s empowerment related activities, outcomes and benefit streams.

Name: F-PP-2022-1020-Climate-Resilient Aquaculture Systems for Afr (AG10618)
Dscription: Climate-Resilient Aquaculture Systems for Africa (CASA)
Project Summary: WorldFish and partners are Implementing the “Climate-Resilient Aquaculture Systems for Africa - CASA” in Kenya and Nigeria. CASA delivers solutions that simultaneously increase productivity and profitability, climate adaptation and environmental sustainability, food and nutrition security, and the expansion of employment and income opportunities within the aquaculture systems. CASA has four Interlinked Intervention Areas (IAs). Under IA1, CASA will deliver income and productivity gains for most of the farmers, with an estimated 1x - 2x increase in productivity for the average farmer per cycle, and the best-performing farmers obtaining 3x increase in productivity per cycle. Under IA2, CASA aims to reach 1,000 fish processors with improved, climate-smart fish processing technologies and practices. Under IA3, CASA will support the development and piloting of financial products and services which target the aquaculture value chains. Under IA4, CASA will support the strengthening of the policy environment and the capacity development of policymakers.

Name: T-PJ-003801-Cassava Value Chain Development to Support Food Security and the Bread Industry in DRC
Dscription: Cassava Value Chain Development to Support Food Security and the Bread Industry in DRC
Project Summary: This cassava value chain project, aligned with USAID's Feed the Future program, aims to enhance the economic potential of the cassava sector in the DRC. This project focuses on improving livelihoods for small producers, women and youth through the adoption of post-harvest technologies and scaling up cassava processing to produce high-quality, locally processed foods that can substitute for imports like wheat flour. Key outcomes include the development of the cassava agro-industrial sector, innovation in cassava processing and capacity building for bread bakers. This project also emphasizes the importance of government policies and infrastructure to support the sustainable growth of the cassava sector.
Project results: Produce 30,000 tons of high-quality cassava products annually from 120,000 tons of smallholder-grown roots. Cultivate 12,000 ha with improved varieties, benefiting 12,600 farmers, including 600 seed growers. Establish 1,200 ha of seed farms, with INERA supplying 60 ha of basic seeds.
Countries: The Democratic Republic of the Congo

Name: CANADA -GAC - Beans For Women For Empowerment  In Eastern DRC (BEANS4WOMEN)
Dscription: Both description and project summary both filled. The "Beans for Women for Empowerment (Beans4Women)" project aims to strengthen gender equality, participation in decision-making, and resource rights of women and girls active in the bean sector in three conflict-prone provinces of Eastern DRC: North Kivu, South Kivu, and Tanganyika. The project addresses social inequalities, poverty, and gender-based violence while leveraging the significant participation of women in the bean economy to drive transformative change. Scope and Activities are: 1) Business Environment: Upskilling and brokering new business opportunities for women-led bean enterprises and female entrepreneurs + Accelerating the uptake of climate-smart technologies by women bean producers; 2) Leadership and Decision-Making: Empowering women and girl voices in bean economy leadership and decision-making + trengthening the capacity and responsiveness of the public sector to deliver gender-responsive programming and policies; 3) Resource Rights: Improving and enforcing institutional arrangements around women’s land rights + Outreach, education, and bean product development activities aimed at influencing nutrition behaviors and attitudes, making fortified, improved, or high-iron beans a bigger part of diets in Eastern DRC. Implementation and Partnerships are: 1) Building on robust existing partnerships with local and international NGOs and bean value chain actors; 2) Engaging with local partners to co-develop activities that generate sustainable and gender-sensitive outputs; 3) Monitoring and Evaluation: Establishing a conflict sensitivity action plan + Regular reporting on progress and achievements, linked directly to the performance measurement framework.
Project Summary: The "Beans for Women for Empowerment (Beans4Women)" project in Eastern DRC focuses on enhancing gender equality, decision-making, and resource rights for women and girls in the bean sector. Targeting North Kivu, South Kivu, and Tanganyika provinces, the project aims to upskill women-led enterprises, promote climate-smart agricultural practices, and empower women in leadership roles. Major outcomes include increased business opportunities, improved climate resilience, and strengthened land rights. The project is expected to directly benefit 2 million people, primarily women and girls, and indirectly reach 10 million people over five years by leveraging robust partnerships and inclusive, sustainable interventions.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)
Countries: Madagascar, Uganda, Mozambique, Malawi, Zambia, Zimbabwe, Ghana, Burundi, Ethiopia, Guinea, Cameroon, Rwanda, Kenya

Name: F-AG10607-ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Dscription: ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Project Summary: Asia–Africa BlueTech Superhighway (AABS) harnesses South–South collaboration to assess, adapt and scale evidence-based innovations and models for delivering nature-positive impact through aquatic food systems. Implemented by WorldFish in collaboration with a host of partners, AABS aims to transform livelihoods of coastal communities across African and Asian countries, particularly for women and youth, and help restore marine and coastal ecosystems by developing sustainable and resilient aquatic food systems. Funded by the UK Government under its Blue Planet Fund, AABS is being implemented in two phases over the course of seven years (2023–2030).

Name: T-PJ-003262-An innovative approach to agribusiness training and start-up for Nigerias young people within the Young Africa Works Program
Dscription: An innovative approach to agribusiness training and start-up for Nigerias young people within the Young Africa Works Program
Project Summary: This IITA-led project, in partnership with the MasterCard Foundation's Young Africa Works (YAW) Program, aims to enhance self-employment and employment opportunities for Nigerian youth aged 15-35 in agrifood systems across Kaduna, Kano and Lagos. This project focuses on improving education and training systems, connecting youth with agribusinesses and providing access to specialized credit. Key outputs include strengthened training curricula, establishment of Youth Agribusiness Parks and development of youth-sensitive financial support programs. This project targets over 128,000 youth, creating approximately 9,180 new jobs and delivering services through a network of diverse partners. It is set to improve youth engagement and support in agriculture, especially for young women, over five years with a budget of $12.4 million.
Project results: In Q2YR5, I-Youth trained 4,286 youths (39% of the 10,940 target) for employment and entrepreneurship, with 81% and 49% female participation, respectively. 7,293 jobs were created, mainly in Kaduna and Adamawa. Business registration support was provided to 3,141 businesses, and ₦10.3M in loans was accessed. Agribusiness parks expanded in Kano, Lagos, and Kaduna, enhancing digital and e-commerce training.

Name: V0146-AID-I : Southern Africa
Dscription: AID-I : Southern Africa
Project Summary: The Southern Africa Accelerated Innovation Delivery Initiative (AID-I) Rapid Delivery Hub, led by USAID under Feed the Future, is a two-year rapid response effort to mitigate the impact of high fuel and fertilizer prices on farmers. Targeting up to three million smallholder farmers in Malawi, Tanzania, and Zambia, AID-I improves soil health, fertilizer management, seed systems, financial access, and extension services. It establishes agile rural innovation hubs to enhance technology adaptation, sustainable input and output development, and value chain diversification. The project focuses on scaling resilient agronomic practices and strengthening rural-urban linkages for better national cross-network management.
Center: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center / Centro Internacional de Mejoramiento de Maíz y Trigo
Countries: South Africa, Lesotho, Eswatini, Namibia, Zambia, Botswana, Mozambique, Malawi

Name: D-200399-AFESD - ICARDA Dryland Academy: Capacity Developmen
Dscription: AFESD - ICARDA Dryland Academy: Capacity Development and Technical Agricultural Assistance to Arab Countries to Support Food Security and Climate Change Adaptation
Center: International Center for Agricultural Research in the Dry Areas

Name: A-AG10171-ARISE PDRCC
Dscription: ARISE PDRCC
Project Summary: The African Research Initiative for Scientific Excellence (ARISE) project is designed to advance scientific research and innovation across Africa by supporting early-career researchers in addressing critical challenges on the continent. ARISE provides funding, mentorship, and resources to enable these researchers to pursue cutting-edge projects that contribute to the continent's development. The initiative emphasizes the importance of building local research capacities, fostering collaboration among African scientists, and promoting research that is relevant to the needs of African societies. Through ARISE, researchers are empowered to make significant contributions to fields such as agriculture, health, environment, and technology, ultimately driving progress and enhancing the continent's scientific and economic landscape.
Project results: (1) Acquisition of new knowledge on rice-rice blast interaction, (2) development of new resistant breeding lines, (3) capacity building and (4) broadening collaboration - all of which will accelerate rice breeding and ensure the sustainability of rice production in sub-Saharan Africa in the face of climate change

Name: R-A-2022-71-ICAR 2023 - 2027
Project Summary: The project has 5 thematic work areas, namely: 1) Establish a framework for developing market oriented rice varieties with higher genetic gains using IRRI’s OneRice Breeding Strategy; 2) Integrate direct seeded rice system for India by combining the strengths of DSR-breeding with best agronomic practices, with special emphasis on the Eastern India; 3) Rice-based Health and Nutrition through improved starch quality to lower the glycemic index (GI) and increased nutrient density in the milled rice through breeding interventions; 4) Climate Resilient Rice through the development of agronomic practices that help to mitigate the effects of rice cultivation on the climate, specifically in terms of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.; 5) Integrated Capacity Building.

Name: D-100817-AFESD Dryland Academy
Dscription: ICARDA Dryland Academy - Capacity Development and Technical Agricultural assistance to Arab Countries
Project Summary: The ICARDA-AFESD partnership has a rich history of increasing Arab agricultural knowledge and capability. The AFESD-support to ICARDA’s Capacity Development Program and its Arab Dryland Academy under the proposed project will aim to continue to strengthen the capacity of Arab national agricultural research programs and their extension services as well as Arab Universities to ensure that they continue to fully benefit from and make use of the expertise and solutions provided by ICARDA and One CGIAR.  It will also enable them to get the needed exposure to cutting-edge research innovations and modern technologies and approaches to ensure their transfer to local farmers for adoption and impact on the ground.  The proposed project facilitates access of Arab national technical, managerial staff and students along the agriculture value chains in Arab countries to the capacity development opportunities. ICARDA Dryland Academy will be open to all AFESD member countries with a special attention to the Arab countries facing agricultural challenges due to climate change, political and economic instability, and food insecurity. The Academy will offer Mid-Career researchers an opportunity to conduct their research under the supervision of ICARDA experts and scientists, receive mentorship and thematic training programs.
Center: International Center for Agricultural Research in the Dry Areas

Name: R-A-2024-19-ISARC Phase III
Dscription: ISARC Phase II
Project Summary: The ISARC program will increase farmers’ incomes, improve food and nutrition security, health, and well-being of smallholder farmers through increased system productivity, reduced yield gaps, enhanced climate resilience, mechanized and digital farming, improved market linkages, modernized value chains enabling entrepreneurship for women and youth, and capacity development.

Name: A-AG10140-KAFACI Phase 3
Dscription: KAFACI Phase 3
Project Summary: The KAFACI (Korean-African Food and Agriculture Cooperation Initiative) project, led by AfricaRice, is a transformative effort aimed at revolutionizing rice production across Africa. The initiative addresses the continent’s rice production challenges through a comprehensive strategy. It focuses on developing and disseminating high-yield, disease-resistant rice varieties, promoting advanced farming technologies, and implementing best practices in water and nutrient management. The project collaborates closely with local farmers, agricultural researchers, and government agencies to ensure that the improvements are both practical and sustainable. A significant aspect of KAFACI is its emphasis on capacity-building. The project includes training programs and knowledge exchange activities designed to empower local stakeholders, enhancing their ability to implement new technologies and practices effectively.
Project results: (1) Development of High-Yielding Rice Varieties; (2) Establishment of the Africa-Korea Rice Breeding Laboratory; (2) Yields, income  and food consumption scores increased

Name: R-A-2021-28-KOICA-UPLB Project
Dscription: KOICA-UPLB Project
Project Summary: The project is a partnership between IRRI, the Korean Internatiol Cooperation Agency and the University of the Philippines, Los Banos and is aimed at establishing a state-of-the-art Genome research center, enhancing the R&D capacity through training, education and scientific exchange between the university and IRRI in the area of genomic research.

Name: A-HP2023-002-MasterCard - WAEMU
Dscription: Mastercard - RIZAO
Project Summary: The Mastercard-RIZAO project (West African Economic and Monetary Union), spearheaded by AfricaRice, aims to significantly improve agricultural productivity and economic development within the West African Economic and Monetary Union (WAEMU) by addressing critical challenges in the rice sector. The initiative focuses on enhancing financial inclusion by leveraging MasterCard’s digital tools to provide rice farmers with access to credit, savings, and insurance, thereby enabling better investment and risk management. In parallel, AfricaRice will introduce and promote climate-smart and high-yielding rice varieties, alongside modern farming techniques in water management, pest control, and soil fertility, to boost productivity and resilience. The project also aims to strengthen market connections by improving the supply chain and reducing transaction costs, thereby increasing farmers' incomes and facilitating better access to local, regional, and international markets. Capacity building is a key component, with extensive training provided to farmers, extension workers, and other stakeholders to enhance rice quality and reduce post-harvest losses.
Project results: A-HP2023-002-MasterCard - WAEMU

Name: A-AG10183-SGP-AGGRI2 Accelerating Genetic Gain and Varietal
Dscription: SGP-AGGRI2 Accelerating Genetic Gain and Varietal Replacement in Rice - Phase 2 Project
Project Summary: AGGRi2 is BMGF's investment in CGIAR's rice breeding and seed delivery work. This project builds on the accomplishments of previous BMGF projects such as Stress Tolerant Rice for Africa and South Asia (STRASA), Transforming Rice Breeding (TRB), and the AGGRi Alliance [Phase 1]. This second phase aims to develop and deliver improved rice varieties that respond to the needs of smallholder farmers in the SSA region. AGGRi2 is well aligned and linked to the CGIAR's Market Intelligence, Accelerated Breeding, and Seed Equal initiatives.
Project results: (1) Development and supply of improved rice varieties that meet the needs of small-scale farmers in the Sub-Saharan African region; (2) Replacement of older, lower-yielding varieties.

Name: V0215 VACS Capacity
Dscription: VACS Capacity
Project Summary: The VACS Movement, launched in February 2023 by the US State Department in partnership with the African Union and FAO, aims to build resilient agrifood systems in Africa. It focuses on enhancing opportunity crop productivity, improving nutrition through climate-resilient cropping systems, building healthy soils, and strengthening African research capacities. USAID and CIMMYT are implementing the VACS Capacity activity to train African breeding programs and research professionals. This initiative will develop a cohort of 60 VACS Fellows, comprising post-graduate students and professional trainees, mentored by CGIAR and global partners, with additional training for hundreds more through intensive short courses.
Center: International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center / Centro Internacional de Mejoramiento de Maíz y Trigo

Name: R-A-2023-19-EBS Phase 3
Dscription: EBS Phase 3
Project Summary: The project supports the deployment of the Enterprise Breeding System (EBS) -  an effective data management system that allows the integration of genotypic data into complex statistical analyses to drastically increase the data accuracy at a fraction of the cost of more traditional methods. Through this, breeding networks serving small-scale producers (SSPs) throughout the developing areas worldwide will have access to genomic selections, and drive the positive transformation of breeding networks.
Countries: Morocco, Mexico, Kenya, Nigeria, Senegal, Philippines
Funder: Fundación para el Desarrollo Empresarial y Agrícola

Name: D-100777-GCC Date Palm
Dscription: GCC Date Palm Project
Project Summary: The proposed program envisions to increase the adoption of appropriate desert farming innovation packages and evidence-based investment in desert farming by public and private sectors. This will be done through establishment of Innovation Platforms (IPs) which will be physically located in strategically selected locations considering the mobility zones of stakeholders across the relevant agribusiness landscape. Main and satellite IPs will be developed, operated and managed. The main IPs are full-fledged integrated research and development sites managed by networks of collaborators across ecosystems within one of the three prevalent production systems in the region, namely seasonal crops, fruit trees and livestock and feed.  The program will also establish and strengthen partnerships and capacities and devise policy pathways for sustainable transformation to address common challenges in desert farming. Additionally, the project will support inclusive entrepreneurship through an incubator component for capacity development of youth and women.
Center: International Center for Agricultural Research in the Dry Areas
Countries: Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain, The United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia, Oman

Name: GAC-SCALING UP CLIMATE INFORMATION SERVICES AND CLIMATE- SMART AGRICULTURE FOR WOMEN FARMERS IN NICARAGUA’S  DRY CORRIDOR
Dscription:
Nicaragua is the fourth poorest country1 in Latin America and the Caribbean (LAC) after Haiti, Honduras and Guatemala, and ranks 21st out of 181 countries with the highest disaster risk.2 The development of Nicaragua, which relies heavily on agriculture, is disproportionally impacted by its vulnerability to climatic events: 25% of the population lives in areas at risk of hurricanes and tropical storms and 40% is affected by recurring drought, with communities in the Dry Corridor experiencing the greatest impacts of climate change. According to climate models, Nicaragua could become as warm as the Sahara by 2070, with average annual temperatures over 29°C, only found in 0.8% of the
globe today. The country will suffer more intense droughts and variable rainfall patterns. This threatens food and water security, along with biodiversity, and is expected to worsen the existing poverty and climate vulnerability of millions of households in Nicaragua, and further exacerbate irregular migration.Accessible, credible, and relevant climate and weather information services (CWIS)3 can vastly improve decision-making in disaster management, agriculture, biodiversity conservation and water
resources management. However, the access and use of CWIS is still limited in Nicaragua. The poorest and most vulnerable, especially in remote rural communities, are not benefitting from the scientific and technological advancements that could help manage climate risks and adapt their farming practices. Women farmers (including those from indigenous and ethnic minority groups) face the most barriers in accessing CWIS, as gender norms and patriarchal values in Nicaragua reinforce inequalities in access to and management of productive resources. Through the Project, the Organization will scale-up climate information and weather services and promote the adoption of climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices for at least 1,000 women from rural communities in the Dry Corridor of Nicaragua. Activities are designed to support the following:  
• Rural women smallholders and their organizations active in Nicaragua’s Dry Corridor actively access and use CWIS tailored to their specific information needs to manage climate and weather risk more effectively.
• Women smallholders empowered to adopt CSA technologies (climate smart crop varieties, best-fit crop management practices, digital tools, etc.) at a greater rate. 
• Service providers (including farmers’ organizations, National Agricultural Research and Extension Systems, NGOs, etc.) improve the quality and gender-responsiveness of the services and information they provide to women farmers and value chain actors.
• A network of at least 15 female community leaders become powerful advocates for women and girls in the design of gender responsive CWIS, the adaptation of CSA innovation bundles, and in climate governance and decision-making.
Men and women in farming households participate in community dialogues and household methodologies to reflect on and question harmful gender norms and attitudes and to foster more equitable gender relations and workloads.
• Women and girls in the Dry Corridor use access to small grants (incentives) and training and technical assistance (TTA) products to scale up adoption of CSA and increase income from farm business ideas centering around climate adaptation, biodiversity conservation, nature-positive solutions, and circular economy principles.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: N-633006-IICOF IN JIMMA ETH
Dscription: IICOF IN JIMMA ETH
Project Summary: The project includes a learning agenda intended to produce evidence on the effectiveness of bundled interventions as compared to narrowly designed projects that focus on addressing a specific adoption constraint. At the request of HereWeGrow for an evaluation partner organization to support its learning agenda, we present in this proposal an evaluation plan to rigorously measure the impact of the project. The proposed evaluation will mainly rely on a randomized controlled trial (RCT) to assess the causal effect of the interventions on the adoption of good agricultural practices, productivity, and income. The evaluation will also assess potential synergies between the interventions, provide insights into the pathways that help explain the realized impacts, and potential heterogeneity of impacts along economic and social dimensions. The RCT will be supplemented by a qualitative assessment both to have a deeper understanding of the impact pathways and mechanisms and gather evidence on project components that are not included in the experimental assessment.

Name: T-PJ-003942-IITA Accelerated Innovation Delivery Initiative (AID-I) for Mozambique
Dscription: USAID/IITA - IITA ACCELERATED INNOVATION DELIVERY INITIATIVE (AID-I) FOR MOZAMBIQUE (RAFS)-PJ-3942...S.BOAHEN
Project Summary: This initiative seeks to boost food and nutrition security, incomes and resilience for smallholder farmers in Mozambique's Nampula, Zambézia and Niassa provinces. It aims to scale proven agricultural and nutritional innovations to tackle low productivity, extreme weather impacts and global supply chain disruptions. This program focuses on improving seed systems, enhancing village livestock productivity, integrating soil and crop management, increasing access to nutrient-dense foods and expanding market access and credit. Targeting 750,000 smallholder farmers, this initiative emphasizes gender equality, social inclusion and capacity development. By addressing these key areas, this project intends to increase productivity and profitability, ultimately improving the livelihoods of farmers and their communities.
Project results: Improved functionality of seed systems that cater to the needs of smallholder farmers, leading to increased productivity, enhanced market access, and greater availability of nutrient-dense foods.

Name: Climate-smart initiatives for climate change adaptation and sustainability in prioritized agricultural production systems in Colombia (CSICAP)
Dscription: The Climate-Smart Initiatives for Climate Change Adaptation and Sustainability in Prioritized Agricultural Production Systems in Colombia (CSICAP) project aims to safeguard livelihoods, boost productivity, and enhance profitability in nine of Colombia's key agri-food value chains, while increasing resilience to climate change impacts and reducing greenhouse gas emissions in the agriculture sector. With a USD $100 million budget, funded by the Green Climate Fund (GCF), Development Bank of Latin America (CAF), the government of Colombia, and partner contributions, CSICAP aims to benefit 200,000 people directly and nearly one million indirectly. The project will help reduce over 2 million tons of CO2 equivalent emissions through digital agroclimatic services, climate-resilient crops, water-efficient climate-smart practices, inclusive capacity-building, and innovative business models. It will be implemented in 22 out of 32 departments in Colombia.
Project Summary: The Climate-Smart Initiatives for Climate Change Adaptation and Sustainability in Prioritized Agricultural Production Systems in Colombia (CSICAP) project aims to safeguard livelihoods, boost productivity, and enhance profitability in nine of Colombia's key agri-food value chains, while increasing resilience to climate change impacts and reducing greenhouse gas emissions in the agriculture sector. With a USD $100 million budget, funded by the Green Climate Fund (GCF), Development Bank of Latin America (CAF), the government of Colombia, and partner contributions, CSICAP aims to benefit 200,000 people directly and nearly one million indirectly. The project will help reduce over 2 million tons of CO2 equivalent emissions through digital agroclimatic services, climate-resilient crops, water-efficient climate-smart practices, inclusive capacity-building, and innovative business models. It will be implemented in 22 out of 32 departments in Colombia.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: N-600317-STATS FR SPACE-MOZAMBIQUE
Dscription: STATS FR SPACE-MOZAMBIQUE
Project Summary: This project will produce and disseminate accurate agricultural production statistics data in Mozambique in a sufficiently timely manner so that all market participants can use the information for decision-making. New workflows utilizing satellite remote sensing-based analytics and systematically collected quality groundtruth data will be developed and delivered to the Ministry of Agriculture and the Bureau of Statistics to improve their technical capacity to incorporate earth observation technology in the national agricultural statistics system.

Name: PEPSI-Climate-Resilient Agriculture/Thailand
Dscription: See project summary.
Project Summary: The Climate Resilience Platform (CRP) is a collaborative initiative between PepsiCo, the Alliance of Bioversity International and CIAT, and FFAR, aimed at understanding and addressing the risks and opportunities posed by climate change in agriculture. The project focuses on assessing climate risks for potato production and rice sourcing in Thailand, with projections extending to 2030. Through climate impact assessments, business risk quantification, and adaptation recommendations, the CRP will help farmers and businesses enhance resilience and promote sustainable agricultural practices. The findings will be integrated into the CRP platform, supporting decision-making for long-term sustainability. By leveraging data-driven insights, this initiative aligns with Pepsico’s broader sustainability agenda and the promotion of regenerative agriculture practices.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation(BMGF)-1000 Farms Research Platform
Dscription: See project summary.
Project Summary: 1000FARMS aims to implement an Africa-wide network for on-farm variety testing that is digitally supported to provide vital performance data and farmer feedback. This will accelerate breeding of new climate-adapted and farmer-preferred crop varieties.  VISION: 1) CGIAR-NARES breeding networks have sufficient on-farm data on late-stage selection candidates and new releases to ensure they will be high-performing in the hands of farmers and can be recommended for scaling by the seed system; 2.) Farmers have sustainable access to new varieties with demonstratively improved performance over the varieties they currently grow across mandate crops in Africa and 3) Breeding programs deliver, sustain and accurately measure ‘realized’ or on-farm genetic gains in the face of climate change.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: USA-BMGF-Artemis II - Field Phenotyping Prototype
Dscription: See project summary.
Project Summary: Artemis aims to transform breeding processes by leveraging AI and mobile phone technology to shift breeding programs from majority on-station to majority on-farm, alleviating critical logistical bottlenecks in phenotyping and shortening the breeding cycle. This is achieved through more efficient use of breeding technician time and provision of high quality and replicable data by enhancing the collection of reliable phenomic data through smartphones and to support breeder decision-making using on-farm collected data. A smartphone-deployable computer vision phenotyping tool makes phenotyping more efficient by reducing labor investment and overcoming limitations to the availability of highly trained technicians; reliability is increased as computer vision standardizes data collection and interpretation; and allows for the execution of on-farm breeding process as the phenotyping technology can be mobilized remotely. Computer vision leverages technology to collect data not possible by human eyes and it increases the amount, frequency and types of data, which can be used to derive novel breeding analytics expanding the data for decision making.
Center: Alliance of Bioversity and CIAT - Regional Hub (International Center for Tropical Agriculture / Centro Internacional de Agricultura Tropical)

Name: T-PJ-003866-Liberia Land and Soil Resources Knowledge project (Soils4Liberia)
Dscription: PJ-003866: Liberia Land and Soil Resources Knowledge project (Soils4Liberia)
Project Summary: This project aims to develop a Land & Soil Information System for Liberia to enhance land and soil management. Its main objectives are to create a functional system with reliable data, increase stakeholder awareness and support decision-making for resource conservation, sustainable agriculture and climate resilience. Key outputs include protocols, a comprehensive database, soil property maps, a land use map, Soil Quality Indicator (SQI) maps and land and crop suitability maps. These will aid government agencies, research institutions and NGOs by providing crucial data for policy development, agricultural planning and climate change mitigation. This project seeks to improve land and soil health and support sustainable development.
Project results: Develop standard protocols and tools for soil and land use inventory and field campaign management, including a database with primary soil and land use data, soil property maps, land use maps of Liberia, Soil Quality Indicator (SQI) maps, and land and crop suitability maps.

Name: W-D-0392-NET: WaPOR Phase 2
Dscription: Monitoring land and water productivity by Remote Sensing (WaPOR Phase 2)
Project Summary:
The outcome of the project is defined as “Increased capacity of FAO Members (Member Nations and Member Organizations) in monitoring terrestrial biomass production and evapotranspiration to assess and improve land and water productivity in rainfed and irrigated agriculture, and contribute to sustainable crop production intensification adapted to a changing climate.”

The three major Outputs of the project are:
1. Consolidated open-access information and related operational services on the terrestrial soil water balance and agricultural land and water productivity, including refinement and expansion towards global coverage of the spatial scales, and creation of longer time series (Spatial database);
2. Strengthened capacities of FAO Members to assess land and water productivity aimed at informed decision making for improved agricultural practices and efficient water management by multiple stakeholder groups to minimize impacts and adapt to climate change and other environmental and societal changes (Capacity development); and
3. Compendium of implementable solutions and tools, co-developed with various stakeholders at different operational scales, from farm to the national level, to effectively increase land and water productivity in agriculture sustainably at field level as well as to support water management, water governance and agricultural policies (Compendium of implementable solutions and tools).
Project results:

Countries: Pakistan, Jordan, Algeria, Mozambique, Iraq, Mali, Tunisia, Kenya, Ethiopia

Name: R-A-2024-19-ISARC Phase III
Dscription: ISARC Phase II
Project Summary: The ISARC program will increase farmers’ incomes, improve food and nutrition security, health, and well-being of smallholder farmers through increased system productivity, reduced yield gaps, enhanced climate resilience, mechanized and digital farming, improved market linkages, modernized value chains enabling entrepreneurship for women and youth, and capacity development.

Name: T-PJ-003763-IITA Accelerated Innovation Delivery Initiative
Dscription: IITA Accelerated Innovation Delivery Initiative – AID-I: Great Lakes Highlands
Project Summary: The Accelerated Innovation Delivery Initiative in the Great Lakes Region (AID-I GLR) is a two-year initiative funded by USAID to enhance food and nutrition security in Burundi, the Democratic Republic of Congo and Rwanda. It aims to reach 1.2 million farmers by rapidly scaling up proven agricultural technologies and innovations developed primarily by CGIAR and partners. This initiative focuses on creating and structuring partnerships with the private sector, government agencies and NGOs to deliver these technologies, adapted to local needs, along with advisory services to a large network of farmers. AID-I GLR is part of the U.S. response to the global food security crisis, targeting both immediate and long-term impacts on food security in the region.
Project results: Two new partnerships were established in Burundi and DRC, and nine market actors enrolled. The project reached 774,965 participants (50% women, 22% youth), exceeding targets. Over 600,000 smallholder farmers received quality seeds, and 92,000 accessed fortified maize flour. Scaling events engaged 350,000 farmers, 770 demonstration plots were established, and 99,000 stakeholders trained. Additionally, 147.2 metric tons of Early Generation Seeds were produced, and market actors sold 8,200 Mt of inputs worth $3.6 million.

Name: N-343005-GAIA Phase II
Dscription: GAIA II - Data Corpus and Model Fine Tuning (FCDO-DF) #AITF
Project Summary: Building on GAIA Phase I, this investment will focus on a number of key areas: 1) Content Aggregation 2) Data and AI governance 3) Dynamic Advisories 4) Evaluation and Benchmarking. Through these focused efforts, this investment collectively aims to enhance the effectiveness and reach of generative AI-powered agricultural advisory services for small-scale producers.

Name: W-D-0523-DIWASA - Phase II
Dscription: Digital Innovations for Water Secure Africa - Phase 2
Project Summary: The project aims to enhance water security across scales in Africa through improved water data availability, accessibility, and institutional capacity and to leverage on innovative digital techniques for sustainable water resources management. It is structured as four pillars with user engagement and capacity development (Pillar 2) taking the bulk of the project budget. Within Pillar 1, the data products and tools developed under Phase I will be further enhanced and, more importantly, user application cases (use cases) developed together with end users. The Pillar 2 will involve activities that will help integrate the data layers and products into DEA and other ODC platforms. A fully dedicated Pillar 3 is included in the project to strengthen the integration of the datasets and tools in the DE Africa platform. Under Pillar 4, the prototype of river basin Digital Twin (for the Limpopo basin) will be developed to demonstrate use of advanced technology in water resources decision-making.
Project results:
•	Improved Water Management Decisions - This will encompass several multi-scale baseline and future scenario water resource assessments to include seasonal and mid-range forecasting; flood and drought risk mapping; suitability and sustainability mapping for vegetable production; appropriate irrigation technology; access to streams, surface water, groundwater; assessing rainfed and irrigated area, monthly and season rainfed, irrigation, surface, and groundwater use and deficits; and nutritional security data that will support advancement and refinement of the technological tools. 
•	Enhanced Water Management Capacity and Thriving Community Application- The data layers and products will be incorporated into the Digital Earth Africa and other Open Data Cube platforms to underpin strong stakeholder engagement, increased awareness, co-development efforts, and testing of the DIWASA tool. 
•	Reinforced Digital Earth Africa Water Data Products and Tools-To strengthen the integration of datasets and tools in the Digital Earth Africa platform, project-level agreements will be developed and signed
•	Implementation of the first Digital Twin Model for enhanced river basin management - A prototype of the Digital Twin will be developed for the Limpopo River basin to demonstrate the use of advanced technology in water resource decision-making.
Countries: South Africa, Ethiopia, Zambia, Mozambique, Burkina Faso, Kenya, Uganda, Zimbabwe, Ghana

Name: F-AG10607-ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Dscription: ASIA-AFRICA BLUETECH SUPERHIGHWAY PROJECT (COAST)
Project Summary: Asia–Africa BlueTech Superhighway (AABS) harnesses South–South collaboration to assess, adapt and scale evidence-based innovations and models for delivering nature-positive impact through aquatic food systems. Implemented by WorldFish in collaboration with a host of partners, AABS aims to transform livelihoods of coastal communities across African and Asian countries, particularly for women and youth, and help restore marine and coastal ecosystems by developing sustainable and resilient aquatic food systems. Funded by the UK Government under its Blue Planet Fund, AABS is being implemented in two phases over the course of seven years (2023–2030).

Name: N-343004-AgPile
Dscription: A data ecosystem enabling AI for small scale producers- phase 1
Project Summary: This project aims to develop and implement a federated, collaborative data and analytics platform that will revolutionize how agricultural research data is managed, shared, and utilized within CGIAR system and beyond. By providing FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) and AI-ready data, the project seeks to accelerate the digital transformation of agricultural research and development.
